explanation: Section 163, SFO makes it an offence to interfere with a Part VI audit by deleting, destroying, mutilating, falsifying, concealing, altering or making records unavailable (or helping another to do so), by disposing of or procuring the disposal of property relevant to the audit, or by leaving or attempting to leave Hong Kong. Requesting more time, appointing a new auditor and querying fees are not listed as interference in the text. (PDF p.83)

question: A licensed representative fails to comply with an SFC code of conduct issued under s. 169, SFO. Which statement is correct?
options: The breach is not itself a breach of law, but the SFC may consider whether he remains a fit and proper person, and a court shall consider the code's provisions if relevant to proceedings under the SFO
The breach automatically constitutes a criminal offence under the SFO
The breach is irrelevant in any court proceedings under the SFO
The breach becomes an offence only if it is repeated
explanation: Failure to comply with a code of conduct is not a breach of law and does not by itself constitute an offence, but it may raise questions about whether the person is fit and proper to remain licensed or registered, and a court hearing proceedings under the SFO shall consider the code's provisions if relevant — giving the codes a degree of legal recognition. It is therefore neither automatically criminal nor irrelevant in court, and the text states no 'repeat breach' rule. (PDF p.84)

section: Representations by an intermediary or its representatives (s. 176, SFO)
question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, tells a prospective client during a meeting that his investment abilities have been endorsed by the SFC. Which statement is correct?
options: Mr Chan commits no offence because the statement was made orally
Mr Chan commits an offence only if the claim of endorsement is made in writing
Mr Chan commits an offence under s. 176, SFO
Mr Chan commits no offence provided the client did not rely on the statement
explanation: Section 176, SFO makes it an offence for an intermediary or its representative to represent in any manner, whether expressly or by implication, that its or his abilities or qualifications have been endorsed or warranted by the Government or the SFC. Because the prohibition covers a representation made 'in any manner', oral statements are caught, no writing is required, and the offence does not depend on the client's reliance. (PDF p.84)

question: Section 170, SFO prohibits a person from selling shares in which of the following circumstances?
options: Where he does not already own the shares or have a currently exercisable right to vest the securities in the buyer
Where he owns the shares but has held them for less than one month
Where the shares are traded on an overseas exchange
Where the sale is made through an unsolicited call
explanation: The SFO prohibits selling shares where the seller does not already own them or does not have a currently exercisable right to vest the securities in the buyer; the general prohibition has specific exclusions discussed in Topic 7. There is no minimum holding period, no overseas-exchange test, and unsolicited calls are dealt with separately under s. 174, SFO, not under the short selling provision. (PDF p.84)

question: Offers to buy or sell securities under s. 175 and Schedule 7, SFO apply to intermediaries carrying on which regulated activities?
options: Types 1, 2 and 3
Types 1, 4 and 6
Types 4, 5 and 9
Types 2, 5 and 7
explanation: Section 175 and Schedule 7, SFO apply to offers by securities dealers (Type 1 regulated activity), investment advisers (Type 4) and corporate finance advisers (Type 6) or their representatives to buy or sell securities. The other combinations include regulated activity types not mentioned in this provision. The detailed requirements are stated to be beyond the scope of the Study Guide. (PDF p.85)

question: Two corporations are each controlled by the same individual. Which of the following levels of control makes them 'related corporations' under the SFO?
options: The individual controls not less than 20% of the voting power of each corporation
The individual holds exactly half of the issued participating share capital of each corporation
The individual is a director of each corporation
The individual controls more than half the voting power at general meetings of each corporation
explanation: Where an individual controls the composition of the board of directors, controls more than half the voting power at general meetings, or holds more than half the issued participating share capital of one or more corporations, each of those corporations and each of their subsidiaries are related corporations. The 20% threshold belongs to the 'controlling entity' definition, exactly half does not exceed the 'more than half' test, and merely being a director is not control of the composition of the board. (PDF p.86)

question: During an SFC enquiry, when must an AFI (which is not an intermediary, an associated entity of an intermediary, a related corporation or its associated entity) provide information regarding its customers?
options: Whenever any SFC staff member orally requests the information
Never; the customer information of an AFI is absolutely protected
Only where a relevant authority (the HKMA or the SFC) certifies in writing that the disclosure or production is necessary for the purposes of the enquiry
Only where a magistrate has issued a warrant authorising entry to its premises
explanation: Such an AFI need not provide information regarding its customers unless a relevant authority — either the HKMA or the SFC — certifies in writing that the disclosure or production of the information is necessary for the purposes of the enquiry. The protection is therefore conditional, not absolute, and an oral request alone is insufficient. A magistrate's warrant is a separate mechanism under the investigation provisions, not the trigger stated here. (PDF p.87)

question: Mr Wong is not a party to a securities transaction being examined by the SFC under s. 181, SFO, but he has an interest in the transaction. He refuses, without reasonable excuse, to disclose details of the transaction when properly requested. Which statement is correct?
options: He commits an offence, because specified persons include any person with an interest in the transaction
He commits no offence, because only intermediaries can be required to disclose transaction information
He commits no offence, because he is not a direct party to the transaction
He commits an offence only if he is licensed by the SFC
explanation: The SFC, or a person authorised by it, may require specified persons to disclose information related to transactions in securities, futures contracts and CISs; specified persons include the parties to the transaction — including any person with an interest in the transaction or its subject — and any intermediary involved in executing it. Failure to comply with a proper request without reasonable excuse is an offence. The power is not limited to intermediaries, direct parties or SFC licensees. (PDF p.87)

question: Which of the following is a person under investigation by the SFC under ss. 182 and 183, SFO required to do?
options: Surrender his travel documents to the investigator
Attend before the investigator and answer questions
Pay the costs of the investigation in advance
Cease carrying on his business for the duration of the investigation
explanation: A person under investigation must provide documents and explanations, attend before the investigator and answer questions, give all reasonable assistance, support his evidence by statutory declaration, and, if unable to provide evidence, make a statutory declaration stating the reasons. The text imposes no requirement to surrender travel documents, pay investigation costs or suspend business. Failing to comply without reasonable excuse, or giving false or misleading responses, is an offence. (PDF p.88)

question: A magistrate issues a warrant under Part VIII, SFO. Within what period must entry to the premises take place, and for how long may seized documents be retained?
options: Entry within six months; documents retained for seven days
Entry within one month; documents retained for six months with no possibility of extension
Entry within seven days; documents retained for six months, a period which may be extended
Entry within seven business days; documents may be retained indefinitely
explanation: The warrant authorises entry to the specified premises, if necessary by force, at any time within seven days, and seized documents may be retained for six months, a period which may be extended (s. 191, SFO). Option (a) reverses the two periods, option (b) wrongly excludes extension, and option (d) wrongly states business days and indefinite retention. (PDF p.89)

question: Under s. 194, SFO, what is the maximum pecuniary penalty the SFC may order a regulated person to pay for misconduct?
options: HK$10 million in all cases
Three times any profit gained, capped at HK$1 million
The lesser of HK$10 million or three times any profit gained or loss avoided
The greater of HK$10 million or three times any profit gained or loss avoided as a result of the misconduct
explanation: The SFC may, separately or in addition to its other disciplinary sanctions, order the regulated person to pay a penalty up to the GREATER of HK$10 million or three times any profit gained or loss avoided as a result of his misconduct. It is not a flat HK$10 million cap, not the lesser of the two amounts, and there is no HK$1 million cap in the text. (PDF p.90)

question: Under s. 193(2), SFO, a responsible officer of a licensed corporation that is guilty of misconduct may also be liable if the misconduct is:
options: attributable to his neglect, or occurred with his consent or connivance
committed by any employee under his supervision, regardless of his own conduct
discovered by him but reported to the SFC more than three business days later
connected to an activity from which he personally profited
explanation: Section 193(2), SFO makes a responsible officer, executive officer or person involved in management liable where the firm's misconduct is attributable to that person's neglect or occurred with their consent or connivance. There is no automatic liability for all supervised employees, no three-business-day reporting test in this provision, and personal profit is not the statutory test. (PDF p.91)

question: Which of the following is NOT included in the definition of a "regulated person" under s. 196, SFO?
options: A registered institution
An executive officer of a registered institution
A licensed representative of a licensed corporation
An individual whose name is recorded on the HKMA register as engaged in a regulated activity
explanation: Section 196, SFO concerns registered institutions: a "regulated person" is a registered institution, an executive officer of a registered institution, a person involved in the management of a registered institution, or an individual recorded on the HKMA register as engaged in a regulated activity. A licensed representative of a licensed corporation falls under the parallel provision for licensed persons (s. 193), not s. 196. (PDF p.91)

question: Sections 195 and 197, SFO allow the SFC to suspend or revoke a licence or registration in certain circumstances. Which of the following is NOT one of those circumstances?
options: Bankruptcy, receivership or arrangements with creditors
Failure to satisfy a levy of execution
Mental incapacity of individuals or directors
Failure to pay the annual licence fee on time
explanation: Sections 195 and 197 list bankruptcy/receivership or arrangements with creditors, failure to satisfy a levy of execution, mental incapacity, and conviction for an offence casting doubt on fitness and properness. Late payment of an annual licence fee is not among the listed grounds. Note also that suspension or revocation may apply to the entire regulated business or only to a specific part of it. (PDF p.91)

question: The SFC Disciplinary Fining Guidelines, which set out the considerations, policies and practices the SFC applies in exercising its fining powers, are issued pursuant to which section of the SFO?
options: Section 193
Section 196
Section 199
Section 399
explanation: The SFC Disciplinary Fining Guidelines are issued pursuant to s. 199, SFO. Section 193 defines misconduct and liability of licensed persons, and s. 196 deals with regulated persons connected to registered institutions. Section 399 is the SFC's general power to issue guidelines (under which, for example, the Guidelines on Disclosure of Inside Information were issued), not the specific basis for the fining guidelines. (PDF p.91)

question: Under the SFC's "Guidance Note on Cooperation with the SFC", by how much may the SFC reduce disciplinary sanctions to recognise an intermediary's cooperation?
options: Up to 10%
Up to 25%
Up to 30%
Up to 50%
explanation: The Guidance Note states that the SFC may recognise cooperation by reducing up to 30% of the disciplinary sanctions imposed. As a general principle, the earlier the cooperation is given and recognised, the higher the reduction may be. The figures of 10%, 25% and 50% are not the stated maximum. (PDF p.92)

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the ways of cooperating with the SFC identified in the "Guidance Note on Cooperation with the SFC"?
options: Voluntarily and promptly reporting any breaches or failings to the SFC
Providing true and complete information regarding breaches or failings
Voluntarily surrendering the licence before disciplinary proceedings begin
Taking rectification measures such as making full and prompt compensation to clients
explanation: The Guidance Note lists four ways of cooperating: voluntary and prompt reporting of breaches, providing true and complete information, acceptance of liability (such as bringing the case to an early conclusion), and taking rectification measures including containing breaches and compensating clients. Voluntarily surrendering the licence is not one of the listed forms of cooperation. (PDF p.92)

question: ABC Securities, a licensed corporation, discovers a regulatory breach by its staff. It immediately self-reports to the SFC, provides complete information, accepts liability and promptly compensates affected clients. What is the likely consequence under the SFC's approach to cooperation?
options: The SFC must waive all disciplinary sanctions
The SFC may reduce the disciplinary sanctions by up to 30%, with earlier cooperation attracting a higher reduction
ABC Securities becomes immune from any disciplinary action under Part IX, SFO
The cooperation is only relevant to criminal prosecution, not disciplinary sanctions
explanation: Cooperation may be recognised by the SFC in the form of reduced sanctions, up to 30%, and the earlier the cooperation is given and recognised, the higher the reduction may be. Cooperation does not oblige the SFC to waive sanctions entirely and does not confer immunity from disciplinary action. The Guidance Note concerns the SFC's investigation and enforcement proceedings and disciplinary sanctions, not criminal prosecution only. (PDF p.92)

section: Part X – Powers of intervention and proceedings
question: The SFC's intervention powers under Part X, SFO are to be used where a licensed corporation has: (i) shown that it does not meet the fit and proper criteria; (ii) contravened the specified provisions of the SFO; (iii) had its licence suspended or revoked. Which of these are correct?
options: (i) only
(i) and (ii) only
(ii) and (iii) only
(i), (ii) and (iii)
explanation: All three are listed in the source: the powers are to be used where the licensed corporation has shown it does not meet the fit and proper criteria, has contravened the specified provisions of the SFO, or has had its licence suspended or revoked. The powers are also designed to protect investors, minority shareholders of listed corporations and creditors, and to be used in the interests of the investing public or the public interest. Any answer omitting one of the three limbs is incomplete. (PDF p.93)

section: Part X – Powers of intervention and proceedings
question: Which section of the SFO allows the SFC to issue a notice prohibiting a licensed corporation from disposing of or dealing with property belonging to its clients or to itself?
options: Section 204
Section 205
Section 209
Section 211
explanation: Section 205, SFO covers notices prohibiting disposal of or dealing with property belonging to clients (whether held by the firm or others) or belonging to the firm itself. Section 204 covers restricting business activities or prohibiting specified activities; s. 209 allows the SFC to withdraw, vary or substitute notices; and s. 211 allows the SFC to apply to the Court of First Instance if a notice is not complied with. (PDF p.93)

section: Part X – Powers of intervention and proceedings
question: A licensed corporation subject to an SFC intervention notice subsequently has its licence revoked. What is the effect on the notice?
options: The notice automatically lapses upon revocation
The notice remains valid despite the revocation (s. 210, SFO)
The notice must be reissued by the Court of First Instance to remain effective
The notice converts into a winding-up petition
explanation: Under s. 210, SFO, intervention notices remain valid even if the licence is suspended or revoked. The notice does not lapse, does not need to be reissued by a court, and does not convert into a winding-up petition; a winding-up petition is a separate step the SFC may take under s. 212, SFO. (PDF p.93)

section: Part X – Powers of intervention and proceedings
question: Mr Lau is a responsible officer of a licensed corporation that has received an SFC notice under s. 205, SFO restricting it from dealing with client property, but the firm continues to deal with that property. What may the SFC do?
options: Apply to the Court of First Instance for an order requiring the firm to comply (s. 211, SFO)
Refer the matter to the Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal for an enforcement order
Apply to the Market Misconduct Tribunal to enforce the notice
Nothing further, as the notice ceases to have effect once breached
explanation: If a licensed person fails to comply with an intervention notice, the SFC may apply to the Court of First Instance to order it to comply (s. 211, SFO). The SFAT hears appeals against specified SFC decisions, not enforcement applications, and the Market Misconduct Tribunal has no role in enforcing intervention notices. The notice does not cease to have effect; the SFC may also withdraw, vary or substitute notices under s. 209. (PDF p.93)

section: Part X – Powers of intervention and proceedings
question: Under ss. 212(1) and 212(1A), SFO, the SFC may petition the Court of First Instance for a winding-up order against a licensed corporation (other than an AFI or OFC) under which ordinance, and on what grounds?
options: The Bankruptcy Ordinance, on just and equitable grounds
The CWUMPO, on just and equitable grounds
The OFC Rules, where it is in the public interest
The SFO itself, where the firm is no longer fit and proper
explanation: Winding-up orders for corporations (other than AFIs and OFCs) are sought under the CWUMPO on just and equitable grounds; for OFCs the petition is made under the OFC Rules. The Bankruptcy Ordinance is used for bankruptcy orders against licensed representatives where the SFC considers it desirable in the public interest. The SFO itself is not the ordinance under which the winding-up petition is brought. (PDF p.94)

section: Part X – Powers of intervention and proceedings
question: Before applying to the Court of First Instance under ss. 214 and 214A, SFO for orders against a listed corporation that is an AFI whose affairs are being conducted in a manner oppressive to its members, the SFC must first:
options: Consult the HKMA
Obtain the consent of the Financial Secretary
Obtain leave from the Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal
Notify the Investor Compensation Company Limited
explanation: The SFC may apply for orders under ss. 214 and 214A, SFO where the business or affairs of a listed corporation or an OFC are conducted in a manner that is oppressive, fraudulent or unfairly prejudicial to its members, but in the case of an AFI it must act after consultation with the HKMA. The Financial Secretary's role in this chunk concerns appointing inspectors and prescribing financial products, not consenting to s. 214 applications. The SFAT hears appeals against specified decisions, and the ICC administers the Investor Compensation Fund. (PDF p.94)

section: Part XI – Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal
question: Which of the following statements about the Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal (SFAT) is correct?
options: It is a part-time tribunal chaired by an SFC executive director
It is a full-time tribunal chaired by a judge, hearing appeals against specified decisions set out in Part 2, Schedule 8, SFO
It hears appeals against SFC decisions only; decisions of the HKMA cannot be appealed to it
It hears all decisions made by the SFC without limitation
explanation: The SFO establishes a full-time SFAT chaired by a judge, and the specified decisions which can be reviewed are set out in Part 2, Schedule 8, SFO. It also hears appeals against decisions made by the HKMA in the case of registered institutions, so the third option is wrong. It reviews only specified decisions, not all SFC decisions, and it is not chaired by an SFC executive. (PDF p.95)

question: How is the Investor Compensation Fund funded?
options: By annual contributions from all licensed corporations
By a compensation fund levy on transactions on the SEHK and HKFE, payable by the buyer and the seller at specified rates
By a levy on transactions on the SEHK only, payable by the seller
By an appropriation from the general revenue of the Hong Kong Government
explanation: Under the Securities and Futures (Investor Compensation – Levy) Rules, the fund is financed by a compensation fund levy on transactions on both the SEHK and HKFE, payable by the buyer and the seller at specified rates. It is not funded by licence contributions or government appropriation, and the levy is not limited to SEHK transactions or to sellers only. (PDF p.95)

question: Under the Securities and Futures (Investor Compensation – Compensation Limits) Rules, what is the maximum compensation payable from the Investor Compensation Fund, and by reference to whom is the limit set?
options: HK$500,000, set by reference to each individual claimant
HK$500,000, set by reference to each broker
HK$8 million, set by reference to each individual claimant
HK$150,000, set by reference to each broker
explanation: The Compensation Limits Rules prescribe a maximum of HK$500,000 payable to a person making a claim, and the limits are set by reference to individuals, not to brokers as was previously the case. HK$8 million is the maximum regulatory fine the Market Misconduct Tribunal may impose for breach of the inside information disclosure requirement, not a compensation limit. HK$150,000 is not a figure stated in the source. (PDF p.95)

question: Which body administers, manages and operates the Investor Compensation Fund and processes claims made against it?
options: The SFC's enforcement division
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
The Investor Compensation Company Limited
The HKMA
explanation: The Investor Compensation Company Limited has been established and recognised under the SFO to administer, manage and operate the fund and process claims (Division 5, Part III, SFO); its rules for business operations are subject to SFC approval. The SFC empowers and oversees the arrangement but does not itself process claims; HKEX operates the disclosure-of-interests filing system, and the HKMA is the frontline regulator for registered institutions. (PDF p.95)

section: Part XIVA – Disclosure of inside information
question: Under s. 307D, SFO, when must a listed company disclose inside information to the public?
options: Within three business days after the information comes to its knowledge
As soon as reasonably practicable after the information has come to its knowledge, subject to specified exceptions
Before the start of the next trading session on the SEHK
Only when directed to do so by the SFC
explanation: Part XIVA, SFO requires listed companies to disclose inside information to the public as soon as reasonably practicable after it has come to their knowledge, subject to specified exceptions (s. 307D). The three-business-day period applies to disclosure-of-interests filings under Part XV, not inside information. There is no "next trading session" rule and no requirement to wait for an SFC direction. (PDF p.96)

section: Part XIVA – Disclosure of inside information
question: Under s. 307B(2), SFO, inside information has come to the knowledge of a listed corporation if:
options: Any employee of the corporation is aware of the information

An officer, in the course of performing his duties, has or ought reasonably to have known about it, AND a reasonable person acting as an officer would consider it inside information in relation to the corporation
The information has been published on the HKEX website
A majority of the board of directors has formally resolved that the information is inside information
explanation: Both limbs are required: an officer of the corporation, in the course of performing his duties as an officer, has or ought reasonably to have known about the inside information, and a reasonable person acting as an officer would consider that it is inside information in relation to the corporation. "Officer" means a director, manager or company secretary, or any other person involved in the management of the corporation — not any employee. Publication on the HKEX website and a board resolution are not the statutory tests. (PDF p.96)

section: Part XIVA – Disclosure of inside information
question: A listed corporation is in the middle of confidential negotiations for an acquisition that has not yet been concluded. The confidentiality of the information is preserved. Is the corporation required to disclose this inside information?
options: Yes, it must disclose as soon as reasonably practicable in all circumstances
No, because information concerning an incomplete proposal or negotiation where confidentiality is preserved falls within an exception to the disclosure requirement
No, but only if the SFC first grants a waiver
Yes, but it may delay disclosure until the deal is signed provided it notifies the SFAT
explanation: A listed corporation need not disclose inside information where confidentiality is preserved and the information concerns an incomplete proposal or negotiation; trade secrets are similarly excepted. An SFC waiver is a separate exception that applies where disclosure is prohibited by overseas legislation or restriction orders, so it is not needed here. There is no mechanism for delaying disclosure by notifying the SFAT. (PDF p.96)

section: Part XIVA – Disclosure of inside information
question: Where a listed corporation breaches the duty to disclose inside information, what is the maximum regulatory fine the Market Misconduct Tribunal may impose under s. 307N, SFO?
options: HK$1 million
HK$8 million
HK$10 million
explanation: The listed corporation and/or its directors may face liability before the Market Misconduct Tribunal, including a regulatory fine of up to HK$8 million and/or other sanctions (s. 307N, SFO), as well as civil liability to persons who sustained pecuniary loss (s. 307Z, SFO). HK$500,000 is the maximum Investor Compensation Fund payout per claimant, and HK$1 million and HK$10 million are not figures stated in the source. (PDF p.97)

section: Part XIVA – Disclosure of inside information
question: Which of the following statements about the SFC's "Guidelines on Disclosure of Inside Information" (GDII) is correct?
options: They are issued under s. 399, SFO and do not have the force of law
They are issued under s. 199, SFO and have the force of law
They are issued under s. 307D, SFO and have the force of law
They are issued by the HKMA to assist registered institutions
explanation: The GDII were issued by the SFC under s. 399, SFO to assist corporations to comply with their disclosure obligations under Part XIVA, and they do not have the force of law. Section 199 is the basis for the SFC Disciplinary Fining Guidelines, and s. 307D is the disclosure obligation itself, not the guideline-making power. The GDII are an SFC publication, not an HKMA one. (PDF p.97)

question: Under Part XV, SFO, what are the two basic rules on disclosing interests in the relevant share capital of a listed company?
options: Directors and chief executives disclose all interests; other persons must disclose once their interests reach 5%
Directors and chief executives disclose interests above 5%; other persons disclose above 10%
All persons, including directors, must disclose only once their interests reach 5%
Directors disclose all interests; other persons must disclose once their interests reach 10%
explanation: Directors and chief executives are required to disclose all interests, while other persons must make disclosures once their interests in the relevant share capital (i.e. voting shares, whether or not issued) reach 5%. There is no 5% threshold for directors and no 10% disclosure threshold; 10% is the shareholding needed for members to require a listed corporation to investigate ownership under s. 331. "Interest" is widely defined, covering long and short positions without netting, equity derivatives and attributed interests. (PDF p.97)

question: Mr Chan holds 6.8% of the voting shares of a listed company, having previously disclosed his interest. He buys further shares taking his holding to 7.2%. What must he do?
options: Nothing, as further disclosure is only required when his holding reaches 10%
Make a disclosure, normally within three business days, via the online electronic filing system operated by HKEX, because his holding has moved up through a whole percentage level
Notify the SFC in writing within one month of the change
Make a disclosure only if he also changes the nature of his interest
explanation: Once a person is under a disclosure obligation by reaching 5%, further disclosures are required for changes that take the holding up or down through a whole percentage level (or below 5%); moving from 6.8% to 7.2% crosses the 7% level. Disclosure must normally be made within three business days after the relevant event via the HKEX online electronic filing system, and failure to file when required is an offence. There is no 10% trigger or one-month written notice to the SFC, and a change in the nature of interest is a separate, additional trigger. (PDF p.98)

question: Under s. 331, SFO, members of a listed corporation may require it to investigate the interests held in its voting shares if they hold at least what proportion of the total number of issued voting shares?
explanation: Members holding at least 10% of the total number of issued voting shares may require the corporation to conduct such an investigation, provided they give reasonable grounds for the request (s. 331, SFO). 5% is the threshold at which other persons must disclose their interests under Part XV, not the investigation threshold. 25% and 50% are not thresholds stated in the source. Where members initiate the investigation, the corporation must also prepare a report made available to its members. (PDF p.98)

question: Ms Wong, in support of a licence application, recklessly provides the SFC with information that is false as to a material particular. Under ss. 383 and 384, SFO, what is the consequence?
options: She commits an offence, since providing false or misleading information to the SFC knowingly or recklessly is an offence
She faces only disciplinary action, as providing false information to the SFC is not a criminal matter
No consequence, because the provisions apply only to listed companies' public filings
She commits an offence only if the SFC actually grants the application in reliance on the information
explanation: A person commits an offence if he provides the SFC, whether in applications or other circumstances, with false or misleading information as to a material particular, knowingly or recklessly as to whether it is false or misleading (ss. 383 and 384, SFO). It is an offence, not merely a disciplinary matter, and there is no requirement that the SFC act in reliance on the information. The provisions also apply to listed companies' public statements and on-going disclosure obligations under the Listing Rules, but they are not limited to that context. (PDF p.99)

question: Under ss. 392 and 393, SFO, who has the power to prescribe, by notice in the Gazette, financial products as being (or not being) securities, futures contracts, OTC derivative products or structured products?
options: The SFC
The HKMA
The Financial Secretary
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
explanation: The Financial Secretary is given the power to prescribe, by notice in the Gazette, financial products as being (or not being) securities, futures contracts, OTC derivative products or structured products and, subject to other requirements, as being (or not being) a CIS (ss. 392 and 393, SFO); he may also prescribe markets and clearing houses for OTC derivative determinations (s. 392A). The SFC's powers in this Part are to make rules, codes and guidelines (ss. 397 to 399). Neither the HKMA nor HKEX holds this prescription power. (PDF p.100)

question: Which of the following is listed in the Schedule as major subsidiary legislation issued by the SFC?
options: Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules
Code of Conduct for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities and Futures Commission
Fit and Proper Guidelines
Licensing Handbook
explanation: The Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules appear as item 1.1 under "Major Subsidiary Legislation". The Code of Conduct is listed separately under "Important Codes issued by the SFC", while the Fit and Proper Guidelines and the Licensing Handbook are listed under "Special Guidelines and Guidance Notes issued by the SFC" — none of these three is subsidiary legislation. (PDF p.103)

question: How many separate sets of rules in the Schedule of major subsidiary legislation deal specifically with OTC derivative transactions?
options: One
Two
Three
Four
explanation: Two sets are listed: the Securities and Futures (OTC Derivative Transactions – Reporting and Record Keeping Obligations) Rules (item 1.16) and the Securities and Futures (OTC Derivative Transactions – Clearing and Record Keeping Obligations and Designation of Central Counterparties) Rules (item 1.17). "One" overlooks that reporting and clearing are covered by separate rules, while "three" and "four" overstate the count — no other OTC derivative rules appear in the Schedule. (PDF p.103)

section: Important Codes issued by the SFC
question: In the Schedule of important codes issued by the SFC, the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs are classified as codes relating to which group?
options: Fund managers and investment products offered to the public
Corporate finance advisers
Credit rating agencies
Share registrars
explanation: The Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs are listed under item 2.3, codes relating to corporate finance advisers, alongside the Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct. Fund managers and public investment products are covered by item 2.2 (e.g. Fund Manager Code of Conduct), credit rating agencies by item 2.4, and share registration by item 2.5 — none of these categories includes the takeovers codes. (PDF p.104)

section: Important Codes issued by the SFC
question: Which of the following is listed as a code relating to fund managers and to investment products offered to the public?
options: SFC Handbook for Unit Trusts and Mutual Funds, Investment-Linked Assurance Schemes and Unlisted Structured Investment Products
Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct
The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars
Guidelines on Online Distribution and Advisory Platforms
explanation: The SFC Handbook for Unit Trusts and Mutual Funds, Investment-Linked Assurance Schemes and Unlisted Structured Investment Products is named under item 2.2 (codes relating to fund managers and investment products offered to the public), together with the Fund Manager Code of Conduct and the Code on Real Estate Investment Trusts. The Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct falls under corporate finance advisers (2.3) and the share registrars code under share registration (2.5), while the Guidelines on Online Distribution and Advisory Platforms is a guideline, not a code. (PDF p.104)

section: Special Guidelines and Guidance Notes issued by the SFC
question: Which of the following is classified in the Schedule as a licensing-related guideline issued by the SFC?
options: Client Identity Rule Policy
Management, Supervision and Internal Control Guidelines for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities and Futures Commission
Guidelines on Competence
Guidance Note on Cooperation with the SFC
explanation: The Guidelines on Competence appear under item 3.1, licensing-related guidelines, together with the Licensing Handbook, the Fit and Proper Guidelines and the Guidelines on Continuous Professional Training. The Client Identity Rule Policy and the Management, Supervision and Internal Control Guidelines are listed under item 3.2, business conduct-related (general), and the Guidance Note on Cooperation with the SFC also falls under the business conduct-related list. (PDF p.104)

section: Special Guidelines and Guidance Notes issued by the SFC
question: The SFC's Guideline on Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Financing of Terrorism is stated to apply to licensed corporations and which other group?
options: Registered institutions
SFC-licensed Virtual Asset Service Providers
Exchange participants
Approved share registrars
explanation: The guideline is titled "Guideline on Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Financing of Terrorism (For Licensed Corporations and SFC-licensed Virtual Asset Service Providers)", and a companion guideline covers associated entities of those two groups. Registered institutions, exchange participants and approved share registrars are not the groups named in the guideline's title. (PDF p.104)

section: Special Guidelines and Guidance Notes issued by the SFC
question: Ms Wong is a responsible officer at a licensed corporation that offers internet trading to clients. She wants SFC guidance on reducing cybersecurity threats to the firm's online trading system. Which guideline should she consult?
options: Guidelines on Online Distribution and Advisory Platforms
Guidelines for Virtual Asset Trading Platform Operators
Guidelines for Reducing and Mitigating Hacking Risks Associated with Internet Trading
Guidelines on Marketing Materials for Listed Structured Products
explanation: The Guidelines for Reducing and Mitigating Hacking Risks Associated with Internet Trading directly address hacking risks in internet trading. The Guidelines on Online Distribution and Advisory Platforms concern distribution and advisory platforms rather than hacking risks, the Guidelines for Virtual Asset Trading Platform Operators apply to virtual asset platforms, and the Guidelines on Marketing Materials for Listed Structured Products deal with marketing materials. (PDF p.105)

section: Special Guidelines and Guidance Notes issued by the SFC
question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, is preparing an advertisement for a collective investment scheme authorized under the Product Codes. Which SFC guideline is directly relevant to this advertisement?
options: Guidelines on Marketing Materials for Listed Structured Products
Advertising Guidelines Applicable to Collective Investment Schemes Authorized under the Product Codes
Guidelines on Disclosure of Inside Information
Guidance Note on Short Position Reporting
explanation: The Advertising Guidelines Applicable to Collective Investment Schemes Authorized under the Product Codes match the scenario exactly. The Guidelines on Marketing Materials for Listed Structured Products cover listed structured products rather than authorized collective investment schemes, the Guidelines on Disclosure of Inside Information concern inside information, and the Guidance Note on Short Position Reporting concerns short position reporting. (PDF p.105)

section: Special Guidelines and Guidance Notes issued by the SFC
question: Which SFC guidance note covers stock lending record keeping requirements?
options: The Guidance Note on Short Selling Reporting and Stock Lending Record Keeping Requirements
Guidance Note on Short Position Reporting
Guidance Note on Position Limits and Large Open Position Reporting Requirements
Client Identity Rule Policy
explanation: The Guidance Note on Short Selling Reporting and Stock Lending Record Keeping Requirements expressly covers stock lending record keeping. The Guidance Note on Short Position Reporting deals only with short position reporting, the position limits guidance note deals with position limits and large open position reporting, and the Client Identity Rule Policy concerns client identity, not stock lending records. (PDF p.105)

question: According to the Topic 4 table of contents, Part 2 of the Securities and Futures (Client Securities) Rules covers client securities and securities collateral received or held by intermediaries other than in the conduct of which regulated activity?
options: Type 1 regulated activity
Type 8 regulated activity
Type 9 regulated activity
Type 13 regulated activity
explanation: The table of contents describes Part 2 as covering client securities and securities collateral received or held by intermediaries "other than in conduct of Type 13 regulated activity" and their associated entities. Types 1, 8 and 9 are not the activity carved out in this heading. (PDF p.106)

question: Under the "Capital requirements" section of Topic 4, which set of concepts is listed in connection with the Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules?
options: Liquid assets, ranking liabilities and liquid capital
Fixed assets, contingent liabilities and net capital
Client securities, client money and securities collateral
Reporting obligations, record keeping obligations and Legal Entity Identifiers
explanation: The Topic 4 table of contents lists "Liquid assets, ranking liabilities and liquid capital" under the capital requirements section, along with paid-up share capital requirements. "Fixed assets, contingent liabilities and net capital" is not the wording used, client securities and client money belong to sections 3 and 4, and reporting obligations and Legal Entity Identifiers belong to the OTC derivative transactions section. (PDF p.106)

question: Mr Chan's firm plans to provide automated trading services and also carry on securities margin financing. According to the Topic 4 table of contents, how are these two activities treated?
options: Both are listed as regulated activities under the SFO
Only providing automated trading services is listed as a regulated activity under the SFO
Only securities margin financing is listed as a regulated activity under the SFO
Neither is listed as a regulated activity under the SFO
explanation: Section 8 of the Topic 4 table of contents, "Regulated activities under the SFO", lists both "Providing automated trading services" and "Securities margin financing" among the regulated activities. The other options wrongly exclude one or both activities even though each appears in the list. (PDF p.107)

section: Provision of contract notes, statements of account and receipts to clients
question: According to the Topic 4 table of contents, the section on provision of documents to clients covers contract notes, daily statements of account, monthly statements of account and which other document?
options: Audit reports
Receipts
Trade invoices
Annual financial statements
explanation: Section 6 of the Topic 4 table of contents lists contract notes, daily statements of account, monthly statements of account and receipts (plus "other matters"). Audit matters are covered separately in section 7, and trade invoices and annual financial statements do not appear in this section's headings. (PDF p.107)

question: According to the Topic 4 table of contents, which of the following matters is covered under the section on open-ended fund companies ("OFCs")?
options: Share capital of an OFC
Paid-up share capital requirements for licensed corporations
Legal Entity Identifiers
Mandatory clearing obligation
explanation: Section 11 on OFCs lists "Share capital of an OFC", along with OFC registration, formation and power, shareholder meetings and resolutions, operators of the OFC, and financial statements and reports. Paid-up share capital requirements appear under the capital requirements section, Legal Entity Identifiers under the OTC derivative reporting section, and the mandatory clearing obligation under the clearing of OTC derivative transactions section. (PDF p.108)

question: To which entities do the Securities and Futures (Client Money) Rules apply?
options: All intermediaries, including registered institutions
Licensed corporations (not registered institutions) and associated entities of intermediaries
Registered institutions only
Associated entities only, but not licensed corporations
explanation: The Client Money Rules apply to licensed corporations (not registered institutions) and to associated entities of intermediaries. Option A describes the Client Securities Rules, which apply to all intermediaries and associated entities. Options C and D invert or truncate the actual scope. (PDF p.109)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: How many distinct types of regulated activity are specified under the single licensing system in Part V, SFO?
explanation: Part V, SFO sets up a single licensing system covering 13 specified types of regulated activity, listed in Schedule 5, SFO. The distractors 10, 11 and 12 are plausible because Types 11 and 12 are not yet fully in force, but the SFO still specifies 13 types in total. (PDF p.112)

section: Fit and proper guidelines for licensed representatives and responsible officers
question: Under the character and integrity requirements for individual applicants, which of the following must an applicant satisfy the SFC (or HKMA) about?
options: That he has never held shares in any listed corporation
That he has not been a director or substantial shareholder of, or been involved in managing, an insolvent corporation
That he has at least five years of industry experience
That he holds a university degree in finance
explanation: Section 1.23 lists not having been a director or substantial shareholder of, or involved in managing, an insolvent corporation among the character and integrity requirements. Holding shares in a listed corporation is not prohibited. Specific years of experience and educational qualifications relate to the competence tests, not the character and integrity head, and no five-year or degree requirement is stated. (PDF p.119)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: Which types of regulated activity are noted as not yet fully in force?
options: Type 10 and Type 13
Type 11 and Type 12
Type 12 and Type 13
Type 3 and Type 8
explanation: The note states that Type 11 (dealing in or advising on OTC derivative products) and Type 12 (providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions) regulated activities are not yet fully in force. Types 10 and 13 are in force; Types 3 and 8 are the activities for which AFIs are exempted from registration, a different point. (PDF p.112)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: Under Schedule 5, SFO, Type 13 regulated activity is:
options: Providing credit rating services
Providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions
Providing depositary services for relevant CISs
Providing automated trading services
explanation: Type 13 is providing depositary services for relevant CISs. Providing credit rating services is Type 10, client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions is Type 12, and providing automated trading services is Type 7. (PDF p.112)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: An authorised financial institution (AFI) is exempted from the requirement to register with the SFC when carrying out which regulated activities?
options: Type 1 or Type 2
Type 3 or Type 8
Type 4 or Type 9
Type 11 or Type 12
explanation: An AFI carrying out Type 3 (leveraged foreign exchange trading) or Type 8 (securities margin financing) regulated activity is exempted from the registration requirement. Types 1, 2, 4 and 9 would still require registration if conducted by an AFI. Types 11 and 12 are the activities not yet fully in force, a different point. (PDF p.113)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: A Type 1 licence or registration also covers which other regulated activities, if their conduct is wholly incidental to the Type 1 activity?
options: Type 4, Type 6 and Type 9
Type 2, Type 5 and Type 9
Type 4, Type 5 and Type 7
Type 3, Type 6 and Type 8
explanation: A Type 1 licence or registration covers Type 4, Type 6 and Type 9 regulated activities if their conduct is wholly incidental to the Type 1 activity (it also covers Type 8 as specified in the summary table). Type 2 covers Type 5 or 9, so option B confuses the Type 2 rule; options C and D mix in Types 5, 7 and 3, which are not covered by a Type 1 licence. (PDF p.113)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: In what circumstance may the SFC approve a licensed representative's accreditation to more than one licensed corporation?
options: When the corporations are within the same group of companies
When the corporations conduct different types of regulated activity
When the representative has been licensed for at least five years
Never; a licensed representative must always be accredited to exactly one principal
explanation: A licensed representative will usually be accredited to one principal, but the SFC may approve accreditation to more than one licensed corporation within the same group of companies. Option D is wrong because multi-accreditation is possible with SFC approval. Different activity types and years of experience are not the stated conditions. (PDF p.113)

section: Licensing and registration requirements under the Securities and Futures Ordinance ("SFO")
question: An individual may engage in a regulated activity for a registered institution provided that:
options: He is licensed by the SFC as a licensed representative
His name is entered in the register maintained by the HKMA as engaged by the registered institution in respect of that regulated activity
He is approved by the SFC as a responsible officer
He holds a provisional licence issued by the HKMA
explanation: For registered institutions, the individual's name must be entered in the register maintained by the HKMA as engaged by that institution in respect of the regulated activity. A licensed representative's licence and responsible officer approval are SFC mechanisms for licensed corporations, not registered institutions. Provisional licences are granted by the SFC to applicants for representative licences, not by the HKMA. (PDF p.113)

question: Where the board of an intermediary delegates decision-making powers to senior management, including managers-in-charge of core functions (MICs), who remains responsible for the oversight of those powers?
options: The MICs to whom the powers were delegated
The board
The responsible officers
The SFC
explanation: The board remains responsible for the oversight of delegated powers and is ultimately responsible for the conduct, operations and financial soundness of the intermediary. MICs and responsible officers exercise delegated or supervisory functions but the board's responsibility is not transferred to them. The SFC is the regulator, not the body responsible for the intermediary's internal oversight. (PDF p.114)

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the conditions for a person to be a responsible officer?
options: He actively participates in or supervises a regulated activity
He is nominated by the licensed corporation as a responsible officer
He is approved by the SFC as a responsible officer
He is entered in the register maintained by the HKMA
explanation: A responsible officer is a licensed representative who actively participates in or supervises a regulated activity, is nominated by the licensed corporation, and is approved by the SFC. Entry in the HKMA register is the mechanism for individuals engaged by registered institutions, not for responsible officers of licensed corporations. (PDF p.114)

question: Under s. 125, SFO, each licensed corporation must appoint:
options: Not less than two responsible officers, at least one of whom must be an executive director
Not less than one responsible officer, who must be an executive director
Not less than two responsible officers, both of whom must be executive directors
Not less than two executive officers, at least one of whom must be a responsible officer
explanation: Each licensed corporation must appoint not less than two responsible officers, at least one of whom must be an executive director (s. 125, SFO). Option B understates the minimum number; option C overstates the executive director requirement; option D confuses responsible officers (licensed corporations) with executive officers (registered institutions). (PDF p.114)

question: Under s. 118(1)(a)(ii), SFO, a licensed corporation must ensure that, for each regulated activity, it has:
options: At least two responsible officers based in Hong Kong at all times
At least one responsible officer available at all times to supervise the business of that regulated activity, and who is based in Hong Kong
At least one executive officer available during business hours
At least one responsible officer, who may be based anywhere provided he is contactable
explanation: For each regulated activity, the licensed corporation must have at least one responsible officer available at all times to supervise the business of that activity, and who is based in Hong Kong. Option A confuses this with the separate requirement of not less than two responsible officers overall. Option C refers to executive officers, who relate to registered institutions. Option D is wrong because the responsible officer must be based in Hong Kong. (PDF p.114)

question: How does s. 113, SFO define an "executive director" in relation to a licensed corporation?
options: A director who holds more than 10% of the issued shares of the corporation
A director who actively participates in, or is responsible for directly supervising, the business of a regulated activity for which the corporation is licensed
A director appointed under the Banking Ordinance to supervise regulated activities
Any director who attends board meetings of the corporation
explanation: An executive director is one who actively participates in, or is responsible for directly supervising, the business of a regulated activity for which the corporation is licensed (s. 113, SFO). The 10% shareholding threshold relates to substantial shareholders, not executive directors. The Banking Ordinance appointment requirement relates to executive officers of registered institutions. Mere attendance at board meetings is not the definition. (PDF p.114)

question: A licensed corporation carries on a regulated activity in contravention of the responsible officer appointment requirements without reasonable excuse. What penalty applies?
options: A fine at level 6 and, for a continuing offence, a further fine of HK$2,000 for every day the offence continues
A fine at level 6 and, for a continuing offence, a further fine of HK$20,000 for every day the offence continues
A fine at level 2 and, for a continuing offence, a further fine of HK$2,000 for every day the offence continues
Automatic revocation of the corporation's licence
explanation: The offence renders the licensed corporation liable to a fine at level 6 and, for a continuing offence, a further fine of HK$2,000 for every day during which the offence continues. Options B and C alter the daily fine amount or the fine level. Automatic revocation of the licence is not the stated consequence. (PDF p.115)

question: The SFC generally expects which two types of managers-in-charge (MICs) to be licensed and approved as responsible officers?
options: Those undertaking the compliance function and the internal audit function
Those undertaking the overall management oversight function and the key business line function
Those undertaking the risk management function and the finance function
All MICs of core functions, without exception
explanation: The SFC generally expects MICs undertaking the overall management oversight function (directing and overseeing the effective day-to-day management of overall operations) and the key business line function (directing and overseeing a line of business comprising one or more regulated activities) to be licensed and approved as responsible officers. Compliance, internal audit, risk management and finance MICs are not named, and the expectation applies to those two functions, not all MICs. (PDF p.115)

question: In relation to an associated entity of an intermediary, an "executive officer" under Schedule 1, SFO is:
options: A responsible officer of the associated entity
A director of the associated entity who is directly responsible for supervising the receiving or holding by the associated entity of client assets of the intermediary
Any employee handling client assets of the intermediary
The substantial shareholder of the associated entity
explanation: For an associated entity of an intermediary, the executive officer is a director who is directly responsible for supervising the receiving or holding by the associated entity of client assets of the intermediary. Responsible officers relate to licensed corporations, not associated entities. The definition requires a director with direct supervisory responsibility, not any employee handling client assets, and substantial shareholding is a separate concept. (PDF p.115)

question: How many executive officers must a registered institution appoint in respect of each type of regulated activity?
options: At least one, who must be based in Hong Kong
At least two, with at least one available at all times to supervise the business of the regulated activity
At least two, both of whom must be executive directors
At least three, with at least two available at all times
explanation: A registered institution must appoint at least two executive officers in respect of each type of regulated activity, and at all times at least one executive officer must be available to supervise the business of the regulated activity. Option A understates the minimum; option C imports the executive director requirement that applies to responsible officers of licensed corporations; option D overstates both numbers. (PDF p.115)

question: Ms Lee, together with her associates, acquires an interest of 12% of the total number of issued shares of a licensed corporation. Which statement is correct?
options: She is not a substantial shareholder because the threshold is 35%
She is a substantial shareholder and must first be approved by the SFC before becoming or continuing to be one
She is a substantial shareholder but only needs to notify the SFC after the acquisition
She is not a substantial shareholder because she does not hold the shares alone
explanation: A person who alone or together with his associates has an interest of more than 10% of the total issued shares of the corporation is a substantial shareholder, and a person may not become or continue to be a substantial shareholder of a licensed corporation without first being approved by the SFC (s. 131 and s. 6 of Part 1 of Schedule 1, SFO). The 35% figure relates to indirect voting power through another corporation. Prior approval, not after-the-fact notification, is required, and interests held together with associates count. (PDF p.115)

question: Under the indirect test, a person is a substantial shareholder of a corporation if he is able to exercise:
options: 10% or more of the voting power of another corporation which has more than 35% of the voting power of the corporation
35% or more of the voting power of another corporation at a general meeting which in turn has more than 10% of the voting power of the corporation at a general meeting
More than 50% of the voting power of another corporation which holds any shares in the corporation
35% or more of the voting power of the corporation directly at a general meeting
explanation: The indirect limb requires the person to be able to exercise 35% or more of the voting power of another corporation at a general meeting, which in turn has more than 10% of the voting power of the corporation at a general meeting. Option A reverses the two percentages. Option C uses a 50% figure not in the definition. Option D misapplies 35% to direct voting power, where the direct test is more than 10%. (PDF p.116)

question: As front-line regulator of registered institutions, the HKMA applies all SFC regulatory criteria EXCEPT:
options: Fitness and properness, business conduct and capital adequacy
Capital adequacy, the handling of client money and the audit requirements
Business conduct, record keeping and contract notes
Fitness and properness and the handling of client securities
explanation: Registered institutions are jointly regulated by the HKMA and the SFC, with the HKMA as front-line regulator applying all SFC regulatory criteria, including fitness and properness and business conduct, other than capital adequacy, the handling of client money and the audit requirements. Options A and D wrongly include fitness and properness or business conduct among the exceptions, and option C lists items not stated as carve-outs. (PDF p.116)

question: Ms Cheung, a solicitor, gives advice on securities (Type 4) to a client wholly incidentally to her legal practice. Which statement is correct?
options: She must obtain a Type 4 licence before giving the advice
She does not need licensing because such activity is specifically excluded from the definitions of regulated activities in Part 2, Schedule 5, SFO
She must register with the HKMA before giving the advice
She may only give the advice if supervised by a responsible officer
explanation: Certified public accountants, solicitors and counsel conducting Type 4, Type 5, Type 6 and Type 9 regulated activities wholly incidental to their professions are specifically excluded from the definitions of regulated activities in Part 2 of Schedule 5, SFO and do not need licensing for those activities. Options A and C wrongly require licensing or HKMA registration, and option D imposes a supervision condition that does not apply to excluded persons. (PDF p.116)

question: ABC Ltd is licensed for Type 9 (asset management) regulated activity and deals in securities solely for the purposes of its asset management business. Does it need a separate Type 1 licence?
options: Yes, dealing in securities always requires a Type 1 licence
No, persons licensed for Type 9 who carry out Type 1, Type 2, Type 4 and Type 5 activities solely for the purposes of their Type 9 activity are excluded
Yes, unless the dealing is conducted through a registered institution
No, because asset managers are exempt from all licensing requirements
explanation: Persons licensed or registered for Type 9 regulated activity who carry out Type 1, Type 2, Type 4 and Type 5 regulated activities solely for the purposes of their Type 9 activity are excluded and do not need separate licensing for those activities. Option A ignores this exclusion. Option C adds a condition not in the text. Option D overstates the position; asset managers still need their Type 9 licence. (PDF p.116)

question: According to the summary table, a licence for Type 2 regulated activity may also cover which other regulated activities, if wholly incidental to Type 2?
options: Type 4, 6 or 9
Type 5 or 9
Type 1, 2, 4 or 5
Type 1 only
explanation: The summary table shows that a Type 2 licence covers Type 5 or 9 if wholly incidental to Type 2. Type 4, 6 or 9 is what a Type 1 licence covers; Type 1, 2, 4 or 5 is what a Type 9 licence covers; Type 1 only is what a Type 4 or Type 6 licence covers. (PDF p.117)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, resigns from his accredited licensed corporation on 1 March. Which statement correctly describes the consequences?
options: The corporation must notify the SFC within seven business days, and Mr Chan has 180 days to transfer his accreditation, failing which his licence is deemed revoked upon the cessation
Mr Chan must notify the SFC within seven business days, and he has 90 days to transfer his accreditation
The corporation must notify the SFC within 14 business days, and Mr Chan has 180 days to transfer his accreditation
The corporation must notify the SFC within seven business days, and Mr Chan's licence is immediately revoked on cessation
explanation: The licensed corporation must notify the SFC within seven business days after the cessation, and the representative has 180 days to transfer his accreditation to another licensed corporation, failing which his licence is deemed revoked upon the cessation. Option B wrongly places the notification duty on the representative and shortens the transfer period to 90 days; option C doubles the notification deadline; option D ignores the 180-day transfer window. (PDF p.117)

question: XYZ Corp, which principally carries on its business overseas, applies for a temporary licence to conduct regulated activity in Hong Kong. Which statement is correct?
options: A temporary licence may be granted for up to three months, but not for Type 3 and Type 7 to Type 9 regulated activities, and the same person may not hold temporary licences for more than six months in total over a 24-month period
A temporary licence may be granted for up to six months for any of the 13 regulated activities
A temporary licence may be granted for up to three months, and there is no limit on how often it may be renewed
A temporary licence may be granted for up to twelve months, but not for Type 1 and Type 2 regulated activities
explanation: The SFC may grant a temporary licence for a period not exceeding three months to a corporation principally carrying on business overseas, for regulated activities other than Type 3 and Type 7 to Type 9, and the total period of temporary licences held by the same person may not exceed six months over a 24-month period. Option B wrongly extends the single-licence period and removes the activity exclusions; option C ignores the six-months-per-24-months cap; option D invents a twelve-month period and the wrong excluded activities. (PDF p.118)

question: When is a provisional licence granted to an applicant for a representative licence revoked?
options: Three months after it is granted
Upon the approval or refusal by the SFC of the representative's licence application
Only if the licence application is refused
After six months over a 24-month period
explanation: Provisional licences cover the period before the SFC finalises a representative licensing application and are revoked upon the approval or refusal of that application. Option C is wrong because revocation also occurs on approval, when the full licence takes over. The three-month and six-months-per-24-months limits apply to temporary licences, not provisional licences. (PDF p.118)

question: Mr Wong is not licensed for any regulated activity. He starts describing himself as a "stockbroker" on his business cards. Which statement is correct?
options: This is permitted as long as he does not actually deal in securities
This is prohibited; a person not licensed for Type 1 regulated activity cannot use specified titles such as stockbroker set out in Schedule 6, SFO
This is permitted if he registers the title with the HKMA
This is prohibited only if he uses the title in written advertisements
explanation: Unlicensed or unregistered persons may not use specified titles set out in Schedule 6, SFO; a person not licensed for Type 1 regulated activity cannot describe himself as a bond broker, bond dealer, securities dealer, stock dealer or stockbroker. Options A and D invent conditions under which use would be allowed, and the HKMA has no title-registration role here. (PDF p.118)

section: Fit and proper guidelines for corporate applicants and intermediaries
question: Under the fit and proper guidelines on financial status, an intermediary must be able to comply with which capital requirements?
options: Licensed corporations: the FRR; registered institutions: the HKMA's capital adequacy requirements
Licensed corporations: the HKMA's capital adequacy requirements; registered institutions: the FRR
Both licensed corporations and registered institutions: the FRR
Both licensed corporations and registered institutions: the HKMA's capital adequacy requirements
explanation: For licensed corporations the applicable capital requirements are the Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules (FRR), while for registered institutions they are the HKMA's capital adequacy requirements. Option B reverses the two regimes, and options C and D wrongly apply a single regime to both categories. (PDF p.120)

question: What is the minimum paid-up share capital that a sponsor or compliance adviser must maintain at all times?
options: HK$5 million
HK$10 million
HK$30 million
HK$100 million
explanation: Sponsors and compliance advisers must meet minimum capital requirements, including a minimum paid-up share capital of HK$10 million at all times. HK$30 million is the upper end of the general paid-up share capital scale for licensed corporations under the FRR, not the sponsor figure. HK$5 million and HK$100 million are not amounts stated anywhere in the text. (PDF p.121)

question: How many Principals must a sponsor firm appoint to be in charge of the supervision of the transaction team at all times?
options: At least one
At least two
At least three
One per transaction
explanation: Sponsors and compliance advisers must have at least two Principals appointed by the firm to be in charge of supervising the transaction team at all times; each Principal must be a responsible officer (licensed corporation) or executive officer (registered institution). "At least one" confuses this with the TCRO rule, under which at least one TCRO must supervise each Codes transaction. "At least three" and "one per transaction" are not requirements stated in the text. (PDF p.121)

question: Under the TC Adviser Guidelines, which of the following supervision requirements applies to each transaction subject to the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs?
options: At least two TCROs must jointly supervise the transaction
At least one TCRO must supervise and be involved in the transaction
Any licensed representative of the TC Adviser may supervise the transaction
A Principal appointed under the Sponsor Guidelines must supervise the transaction
explanation: At least one TCRO must supervise and be involved in each transaction subject to the Codes that the TC Adviser is advising on. "Two" confuses this with the sponsor requirement of at least two Principals. Licensed representatives of TC Advisers must meet additional eligibility and CPT requirements but are not stated to be the supervisors; sponsor Principals relate to sponsor work, not Codes transactions. (PDF p.121)

question: Under the SFC's Guidelines on CPT, what is the minimum number of CPT hours a licensed representative must undertake per calendar year?
options: 5 hours
10 hours
12 hours
2.5 hours
explanation: A licensed representative or relevant individual must undertake a minimum of ten CPT hours per calendar year, regardless of the number and types of regulated activities engaged in. Twelve hours is the minimum for responsible officers and executive officers. Five hours is the minimum on topics directly relevant to the licensed regulated activities, and 2.5 hours is the sponsor-related or Codes-related CPT requirement. (PDF p.122)

question: A responsible officer must undertake a minimum of twelve CPT hours per calendar year. What must the two hours in excess of the licensed representative minimum relate to?
options: Regulatory compliance
Ethics
Sponsor-related work
Business management
explanation: A responsible officer or executive officer must undertake a minimum of twelve CPT hours per calendar year, in which the two additional CPT hours should be related to regulatory compliance. Ethics is the subject of the separate two-hour annual ethics/compliance requirement and the one-off requirement for new practitioners. Sponsor-related CPT (2.5 hours) applies only to those engaging in sponsor work, and business management is merely a relevant CPT topic for responsible officers, not the prescribed subject of the two extra hours. (PDF p.122)

question: Ms Lee, a licensed representative, engages in sponsor work. In each calendar year, how many hours of sponsor-related CPT must she take as a minimum?
options: Not less than 2 hours
Not less than 2.5 hours
Not less than 5 hours
Not less than 10 hours
explanation: Those engaging in sponsor work must take not less than 2.5 hours of sponsor-related CPT per calendar year (the same 2.5-hour figure applies to TC Advisers and TCROs for Codes-related CPT). Two hours is the annual ethics or compliance requirement, five hours is the requirement for topics directly relevant to the licensed regulated activities, and ten hours is her overall annual CPT minimum as a licensed representative. (PDF p.122)

question: Which of the following activities will NOT count towards CPT under the Guidelines on CPT?
options: Distance learning with submission of assignments
Publication of papers
Reading financial journals and newspapers
Delivery of speeches
explanation: Reading financial journals, newspapers and other technical publications, and undertaking normal work activities that do not involve interaction with other persons, will not count as CPT. Distance learning with submission of assignments, publication of papers and delivery of speeches are all expressly listed as ways CPT may be achieved. (PDF p.122)

question: Which of the following is a relevant CPT topic prescribed for responsible officers IN ADDITION to the topics applicable to licensees generally?
options: Fintech
Cybersecurity
Risk management and control strategies
Environmental, social and governance (ESG)
explanation: Risk management and control strategies is one of the additional topics listed for responsible officers, together with business management, general management and supervisory skills, macro and micro economic analysis, and financial reporting and quantitative analysis. Fintech, cybersecurity and ESG are all topics listed for licensees generally, so they are not additional responsible officer topics. (PDF p.123)

question: An executive officer of a registered institution fails to comply with the Guidelines on CPT. Which regulator may take disciplinary proceedings against him?
options: The SFC
The HKMA
The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
The SFC and the HKMA jointly
explanation: Failure to comply with the Guidelines on CPT may lead to disciplinary proceedings by the HKMA against executive officers and relevant individuals of registered institutions. The SFC takes such proceedings against responsible officers and licensed representatives of licensed corporations, not against registered institution staff. The Stock Exchange is not given any disciplinary role here, and the text does not describe joint proceedings. (PDF p.123)

section: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority requirements for staff engaged in regulated activities
question: Relevant individuals of a registered institution who conduct regulated activities must have their names entered in a register maintained by which body?
options: The SFC
The HKMA
The registered institution itself
The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
explanation: Relevant individuals who conduct regulated activities must have their names entered in a register of such persons maintained by the HKMA. The SFC maintains the Public Register under the Securities and Futures (Licensing and Registration) (Information) Rules, which is a different register. The registered institution itself and the Stock Exchange are not stated to maintain this register. (PDF p.124)

section: The Hong Kong Monetary Authority requirements for staff engaged in regulated activities
question: A registered institution is registered for Type 1 regulated activity. Which of the following correctly states the executive officer requirements?
options: At least one executive officer must be appointed, available at all times
At least two executive officers must be appointed, and at least one must be available at all times to supervise the business
At least two executive officers must be available at all times to supervise the business
One executive officer must be appointed for all regulated activities combined
explanation: At least two executive officers must be appointed in respect of each type of regulated activity, and at all times there must be at least one executive officer available to supervise the business of the regulated activity concerned. The first option understates the appointment requirement of two; the third overstates the availability requirement, which is one at all times, not two. The appointment requirement applies per type of regulated activity, not one officer covering all activities combined. (PDF p.124)

question: Which of the following is NOT a reason given for requiring a licensed corporation to have enough capital?
options: To support the level of its business activities
To meet its liabilities as they fall due
To guarantee a minimum level of profitability
To provide a buffer in the event of sudden market changes, disruptions or loss of confidence
explanation: Guaranteeing profitability is not mentioned; capital requirements are about financial soundness, not profit. The three stated reasons are that the corporation must have enough capital to support the level of its business activities, to meet its liabilities as they fall due, and to provide a buffer in the event of sudden market changes, disruptions or loss of confidence. (PDF p.125)

section: Capital requirements for licensed corporations and registered institutions
question: Which of the following statements about the Financial Resources Rules (FRR) is correct?
options: Both licensed corporations and registered institutions must meet the FRR set by the SFC
Licensed corporations must meet the FRR set by the SFC, but registered institutions need only meet the capital requirements set by the HKMA
