Registered institutions must meet the HKMA capital requirements and additionally demonstrate compliance with the SFC's capital requirements
Neither licensed corporations nor registered institutions are subject to the FRR
explanation: Licensed corporations (but not registered institutions) must meet the FRR set by the SFC; for registered institutions it is sufficient to meet the capital requirements set by the HKMA. The text expressly states there is no additional requirement for registered institutions to demonstrate they also meet SFC capital requirements, which eliminates the first and third options. The FRR requirements for licensed corporations are specified in ss. 145 to 147, SFO, so the fourth option is wrong. (PDF p.125)

question: For the purposes of the FRR, on what basis must the individual items of a licensed corporation's balance sheet be calculated?
options: On settlement dates
On trade dates
On either trade or settlement dates, at the licensee's option
On the dates the SFC specifies for each transaction
explanation: The individual items must be calculated on trade dates and not on settlement dates. Settlement dates are expressly excluded, and there is no option for the licensee to choose between the two bases. The SFC does not set the dates transaction by transaction; the other prescribed bases are that the balance sheet follows generally accepted accounting principles, recognises the substance of transactions, and normally includes off-balance-sheet liabilities. (PDF p.126)

section: Liquid assets, ranking liabilities and liquid capital
question: Under the FRR approach to liquid assets, what "haircut" deduction is applied to fixed assets such as real estate and intangible assets such as goodwill and patents?
options: 0%, because they are valuable assets
50%, reflecting moderate liquidity
100%, so they are marked down to nil value
A percentage set case by case by the SFC for each licensee
explanation: Fixed assets and intangible assets such as goodwill and patents cannot be converted into cash quickly, so they are given a haircut of 100% and marked down to nil value. A 0% deduction (full 100% value) applies at the other extreme to cash and near-cash items that are readily available. The intermediate haircut percentages for other asset classes are set by rules based on the regulators' experience, not negotiated case by case per licensee, and 50% is not the stated figure for fixed or intangible assets. (PDF p.126)

question: Under the FRR, how is liquid capital calculated?
options: Liquid assets minus ranking liabilities
Liquid assets plus ranking liabilities
Total assets minus total liabilities
Paid-up share capital minus ranking liabilities
explanation: Liquid capital = liquid assets minus ranking liabilities, after all the haircut and liability adjustments have been made. Adding ranking liabilities is the opposite of the formula. Total assets minus total liabilities is an unadjusted balance-sheet net asset figure, not liquid capital, and paid-up share capital is a separate requirement that does not enter this formula. (PDF p.127)

question: Which of the following licensed corporations have LOWER required liquid capital (RLC) requirements?
options: All corporations licensed for Type 1 regulated activity
Approved introducing agents and corporations licensed for Type 4, 5, 6, 9 and 10 regulated activities which do not hold client assets
Sponsors and compliance advisers
All licensed corporations that maintain liquid capital above 120% of the RLC
explanation: Approved introducing agents and licensed corporations for Type 4, Type 5, Type 6, Type 9 and Type 10 regulated activities which do not hold client assets have lower RLC requirements. Type 1 dealers are not given this concession. Sponsors and compliance advisers face additional requirements, including HK$10 million minimum paid-up share capital, rather than lower RLC, and 120% of RLC is a notification threshold, not a basis for a lower RLC. (PDF p.127)

question: Mr Chan's licensed corporation conducts two regulated activities with different paid-up share capital requirements under the FRR. What paid-up share capital must the corporation maintain?
options: The sum of the requirements for both activities
The lower of the two requirements
The highest single requirement applicable to the individual activities
A flat HK$10 million regardless of the activities conducted
explanation: If a licensed corporation conducts more than one regulated activity, it must maintain the highest single requirement applicable to the individual activities; requirements run up to HK$30 million depending on the regulated activity. The requirements are not summed and the lower figure would not satisfy the higher activity's requirement. HK$10 million is the sponsor/compliance adviser minimum, not a flat figure for all licensed corporations. (PDF p.127)

question: A licensed corporation must notify the SFC in writing if its liquid capital falls below which percentage of its required liquid capital (RLC)?
options: 100% only
explanation: Written notification is required if liquid capital falls below 120% of the RLC, or falls below the RLC itself, or falls below 50% of the last liquid capital reported. Because notification is triggered at the 120% level, "100% only" is wrong, and 150% and 110% are not thresholds stated in the text. Notification is also required if information in an earlier return has become materially false or misleading. (PDF p.128)

question: Mr Chan manages a corporation licensed only for Type 9 (asset management) regulated activity that does not hold client assets. How frequently must it make returns of its financial position to the SFC?
options: Monthly returns of liquid capital and RLC with supporting information
Half-yearly returns containing financial statements with less detail specified
Quarterly returns of liquid capital and RLC
An annual return only, within one month of the anniversary of licensing
explanation: Corporations licensed only for pure advising activities (Types 4, 5, 6, 9 and 10) not involving the holding of client assets need only make a half-yearly return containing financial statements with less detail specified. Monthly returns of liquid capital and RLC apply to corporations licensed for the other regulated activities. Quarterly returns are not mentioned, and the annual return within one month of the licensing anniversary is a separate requirement under the Licensing and Registration (Information) Rules, not the FRR financial return. (PDF p.128)

section: Request and approvals by the SFC
question: On application by a licensed corporation, the SFC may approve the exclusion of which items from ranking liabilities in the calculation of liquid capital?
options: Redeemable shares and subordinated loans
Fixed assets and intangible assets
Client money and client securities
All loans from the corporation's shareholders
explanation: The SFC may, on application by a licensed corporation, approve the exclusion of redeemable shares and subordinated loans from ranking liabilities in the calculation of its liquid capital (as approved redeemable shares and approved subordinated loans). Fixed and intangible assets are asset items dealt with by 100% haircuts, not liability exclusions. Client money and client securities are client assets protected under separate rules, and only loans the lenders have agreed to subordinate qualify, not shareholder loans generally. (PDF p.128)

question: Under which section of the SFO has the SFC made the Client Securities Rules?
options: Section 104
Section 148
Section 149
Section 164
explanation: The Client Securities Rules, governing the holding of and dealing with client assets and securities collateral, are made under s. 148, SFO. Section 149 is the rule-making power for client money, s. 104 concerns SFC authorisation of collective investment schemes, and s. 164 relates to the definition of excluded persons who may receive or hold client assets. (PDF p.129)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, suggests that his brother's unlicensed trading company hold client assets of his firm in Hong Kong. Which of the following persons may NOT receive or hold client assets of an intermediary in Hong Kong?
options: The intermediary itself
An associated entity of the intermediary
An authorized financial institution (AFI), as an excluded person
An unlicensed company that has not been approved by the SFC
explanation: No person may receive or hold client assets of an intermediary in Hong Kong unless the person is the intermediary, an associated entity of the intermediary, or an excluded person. An unlicensed, unapproved company falls into none of these categories. An AFI is an excluded person, as is another intermediary holding client securities collateral under financial accommodation arrangements or a company approved by the SFC (s. 164, SFO). (PDF p.129)

question: Which of the following client securities ARE within the scope of the Client Securities Rules?
options: Overseas stocks received in Hong Kong by an intermediary
Hong Kong stocks held overseas on a client's behalf
Securities listed on the SEHK received in Hong Kong by an intermediary in the course of conducting a regulated activity
Client securities in an account in the client's own name set up with a person other than the intermediary or its associated entity
explanation: The rules apply to client securities or securities collateral that are listed or traded on a recognised stock market (currently only the SEHK) or are interests in an SFC-authorised CIS under s. 104, SFO, and that are received or held in Hong Kong by or on behalf of the intermediary in the course of conducting a regulated activity. They apply only to Hong Kong stocks, so overseas stocks are excluded, and only to securities received or held in Hong Kong, so holdings overseas are excluded. They also do not apply to securities in an account in the client's own name set up with persons other than the intermediary or its associated entity. (PDF p.130)

question: A standing authority given by a client who is NOT a professional investor must specify an effective period of not more than:
options: 6 months
12 months
14 days
24 months
explanation: A standing authority must specify a period of not more than 12 months during which it will be effective; no limit has been specified by the SFC for a client who is a professional investor. Six and 24 months are not the stated maximum. Fourteen days is the minimum advance written notice an intermediary must give before expiry to effect a deemed renewal, not the validity period. (PDF p.130)

question: Mr Chan's firm wishes to renew a client's standing authority by the deemed renewal procedure. Which of the following correctly describes the procedure?
options: Give the client written notice at least 14 days before expiry, then provide written confirmation of the renewal within a week after the expiry date
Give the client written notice at least 7 days before expiry, then provide written confirmation within 14 days after the expiry date
Obtain the client's written request before expiry; no confirmation is needed
Give the client oral notice at any time before expiry, then confirm in writing within one month
explanation: Under the deemed renewal procedure, the intermediary or associated entity gives a written notice at least 14 days prior to the expiry date reminding the client of the impending expiry and informing him it will be renewed on the same terms unless he objects, and must then provide written confirmation of the renewal within a week after the expiry date. The second and fourth options reverse or alter these time limits, and oral notice is not provided for. A renewal on the client's written request is the alternative route, not the deemed renewal procedure. (PDF p.131)

section: Depositing or registration of client assets
question: If an intermediary deposits client securities in safe custody, with which of the following may the deposit be made?
options: An AFI, an approved custodian, or another intermediary licensed for Type 1 regulated activity
An AFI, an approved custodian, or another intermediary licensed for Type 9 regulated activity
Any company incorporated in Hong Kong
Only the SEHK
explanation: A deposit must be made with an AFI, an approved custodian, or another intermediary licensed for dealing in securities (Type 1 regulated activity), in safe custody in separate segregated accounts designated as trust accounts or client accounts. A Type 9 (asset management) intermediary is not one of the listed depositees. Any Hong Kong company would not qualify without falling into the listed categories, and the SEHK is the recognised stock market on which qualifying securities are listed, not the prescribed depositee. (PDF p.131)

section: Depositing or registration of client assets
question: If client securities (not securities collateral) are to be registered rather than deposited, in whose name may they be registered?
options: The client or an associated entity of the intermediary
The intermediary itself only
The client, an associated entity, or the intermediary itself
Any officer or employee of the intermediary
explanation: Registration must be in the name of the client on whose behalf the securities were received or an associated entity of the intermediary; registration in the name of the intermediary itself is additionally permitted only in the case of securities collateral. The third option is therefore correct only for securities collateral, not for client securities, and the intermediary alone is never the only permitted name. Transfers to officers or employees of the intermediary under a standing authority are prohibited unless the officer or employee is the client. (PDF p.132)

section: Dealings with client securities and securities collateral
question: Which of the following uses of a client's standing authority is permitted under the Client Securities Rules?
options: Transferring client securities to the personal account of an employee of the intermediary who is not the client
Dealing with client securities in a manner that is unconscionable
Transferring client securities to an account of an entity with a controlling entity relationship with the intermediary, where the account is not a permitted deposit account
Transferring client securities to a segregated trust account of the kind permitted for deposits of client securities
explanation: The standing authority may not be used to transfer client securities or securities collateral to an account of the intermediary, its associated entity or a controlling-entity-related entity unless that account is one of the permitted deposit accounts, so a transfer to such a permitted segregated account is allowed. Transfers to an officer or employee are prohibited unless that person is the client, and any dealing that is unconscionable (contrary to a sense of justice, decency or reasonableness) is prohibited outright. (PDF p.132)

question: Under the Client Securities Rules, an intermediary licensed for Type 1 regulated activity (dealing in securities) that holds a standing authority may use client securities or securities collateral for which of the following purposes?
options: Pledging the client securities with an overseas broker to finance the intermediary's proprietary trading
A stock borrowing and lending transaction
Lending the client securities to the intermediary's directors for their personal use
Depositing the client securities with a private company as collateral for the intermediary's office rental obligations
explanation: With a standing authority, a Type 1 intermediary may use client securities or securities collateral for stock borrowing and lending, re-pledge securities collateral with an AFI to borrow money, or deposit securities collateral with a recognised clearing house or another Type 1 intermediary for settlement obligations. Financing proprietary trading, lending to directors and securing office rent are not among the permitted transactions, and intermediaries may not deal with client securities otherwise than as provided in Part 2 of the Rules. (PDF p.133)

question: Mr Chan is a responsible officer of a firm licensed for Types 1 and 8 regulated activities. On Monday, the aggregate market value of the firm's re-pledged securities collateral (based on Monday's closing prices) stands at 150% of its aggregate margin loans on that day. What must the firm do?
options: Reduce the aggregate value of re-pledged securities collateral by the close of Tuesday so that it does not exceed 140% of aggregate margin loans
Reduce the aggregate value of re-pledged securities collateral to not more than 150% of aggregate margin loans by the close of Monday
Report the position to the SFC within one business day; no reduction is required
Reduce the aggregate value of re-pledged securities collateral to not more than 140% of aggregate margin loans within three business days
explanation: If the aggregate value of re-pledged securities collateral exceeds 140% of aggregate margin loans on a business day, the intermediary must reduce it to not more than 140% by the close of the next business day, here Tuesday. 150% is not a permitted ceiling, no SFC report substitutes for the reduction, and three business days is too long. (PDF p.133)

question: The requirement to ascertain the aggregate market value of re-pledged securities collateral and keep it within 140% of aggregate margin loans applies to intermediaries licensed for which regulated activities?
options: Types 1 and 2
Types 2 and 8
Types 1 and 8
Types 1 and 13
explanation: The 140% re-pledging limit applies to intermediaries licensed for dealing in securities (Type 1) and for SMF (Type 8), with the value ascertained by reference to closing prices of the collateral on that business day. Type 2 is mentioned only as having similar provisions elsewhere in the Client Securities Rules, and Type 13 relates to depositary services, not this limit. (PDF p.133)

question: Within what period must non-compliance with the specified provisions of the Client Securities Rules be reported to the SFC?
options: Within one business day after discovery
Within two business days after discovery
Within three business days after discovery
Within seven business days after discovery
explanation: Non-compliance with the specified provisions of the Client Securities Rules must be reported to the SFC within one business day after discovery. Two business days is the deadline for issuing contract notes, three business days is the Type 13 deadline for depositing CIS money, and seven business days relates to issuing monthly statements - none applies here. (PDF p.133)

question: The Client Money Rules were made by the SFC under which section of the SFO?
explanation: The Client Money Rules were made by the SFC under s. 149, SFO. s. 151 is the basis for the Keeping of Records Rules and s. 152 for the Contract Notes Rules, while s. 157 concerns special reports auditors may be required to make. (PDF p.134)

question: Which of the following amounts of client money IS subject to the Client Money Rules?
options: Money held in a bank account by the client in his own name
Money received and held outside Hong Kong by the licensed corporation while it remains outside Hong Kong
Money previously held in Hong Kong that has been transferred outside Hong Kong in accordance with the Client Money Rules
Money received in Hong Kong by the licensed corporation's associated entity in relation to the corporation's regulated activity
explanation: The Client Money Rules apply to client money received or held in Hong Kong by or on behalf of a licensed corporation in the course of conducting a regulated activity, or by its associated entity in relation to that activity. They do not apply to money held by the client in a bank account in his own name, money received or held outside Hong Kong while it remains there, or money that has been transferred outside Hong Kong in accordance with the Rules. (PDF p.134)

question: A corporation licensed for Type 13 regulated activity receives money of a CIS for which it provides depositary services. Within what period must it deposit the money into an account designated as a trust account or client account for the relevant CIS?
options: Within one business day of receipt
Within two business days of receipt
Within three business days of receipt
Within seven business days of receipt
explanation: A Type 13 licensed corporation must deposit money of the CIS into a designated trust or client account within three business days of receipt, maintained at an AFI or another person approved by the SFC. One business day is the general rule for other licensed corporations paying client money into segregated accounts, so it is a tempting but wrong answer; two and seven business days relate to contract notes and monthly statements respectively. (PDF p.134)

question: Under the Client Money Rules, payments out of the trust or client account of a CIS maintained by a Type 13 licensed corporation may NOT be made to:
options: Officers or employees of the licensed corporation, its associated entities or its controlling entity
Persons specified in a written instruction
Persons entitled under the scheme documents of the CIS
The relevant CIS itself
explanation: The Client Money Rules permit payments out of the account in accordance with the scheme documents of the CIS or a written instruction, but prohibit any payment to officers or employees of the licensed corporation or its associated entities or its controlling entity. The other options describe payments contemplated by the Rules. (PDF p.134)

section: Mode of treatment of client money on receipt
question: Within one business day of receiving client money, a licensed corporation must deal with it in one of several permitted ways. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
options: Paying the money into a segregated account
Paying the money to the client directly
Paying the money in accordance with a standing authority
Retaining the money in the corporation's own house account until the client gives instructions
explanation: Within one business day of receipt, the licensed corporation or associated entity must pay client money into a segregated account, to the client directly, in accordance with a written direction, or in accordance with a standing authority. Holding the money in the firm's own house account pending instructions is not a permitted treatment. (PDF p.135)

question: Section 4(3) of the Client Money Rules lists money received in respect of which regulated activities, in addition to all other amounts received from or on behalf of clients?
options: Types 1, 2, 4 and 9
Types 1, 2, 3 and 8
Types 1, 3, 7 and 13
Types 2, 5, 8 and 9
explanation: Section 4(3), Client Money Rules lists money received in respect of Type 1, Type 2, Type 3 and Type 8 regulated activities, as well as all other amounts received from or on behalf of clients. The other combinations substitute activities such as Types 4, 5, 7, 9 and 13, which are not the ones listed. (PDF p.135)

section: Mode of treatment of client money on receipt
question: Interest received on client money held by a licensed corporation is:
options: Primarily due to the client unless otherwise agreed in writing
Always retained by the licensed corporation as part of its charges
Payable to the SFC
Split equally between the client and the licensed corporation
explanation: Interest received on client money held is primarily due to the client unless otherwise agreed in writing. The licensed corporation may only keep it where there is such a written agreement, so automatic retention is wrong; nothing in the Rules directs interest to the SFC or imposes an equal split. (PDF p.136)

section: Mode of treatment of client money on receipt
question: Mr Chan, a finance officer of a licensed corporation, discovers that money belonging to the firm itself is sitting in a client segregated account. What must the firm do?
options: Leave the money in the account until the next monthly reconciliation
Report the matter to the SFC within one business day but leave the money in the account
Pay the money out of the segregated account within one business day of becoming aware of the fact
Pay the money out of the segregated account within three business days of becoming aware of the fact
explanation: Money held in a segregated account that is not client money must be paid out of the account within one business day of the firm becoming aware of the fact. Waiting for a monthly reconciliation or three business days is too slow, and the one-business-day SFC reporting obligation applies to failures to open, pay into or retain money in segregated accounts, not as a substitute for removing the firm's own money. (PDF p.136)

section: Mode of treatment of client money on receipt
question: A licensed corporation fails, with an intention to defraud, to pay client money into a segregated account in the manner specified by the Client Money Rules. What is the consequence?
options: No offence is committed provided the money is later paid into the account
It commits an offence and the offender is liable to pay penalties and to imprisonment on conviction
It is required only to report the failure to the SFC within one business day
The SFC may approve the failure in writing subject to conditions
explanation: A failure to comply with the requirements to pay money into or out of a segregated account, without reasonable excuse or with an intention to defraud, is an offence and the offender is liable to penalties and imprisonment on conviction. Later payment does not undo the offence, the SFC report obligation is separate and additional, and SFC written approval relates to custodians under the Client Securities Rules, not to such failures. (PDF p.136)

question: Which statement about the Keeping of Records Rules is correct?
options: They were made under s. 151, SFO, and the records required apply to regulated activities only, although they must show all liabilities
They were made under s. 149, SFO and apply to all businesses of an intermediary
They were made under s. 152, SFO and apply only to associated entities
They were made under s. 151, SFO and require records of regulated and unregulated activities alike
explanation: The SFC made the Keeping of Records Rules under s. 151, SFO, for intermediaries and their associated entities; the records required apply to regulated activities only, but because they must be sufficient to produce financial statements they must show all liabilities. s. 149 is the basis for the Client Money Rules and s. 152 for the Contract Notes Rules, and the Rules cover intermediaries as well as associated entities. (PDF p.137)

question: Under the general requirements of the Keeping of Records Rules, an intermediary's records must be sufficient to enable reconciliations with external parties to be made how often?
options: Daily
Weekly
Quarterly
Monthly
explanation: The general requirements provide that the records must enable monthly reconciliations with external parties. Daily, weekly and quarterly are not the frequencies stated in the Keeping of Records Rules. (PDF p.137)

question: In what form may records be kept under the Keeping of Records Rules?
options: In any language, provided a certified translation is available on request
In Chinese or English, in writing or in any other form that may be readily accessed and converted into writing
Only in writing in English
In electronic form only, with daily backups
explanation: Records must be kept in Chinese or English, in writing or in any other form that may be readily accessed and convertible into writing, and all reasonable procedures must be adopted to guard against and enable discovery of falsifications. Other languages with translations are not permitted, English-only is too narrow, and there is no electronic-only or daily backup requirement. (PDF p.138)

question: Mr Chan, a compliance officer, is setting record retention schedules for his licensed corporation. Which of the following retention periods is correct?
options: Telephone recordings must be kept for at least seven years under the Keeping of Records Rules
Copies of contract notes must be retained for at least seven years under the Contract Notes Rules
Records documenting orders and instructions received must be kept for at least two years
All records of every kind need only be kept for six months
explanation: The general rule is at least seven years, but the Keeping of Records Rules require records documenting orders and instructions received to be kept for at least two years. Telephone recordings are excluded from the definition of a record in the Keeping of Records Rules and are covered by the Code of Conduct, which requires a minimum of six months; copies of contract notes must be retained for at least two years, not seven; and six months is not the general retention period. (PDF p.138)

section: Provision of contract notes, statements of account and receipts to clients
question: Which statement about the Contract Notes Rules is correct?
options: They were made under s. 152, SFO and do not apply to an intermediary licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity in the conduct of that activity
They were made under s. 151, SFO and apply to all intermediaries including those licensed for Type 13 regulated activity
They were made under s. 149, SFO and apply only to securities dealers
They were made under s. 152, SFO and apply to Type 13 intermediaries only
explanation: The SFC made the Contract Notes Rules under s. 152, SFO, and they do not apply to an intermediary licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity in the conduct of that activity, or to their associated entities. s. 151 underpins the Keeping of Records Rules and s. 149 the Client Money Rules, and the Rules are not confined to Type 13 intermediaries. (PDF p.139)

section: Provision of contract notes, statements of account and receipts to clients
question: An intermediary wishes to stop providing contract notes, statements of account and receipts to a client who is a PI as specified in Schedule 1, SFO. What does the exemption in the Contract Notes Rules require?
options: The client must sign a written agreement not to receive the documents
The intermediary must have notified the client in writing about the non-provision of such documents and the client must not object
The intermediary must obtain the SFC's written approval for each PI client
Nothing - the exemption applies automatically to all PIs
explanation: For PIs as specified in Schedule 1, SFO, the exemption applies where the intermediary has notified the clients in writing about the non-provision of such documents and the clients do not object. A written agreement not to receive the documents is the requirement for PIs as specified in the Securities and Futures (Professional Investor) Rules, not Schedule 1 PIs; no SFC approval is involved and the exemption is not automatic. (PDF p.139)

question: By when must an intermediary issue a contract note to the client or any designated person?
options: No later than the end of the business day on which the contract was entered into
Within seven business days of the end of the monthly accounting period
No later than the end of the fifth business day after entering into the contract
No later than the end of the second business day after entering into the contract
explanation: A contract note must be issued no later than the end of the second business day after entering into the contract; several contracts on the same day for the same client may be combined in a single contract note or consolidated into the daily statement. Same-day issue is not required, seven business days is the deadline for monthly statements, and five business days is not a deadline in the Rules. (PDF p.139)

question: Where an intermediary is acting as a principal, the contract note must contain an indication that it is so acting, UNLESS the contract is:
options: A securities contract
A futures contract
A leveraged foreign exchange contract
A securities borrowing and lending transaction
explanation: The contract note must indicate that the intermediary is acting as principal unless the contract is a leveraged foreign exchange contract (LFE contract). Securities contracts, futures contracts and SBL transactions enjoy no such carve-out; they are simply categories for which additional contract note details are specified. (PDF p.139)

question: In which of the following situations must a daily statement of account be prepared under the Contract Notes Rules?
options: When an intermediary conducting margined transactions with or on behalf of a client closes a margined transaction
Whenever any intermediary executes a cash securities trade for a client
Only at the end of each monthly accounting period
Only when the client requests one in writing
explanation: Daily statements must be prepared by intermediaries providing securities margin finance (and their associated entities) when specified events such as deposit or withdrawal of client assets take place, and by intermediaries conducting margined transactions both when a margined transaction is entered into and when it is closed; the statement must be issued by the end of the second business day after the specified events. Ordinary cash trades, month-ends and client requests are not the triggers. (PDF p.140)

question: Which of the following items must be included in a daily statement of account relating to securities margin finance?
options: The client's annual income and net worth
The margin ratio and margin value of each description of securities and collateral as at the end of the day
A list of all other clients' open positions held by the intermediary
The intermediary's liquid capital calculation
explanation: Statements relating to securities margin finance must include the opening and closing balances and movements, the quantity, market price and value of each description of client securities and collateral, the margin ratio and margin value of each description as at the end of the day, and details of all movements of client securities and collateral during the day. Client income data and other clients' positions are not required, and liquid capital calculations belong to the supplementary information under the Accounts and Audit Rules. (PDF p.140)

question: Within what period must a monthly statement of account generally be issued, and what allowance applies to asset managers?
options: Within seven business days of the end of each monthly accounting period; asset managers are allowed ten business days
Within two business days; asset managers are allowed seven business days
Within ten business days; asset managers are allowed fourteen business days
Within seven calendar days; asset managers are allowed ten calendar days
explanation: Monthly statements must be issued within seven business days of the end of each monthly accounting period (which can be four-weekly cycles rather than calendar months), and asset managers are allowed ten business days. Two business days is the deadline for contract notes, daily statements and receipts, and the deadlines are expressed in business days, not calendar days. (PDF p.141)

question: In which of the following cases is an intermediary NOT required to issue a monthly statement of account?
options: The client is a margin client who already receives daily statements
The client has not paid the intermediary's fees for the month
There is absolutely no activity whatsoever on the account during the month and no balances at the end of it
The client traded only once during the month
explanation: The only case in which no monthly statement needs to be issued is where there is absolutely no activity whatsoever on the account during the month and no balances at the end of it; separately, an asset manager need not prepare monthly statements for authorised CISs it manages. Receiving daily statements, unpaid fees or low trading activity do not remove the obligation. (PDF p.141)

question: An associated entity of an intermediary receives client securities on Monday (with no intervening public holidays). By when must it issue a receipt to the client?
options: By the end of Monday, the day of receipt
By the end of Tuesday, the first business day after receipt
Within seven business days of receipt
By the end of Wednesday, the second business day after receipt (T+2)
explanation: Receipts must be issued by an intermediary or associated entity on receiving client assets or security by the end of the second business day after receiving them (T+2), which here is Wednesday. Same-day or next-day issue is not required, and seven business days is the monthly statement deadline; note also that no receipt is required in some cases where the client has received satisfactory confirmation in other ways. (PDF p.141)

question: Under the Contract Notes Rules, for how long must an intermediary or associated entity retain the documents it has issued?
options: All documents for at least two years
Contract notes, daily statements of account and receipts for at least two years; monthly statements of account for at least seven years
All documents for at least seven years
Monthly statements of account for at least two years; all other documents for at least seven years
explanation: Contract notes, daily statements of account and receipts issued must be retained for at least two years, while monthly statements of account must be retained for at least seven years. Treating all documents alike, or reversing the two periods, is wrong. (PDF p.142)

question: Under the Accounts and Audit Rules, which supplementary information must an active licensed corporation prepare as at the end of the financial year?
options: Liquid capital calculations, a business and risk management questionnaire and various analyses of borrowings and client accounts
A list of all telephone recordings made during the year
Copies of all contract notes issued during the year
A register of all employees' personal securities holdings
explanation: Active licensed corporations must prepare supplementary information as at the end of the financial year including liquid capital calculations, a business and risk management questionnaire and various analyses of borrowings and client accounts. Telephone recordings are dealt with under the Code of Conduct, contract notes under the Contract Notes Rules, and an employee holdings register is not among the specified supplementary information. (PDF p.143)

question: The auditor of a licensed corporation is unable to confirm compliance with the provisions of the Client Securities Rules and the Client Money Rules. What is he required to do?
options: Report only to the shareholders, since his duties run primarily to them
Resign as auditor immediately
Report to the SFC, in addition to any special reports he may be required to make under s. 157, SFO
Withhold the audit report until compliance is achieved
explanation: If the auditor is unable to confirm compliance with the Client Securities Rules and the Client Money Rules, or has noted contraventions of the FRR, he is required to report to the SFC, in addition to any special reports under s. 157, SFO. The SFC has extended the normal duties of a company auditor (which are primarily to shareholders) to protect investors and the market, so reporting to shareholders alone is insufficient; resignation and withholding the report are not what the Rules require. (PDF p.144)

question: Under the Schedule 1, SFO definition of "securities", which structured products are included?
options: All structured products without exception
Only structured products listed on the SEHK
Structured products that do not fall within the other limbs of the definition but are the subject of an offer to the public which is authorised, or required to be authorised, under s. 105(1), SFO
No structured products, as they are dealt with under a separate ordinance
explanation: The definition catches structured products not falling within limbs (a) to (f) only where they are the subject of a public offer authorised or required to be authorised under s. 105(1), SFO. The source explicitly notes that NOT all structured products are regarded as securities, so the first option is wrong. Listing on the SEHK is not the test, and structured products are not wholly excluded. (PDF p.145)

question: For the purposes of market misconduct, "derivatives" are defined in which provisions of the SFO?
options: s. 245, Part XIII and s. 285, Part XIV, SFO
Schedule 5, SFO
s. 392, SFO
explanation: Derivatives are defined for market misconduct purposes in s. 245, Part XIII and s. 285, Part XIV, SFO, in relation to listed securities. Schedule 5 defines the regulated activities (e.g. dealing in securities), not derivatives. S. 392 is the Financial Secretary's prescription power, and s. 105(1) concerns authorisation of public offers. (PDF p.145)

question: Why does the Study Guide describe the treatment of warrants and options on listed securities as one of the anomalies of the Hong Kong market?
options: Although they are derivatives, they are traded on the SEHK, customarily called the "Cash Market", rather than on the HKFE, the "Derivatives Market"
They are securities but are traded on the HKFE rather than the SEHK
They are not regulated under the SFO at all
Options on futures contracts are traded on the SEHK instead of the HKFE
explanation: Warrants and options on listed securities are normally considered derivatives, yet they trade on the SEHK (the "Cash Market"), while options on futures contracts trade on the HKFE (the "Derivatives Market") — contradicting those customary labels. The second and fourth options reverse the exchanges, and these instruments are regulated under the SFO, so the third option is wrong. (PDF p.146)

question: Under Schedule 5, SFO, a person deals in securities if he makes or offers to make an agreement, or induces or attempts to induce another person to enter into an agreement:
options: only for acquiring or disposing of securities listed on the SEHK
for acquiring, disposing of, subscribing for or underwriting securities, or for the purpose of making profits from securities
for providing financial accommodation to facilitate the acquisition of securities
only for underwriting new issues of securities
explanation: The definition covers agreements for acquiring, disposing of, subscribing for or underwriting securities, or for the purpose of making profits from securities. It is not limited to SEHK-listed securities or to underwriting alone. Providing financial accommodation to facilitate acquisition of securities is securities margin financing (Type 8), not dealing. (PDF p.146)

question: Which of the following is NOT excepted from the definition of dealing in securities?
options: A recognised stock exchange
A financial journalist issuing investment analyses to the public on subscription
A trust company registered under the Trustee Ordinance acting for a CIS
An open-ended fund company that issues an advertisement in respect of its shares
explanation: Financial journalists appear among the exemptions from the Type 4/Type 5 advising licence requirement, not among the exceptions to the dealing in securities definition. Recognised stock exchanges, trust companies registered under the Trustee Ordinance acting for a CIS, and open-ended fund companies issuing advertisements or invitations in respect of their shares are all expressly excepted from the dealing definition. (PDF p.146)

question: Mr Chan deals in securities only through a licensed securities dealer and so is normally excepted from the definition of dealing in securities. However, in return for remuneration, he makes introductions between a securities dealer and a third person. How is Mr Chan treated under the SFO?
options: He remains excepted because he deals through a licensed dealer
He will be regarded as dealing in securities
He is excepted provided the third person is a professional investor
He will be regarded as advising on securities, not dealing
explanation: The exception for persons dealing through a licensed dealer or registered institution is lost where, in return for remuneration, the person makes introductions between a securities dealer and a third person — he is then regarded as dealing in securities. The exception is therefore not preserved, the professional investor status of the third person is irrelevant to this Note, and the conduct is treated as dealing, not advising. (PDF p.147)

question: A large financial group wishes to structure itself with a separate company that lends money for securities margin financing direct to investors. Which licence would that company hold?
options: Type 1
Type 2
Type 8
Type 9
explanation: An SMF company lending money for securities margin financing direct to investors holds a Type 8 licence, although the source notes the use of this vehicle is diminishing after the SFC tightened the requirements on SMF. Type 1 is for the securities dealing company, Type 2 for the HKFE participant dealing in futures, and Type 9 for the asset management company. (PDF p.148)

question: In view of the limited risk it bears, what capital treatment is given to an approved introducing agent?
options: It must maintain a higher level of liquid capital but no paid-up share capital
It is allowed to maintain a lower level of liquid capital and is not required to maintain paid-up share capital
It is exempted from all financial resources requirements
It must maintain normal liquid capital but a lower level of paid-up share capital
explanation: Because an approved introducing agent does not handle client assets and incurs no legal liability for introduced business except for its own negligence, wilful default or fraud, it is allowed a lower level of liquid capital and is not required to maintain paid-up share capital. It is not exempted from financial resources requirements altogether, and its liquid capital requirement is lower, not higher or normal. (PDF p.148)

section: Dealing in futures contracts (Type 2 regulated activity)
question: According to Schedule 1, SFO, a futures contract means:
options: any contract for the delivery of property at a future time
only contracts traded on the HKFE

a contract or an option on a contract made under the rules or conventions of a futures market, and interests, rights or property prescribed by the Financial Secretary under s. 392, SFO as futures contracts
any contract guaranteed by a central counterparty
explanation: The Schedule 1, SFO definition covers contracts or options on contracts made under the rules or conventions of a futures market, plus interests, rights or property prescribed by the Financial Secretary under s. 392, SFO (and excludes items the Financial Secretary prescribes as not being futures contracts). The definition is not limited to the HKFE, does not catch every future-delivery contract, and CCP guarantee is a feature of the futures market definition, not the test for a futures contract. (PDF p.149)

section: Dealing in futures contracts (Type 2 regulated activity)
question: The term "futures market" as defined in Schedule 1, SFO specifically excludes:
options: the office of a recognised clearing house
any market where contracts are novated by a central counterparty
any market where contractual obligations are normally discharged before the expiry date
places providing facilities for negotiating adjustment contracts referenced to an index
explanation: The definition expressly states that "futures market" excludes the office of a recognised clearing house. Novation or guarantee by a central counterparty, normal discharge of obligations before expiry, and facilities for index-referenced adjustment contracts are all features that bring a place WITHIN the futures market definition, not outside it. (PDF p.149)

section: Dealing in futures contracts (Type 2 regulated activity)
question: Which of the following persons is excepted from the definition of dealing in futures contracts?
options: A member of a commodity exchange in operation on 20 June 1973 who only performs such acts on that exchange
A member of any commodity exchange established before 1980
A person acting as principal who deals with professional investors in futures contracts traded on a recognised futures market
A Type 9 licensee engaging in such activities for any purpose
explanation: Members of a commodity exchange in operation on 20 June 1973 who only act on that exchange are excepted. The 1980 date is invented. The principal/professional investor exception applies only to futures contracts traded OTHERWISE THAN on a recognised futures market, and Type 9 licensees are excepted only where they engage in the activities solely for the purposes of the Type 9 regulated activity. (PDF p.150)

section: Dealing in futures contracts (Type 2 regulated activity)
question: Ms Wong normally engages in futures dealing only through a person licensed for Type 2 regulated activity, and so is excepted from the dealing definition. In return for remuneration, she accepts for a futures dealer an offer by a third person to acquire futures contracts. What is her position?
options: She remains excepted because the order is executed by the Type 2 licensee
She is excepted provided she does not handle client money
She will be regarded as dealing in futures contracts
She only needs a Type 5 (advising on futures contracts) licence
explanation: The exception for persons acting through a Type 2 licensee is lost where, in return for remuneration, the person accepts for a futures dealer an offer by a third person to acquire or dispose of futures contracts — she is then regarded as dealing in futures contracts. Execution by the licensee does not preserve the exception, handling of client money is not the test in this Note, and the conduct is dealing, not advising. (PDF p.150)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: Foreign exchange trading relates to a contract or arrangement where one person undertakes to do which of the following with another person?
options: Exchange currency, deliver foreign currency, or credit the account of another person with foreign currency
Exchange currency, lend foreign currency at interest, or guarantee foreign currency loans
Only deliver foreign currency on a recognised exchange
Only credit accounts with Hong Kong dollars
explanation: The three undertakings are: exchanging currency with another person, delivering foreign currency to another person, or crediting the account of another person with foreign currency. Lending at interest and guaranteeing loans are not part of this definition, the activity is not confined to a recognised exchange, and the definition concerns foreign currency, not crediting Hong Kong dollars. (PDF p.151)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: What is the relationship between leveraged foreign exchange trading (LFET) and foreign exchange trading?
options: Foreign exchange trading is a subset of LFET
LFET is a subset of foreign exchange trading, as it relates only to trading in LFE contracts
They are identical activities under the SFO
LFET covers only currency futures executed on a recognised exchange
explanation: LFET is a subset of foreign exchange trading because it relates only to trading in LFE contracts. The first option reverses the relationship and the two activities are not identical. The fourth option is doubly wrong: an LFE contract is not executed on an exchange at all. (PDF p.151)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: Which of the following statements about an LFE contract is correct?
options: It is not executed on an exchange
It must be executed on a recognised futures market
Settlement must always take place on the value date
It must be novated or guaranteed by a central counterparty
explanation: The source states plainly that the LFE contract is not executed on an exchange, so the second option is wrong. Settlement on value date may be deferred indefinitely until the client agrees to take physical delivery or enters into a netting contract, so the third option is wrong. Novation or CCP guarantee belongs to the definition of a futures market, not LFE contracts. (PDF p.152)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: Which of the following is NOT an example of a transaction exempted from the definition of LFET under Schedule 5, SFO?
options: A transaction by an AFI or a central bank
An exchange transaction under the Money Changers Ordinance (Cap 34)
A transaction in LFE contracts carried out by a corporation as its main business
A transaction executed on a stock exchange by or through a person licensed for Type 1 regulated activity
explanation: Corporations that wish to undertake LFET as a business must apply for and obtain Type 3 licences from the SFC — such trading is the regulated activity itself, not an exemption. Transactions by an AFI or central bank, exchange transactions under the Money Changers Ordinance (Cap 34), and transactions executed on a stock exchange or futures exchange by or through Type 1 or Type 2 licensees (or wholly incidental thereto) are all given as examples of exemptions. (PDF p.152)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: Which statement about approved introducing agents in the LFET regime is TRUE?
options: They are exempted from holding any SFC licence
They must hold Type 3 licences, even though they merely pass on clients' orders to other traders
They may hold and control client assets provided they do not handle discretionary accounts
They are subject to the same capital requirements as full LFE traders
explanation: Although they act merely as introducers passing on clients' orders, LFET approved introducing agents must nevertheless hold Type 3 licences, so they are not exempt. They do not hold or control client assets and do not handle discretionary accounts. Because of this limited role, they are allowed lower capital requirements than full LFE traders, not the same. (PDF p.152)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: What special treatment is given to transactions carried out by licensed LFE traders with recognised counterparties?
options: The transactions are exempt from all SFO provisions
The transactions must be reported to the HKMA
The recognised counterparty will not be considered a client of the LFE trader
The transactions must be settled by arbitration
explanation: Recognised counterparties are generally substantial institutions, and the special treatment cited is that they will not be considered clients of the LFE traders. The source does not say the transactions escape the SFO entirely or must be reported to the HKMA. Arbitration is a separate licensing condition for resolving disputes raised by clients, not a settlement method for counterparty transactions. (PDF p.153)

section: Leveraged foreign exchange trading (Type 3 regulated activity)
question: A client of Mr Lam, a licensed LFE trader, requires a dispute between them about a matter concerning the carrying on of LFET to be settled by arbitration. What must Mr Lam do?
options: He is obliged to have the dispute settled by arbitration, as this is a condition of granting the licence under s. 118(1)(b), SFO
He may insist on having the dispute resolved in court instead
He need only arbitrate if both parties agree to it in writing
He must refer the dispute to the HKMA for adjudication
explanation: S. 118(1)(b), SFO makes it a condition of granting an LFET licence that, if a client requires such a dispute to be settled by arbitration, the licensed trader is obliged to have it so settled. The trader therefore cannot insist on court proceedings, mutual written agreement is not required once the client elects arbitration, and the HKMA has no role here — the SFC made the relevant arbitration rules. (PDF p.153)

section: Advising on securities or futures contracts (Type 4 and Type 5 regulated activities)
question: Which two activities are defined in Schedule 5, SFO as constituting advising on securities (Type 4) and advising on futures contracts (Type 5)?
options: The giving of advice, and the issuing of analyses or reports for the purposes of facilitating recipients to make decisions on the acquisition or disposal of securities or entering into futures contracts
The giving of advice, and the management of discretionary portfolios
The issuing of prospectuses, and the issuing of research reports
The giving of advice, and dealing in the relevant securities or futures contracts
explanation: The two defined activities are the giving of advice and the issuing of analyses or reports to facilitate recipients' decisions on acquiring or disposing of securities or entering into futures contracts. Managing portfolios is the Type 9 activity, issuing prospectuses is an exception to the dealing definition, and dealing itself is Type 1 or Type 2, not advising. (PDF p.153)

section: Advising on securities or futures contracts (Type 4 and Type 5 regulated activities)
question: Advice constituting Type 4 or Type 5 regulated activity includes advice for the purposes of making a decision on each of the following, EXCEPT:
options: whether securities or futures contracts should be acquired or disposed of
which securities or futures contracts should be acquired or disposed of
the tax consequences of continuing to hold securities
the time at which, or the terms or conditions on which, securities or futures contracts should be acquired or disposed of
explanation: The definition lists four matters: whether, which, the time at which, and the terms or conditions on which securities or futures contracts are or should be acquired, disposed of or entered into. Tax consequences of holding are not in this list. The other three options reproduce the statutory limbs exactly. (PDF p.154)

section: Advising on securities or futures contracts (Type 4 and Type 5 regulated activities)
question: Ms Lee, a certified public accountant, gives advice on securities to her audit clients wholly incidental to her accountancy practice. What is her licensing position?
options: She must obtain a Type 4 licence before giving any such advice
She is generally exempted from requiring a licence to advise on securities
She must obtain a Type 6 licence because the advice concerns corporate clients
She is exempted only if she registers with the HKMA
explanation: Solicitors, counsel, certified public accountants, trust companies and persons licensed to carry out asset management who give such advice wholly incidental to their professions are generally exempted from requiring an advising licence. No Type 4 licence is therefore needed, Type 6 concerns corporate finance advice, and HKMA registration applies to authorised financial institutions, not accountants. (PDF p.154)

section: Advising on corporate finance (Type 6 regulated activity)
question: Advising on corporate finance includes giving advice concerning compliance with rules made under s. 23, SFO. By whom are the s. 23 rules made?
options: The SFC
A recognised exchange company, effectively the SEHK
The HKMA
The Financial Secretary
explanation: Rules under s. 23, SFO are made by a recognised exchange company, effectively the SEHK, while rules under s. 36, SFO are made by the SFC — both in respect of the listing of securities. The HKMA is not a rule-maker here, and the Financial Secretary's prescription power is s. 392, SFO. (PDF p.154)

section: Advising on corporate finance (Type 6 regulated activity)
question: Which statement correctly describes the administration of the Listing Rules and the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs?
options: The Listing Rules are administered by the SEHK under the overall supervision of the SFC, and the Codes are administered directly by the SFC
Both the Listing Rules and the Codes are administered directly by the SFC
The Listing Rules are administered by the SFC and the Codes by the SEHK
Both are administered by the SEHK under the supervision of the HKMA
explanation: The Listing Rules are administered by the SEHK under the overall supervision of the SFC, whereas the Codes are administered directly by the SFC. The other options either assign both instruments to one body or swap the administrators, and the HKMA has no supervisory role over the Listing Rules. (PDF p.155)

section: Advising on corporate finance (Type 6 regulated activity)
question: Which of the following falls within the Schedule 5, SFO definition of advising on corporate finance?
options: Advising a retail client on the time at which to buy listed shares
Providing financial accommodation to facilitate the acquisition of listed securities
Issuing research analyses to the public to facilitate decisions to acquire securities
Giving advice to a listed corporation regarding corporate restructuring in respect of securities
explanation: Advice to a listed corporation or public company (or its subsidiaries, officers or shareholders) regarding corporate restructuring in respect of securities is one of the four limbs of advising on corporate finance. Advising a retail client on timing and issuing research analyses are Type 4 advising on securities, and providing financial accommodation to acquire listed securities is Type 8 securities margin financing. (PDF p.154)

section: Providing automated trading services (Type 7 regulated activity)
question: Which of the following is given as an example of automated trading services (ATS) in Hong Kong?
options: The trading operations of a recognised exchange company
Trade confirmation and matching systems provided by brokers
The operations of a recognised clearing house
The trading system operated by the HKFE
explanation: ATS describes any automated system providing, by electronic facilities, a trading mechanism for securities and futures contracts OTHER THAN the operations of a recognised exchange company or a recognised clearing house (currently only the SEHK, the HKFE and their related clearing houses). Examples given include brokers' trade confirmation and matching systems and full trading and settlement systems for non-local securities. The other three options are all expressly carved out of the ATS description. (PDF p.155)

section: Providing automated trading services (Type 7 regulated activity)
question: Under which provision of the SFO may a person be authorised to provide ATS similar to the services provided by a recognised exchange or recognised clearing house?
options: s. 397, SFO
s. 23, SFO
explanation: The SFO covers ATS in two places: authorisation under s. 95(2) for services similar to those of a recognised exchange or clearing house, and licensing (or, for an AFI, registration) as a regulated activity under Part V, SFO. S. 118(1)(b) is the LFET arbitration licensing condition, s. 397 is the SFC's power to recognise counterparties, and s. 23 concerns rules made by a recognised exchange company. (PDF p.155)

question: Which statement about the Schedule 5, SFO definition of securities margin financing (SMF) is correct?
options: It covers only securities listed on a stock market in Hong Kong
It applies only where the securities are pledged as security for the accommodation
It covers the acquisition (and, where applicable, continued holding) of securities listed on any stock market, whether in Hong Kong or elsewhere, whether or not the securities are pledged as security
It covers financial accommodation provided for underwriting securities
explanation: SMF is the provision of financial accommodation to facilitate the acquisition of securities listed on any stock market, whether in Hong Kong or elsewhere, and where applicable their continued holding, whether or not the securities are pledged as security. It is therefore not limited to Hong Kong listings or to pledged securities. Accommodation for underwriting, sub-underwriting and acquisition under a prospectus is expressly excepted from the definition. (PDF p.156)

question: Mr Cheung, an individual, provides financial accommodation to a company in which he holds 10% of its issued shares, to facilitate the company's securities margin financing. Does he need a Type 8 licence?
options: Yes, because all providers of financial accommodation for securities acquisitions need a Type 8 licence
No, because accommodation by an individual to a company in which he holds 10% or more of its issued shares, to facilitate SMF, is excepted from the definition
Yes, unless he holds more than 50% of the company's issued shares
No, because individuals can never carry on SMF
explanation: The provision of accommodation by an individual to a company in which he holds 10% or more of its issued shares, to facilitate SMF, is excepted from the SMF definition, so no Type 8 licence is needed. The threshold is 10%, not over 50%, so the third option is wrong. The first option ignores the list of exceptions, and the fourth states a blanket rule not found in the source. (PDF p.156)

question: A securities margin financier allows a client to draw against his margin account. Which of the following advances would generally fall OUTSIDE the permitted scope of the financier's business?
options: An advance to facilitate the continued holding of securities acquired under the margin account
An advance in excess of the realisable value of the securities collateral
An advance to facilitate the acquisition of listed securities
An advance secured against securities collateral held in the margin account
explanation: The securities margin financier is subject to the sole business requirement and can only provide financial accommodation for facilitating the acquisition and continued holding of listed securities; any advance in excess of the realisable value of the securities collateral would generally fall outside this scope. Options A and C describe precisely what the sole business requirement permits, and option D describes ordinary secured margin lending within the account, so none of them falls outside the permitted scope. (PDF p.157)

question: Before starting to trade on margin for a client, a licensed corporation must enter into a written margin client agreement. Which of the following is among the terms the licensee should ensure the client understands?
options: The notice, if any, the securities margin financier will give before selling client securities collaterals to cover a margin deficiency
The identity of the bank with which the financier re-pledges collateral
The financier's liquid capital position
The names of the financier's other margin clients in the pooled account
explanation: The listed terms a client should understand include what notice, if any, the securities margin financier will give to the client before selling client securities collaterals to cover a margin deficiency, along with interest calculation, repayment, collateral usage, loan limits and lending ratios, and margin call procedures. The identity of any re-pledgee bank, the financier's capital position and the identities of other pooled clients are not among the items listed in the margin agreement requirements. (PDF p.158)

question: What is the limit on re-pledging client securities collateral that applies to securities dealers and securities margin financiers licensed for Type 8 regulated activity?
explanation: The additional requirements for firms that re-pledge clients' securities collateral include a limit of 140% on re-pledging client securities collateral, together with certain changes to relevant haircut percentages and additional disclosures to margin clients about the risks of pooling and pledging. The other percentages are not figures stated in the text and are therefore wrong. (PDF p.158)

question: Under Schedule 5, SFO, "asset management" means:
options: Real estate investment scheme management or securities or futures contracts management
Securities dealing or futures contracts dealing
Providing depositary services or credit rating services
Managing Mandatory Provident Fund schemes or occupational retirement schemes
explanation: Schedule 5, SFO defines asset management as (a) real estate investment scheme management or (b) securities or futures contracts management. Dealing in securities or futures is a different set of regulated activities (Types 1 and 2), depositary and credit rating services are Types 13 and 10 respectively, and interests in MPF and ORSO schemes are specifically excluded from the "securities" being managed. (PDF p.158)

question: For a scheme to fall within "real estate investment scheme management", which TWO conditions must be satisfied?
options: The property managed consists primarily of immovable property, and the scheme is authorised under s. 104, SFO
The property managed consists primarily of immovable property, and the scheme is registered under the MPFSO
The scheme is authorised under s. 104, SFO, and is constituted as a trust
The scheme invests only in Hong Kong real estate, and is listed on a recognised exchange
explanation: Real estate investment scheme management means operating a CIS for another person where the property being managed under the scheme consists primarily of immovable property and the scheme is authorised under s. 104, SFO. MPFSO registration relates to retirement schemes excluded from "securities", the text imposes no trust-form requirement, and there is no condition about Hong Kong-only property or exchange listing. (PDF p.158)

question: The definition of asset management speaks of managing "securities", which includes interests in any CIS. Which of the following CIS interests is NOT excluded from that definition?
options: A registered Mandatory Provident Fund scheme under the MPFSO or its constituent fund
An occupational retirement scheme under the ORSO
A contract of insurance in relation to any class of insurance business under the Insurance Ordinance
A unit trust authorised by the SFC
explanation: The exclusions from "securities" are interests in a CIS that is a registered MPF scheme (or its constituent fund), an ORSO occupational retirement scheme, or a contract of insurance under the Insurance Ordinance. An SFC-authorised unit trust is not among the exclusions — indeed unit trusts are given as an example of CISs the SFC authorises — so managing it remains asset management. (PDF p.159)

question: The functions of the SFC in relation to asset management are two-pronged. From which parts of the SFO are these two functions respectively derived?
options: Authorising CISs from Part IV, and licensing and supervising intermediaries from Part V
Authorising CISs from Part V, and licensing and supervising intermediaries from Part IV
Both functions from Part IIIA
Authorising CISs from Part IIIA, and licensing intermediaries from Part V
explanation: The SFC (a) authorises CISs and supervises their marketing, a function derived from Part IV, SFO, and (b) licenses and supervises intermediaries engaged in asset management and related advisory services, derived from Part V, SFO. Option B reverses the two parts, and Part IIIA is the part dealing with OTC derivative reporting and clearing obligations, not asset management. (PDF p.159)

section: Providing credit rating services (Type 10 regulated activity)
question: Mr Chan's firm publishes opinions, expressed using a defined ranking system, primarily regarding the creditworthiness of individual consumers. Do these opinions constitute "credit ratings" under Schedule 5, SFO?
options: Yes, because creditworthiness opinions on any person are credit ratings
No, because credit ratings concern a person OTHER THAN an individual, debt securities, preferred securities, or an agreement to provide credit
Yes, but only if the opinions are distributed by subscription
No, because credit ratings can only relate to debt securities
explanation: "Credit ratings" are defined as opinions, expressed using a defined ranking system, primarily regarding the creditworthiness of a person other than an individual, debt securities, preferred securities, or an agreement to provide credit — so opinions on individuals fall outside the definition. Option A wrongly includes individuals, option C confuses the separate definition of "providing credit rating services", and option D is too narrow because the definition also covers non-individual persons, preferred securities and credit agreements. (PDF p.159)

section: Providing credit rating services (Type 10 regulated activity)
question: Which of the following is given in the definition of "preferred securities" for credit rating purposes?
options: Preference shares, preferred shares or preferred stock
Debenture stocks, loan stocks and bonds
Indexed bonds and bonds with warrants
Convertible debt securities and notes
explanation: "Preferred securities" is defined as preference shares, preferred shares or preferred stock. The other three options all list instruments that fall within the separate definition of "debt securities" (debenture stocks, loan stocks, debentures, bonds, notes, indexed bonds, convertible debt securities, bonds with warrants, non-interest bearing debt securities and other instruments evidencing indebtedness), not preferred securities. (PDF p.160)

section: Providing credit rating services (Type 10 regulated activity)
question: "Providing credit rating services" is defined as preparing credit ratings for:
options: Internal use by a firm in assessing counterparty risk
Exclusive provision to a single requesting client
Dissemination to the public, or distribution by subscription, whether in Hong Kong or elsewhere, or with a reasonable expectation of such
Submission to the SFC for regulatory approval
explanation: Providing credit rating services means preparing credit ratings for dissemination to the public or distribution by subscription, whether in Hong Kong or elsewhere, or with a reasonable expectation that they will be so disseminated or distributed. Internal-use ratings (option A) and ratings exclusively prepared for a requesting person (option B) are specifically excluded activities, and there is no requirement to submit ratings to the SFC for approval. (PDF p.160)

section: Providing credit rating services - Excluded activities
question: A bank prepares credit ratings exclusively for its internal use when assessing counterparty risk, and conducts no other credit rating activities. What is its licensing position for Type 10 regulated activity?
options: It must be licensed because it prepares credit ratings
It is exempt but must register with the SFC
It does not need to be licensed or registered as a result only of that activity
It must be licensed unless the ratings cover fewer than ten counterparties
explanation: A firm preparing credit ratings for its internal use, such as a bank assessing counterparty risk, is given as an example of an activity that would not incur a requirement to be licensed or registered; internal ratings are not prepared for public dissemination or subscription distribution. The text stresses this is not an exemption (so option B's registration idea is wrong) — if the firm also conducts activities within the definition, a licensing requirement would arise for those — and no counterparty-number test exists. (PDF p.160)

section: Providing depositary services for relevant CISs (Type 13 regulated activity)
question: "Providing depositary services for relevant CISs" covers the provision by a depositary of either or both of which services?
options: Custody and safekeeping of relevant CIS property, and oversight of the relevant CIS to ensure it is operated in accordance with its scheme documents
Portfolio management of CIS assets, and marketing of the CIS to the public
Custody of CIS property, and valuation of the CIS on a daily basis
Oversight of the CIS, and clearing of its OTC derivative transactions
explanation: Type 13 is defined as the provision by a depositary of either or both of (a) the custody and safekeeping of relevant CIS property and (b) the oversight of the relevant CIS to ensure it is operated in accordance with its scheme documents. Portfolio management is Type 9 asset management, and neither marketing, daily valuation nor OTC clearing forms part of the Type 13 definition. (PDF p.161)

section: Providing depositary services for relevant CISs (Type 13 regulated activity)
question: A relevant CIS is constituted in corporate form rather than as a trust. Who acts as its "depositary"?
options: A trustee appointed under a trust deed
A custodian
The SFC
An approved money broker
explanation: "Depositary" means the trustee or custodian of a relevant CIS: schemes constituted as a trust have a trustee appointed under the trust deed, while those otherwise constituted, such as in corporate form, have a custodian. A trustee applies only to trust-form schemes, the SFC is the regulator that authorises the CIS rather than its depositary, and approved money brokers appear in the OTC clearing context as prescribed persons. (PDF p.161)

section: Activities to be added or expanded
question: Which regulated activities are yet to be brought fully into force?
options: Type 11 (dealing in or advising on OTC derivative products) and Type 12 (providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions)
Type 9 (asset management) and Type 10 (providing credit rating services)
Type 12 (providing client clearing services) and Type 13 (providing depositary services)
Type 7 (automated trading services) and Type 8 (securities margin financing)
explanation: Type 11 (dealing in OTC derivative products or advising on OTC derivative products) and Type 12 (providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions) are the two regulated activities yet to be brought fully into force; in addition, Types 7 and 9 will be expanded to cover OTC derivatives, but those amendments are also not yet in force. Types 8, 9, 10 and 13 are existing activities discussed as currently operative, so the other options are wrong. (PDF p.161)

section: Reporting and record keeping obligations for OTC derivative transactions
question: When did the Phase 1 mandatory reporting and record keeping regime for OTC derivative transactions take effect in Hong Kong, and when did Phase 2 take effect?
options: Phase 1 in July 2015; Phase 2 in July 2017
Phase 1 in September 2016; Phase 2 in July 2017
Phase 1 in July 2008; Phase 2 in July 2015
Phase 1 in July 2015; Phase 2 in September 2016
explanation: Phase 1 mandatory reporting and record keeping was introduced in Hong Kong in July 2015, and the Phase 2 regulation, which expands the scope of products and transaction information to be reported, took effect in July 2017. 1 September 2016 is the date the first phase of mandatory CLEARING was introduced, and 2008 is the year of the global financial crisis that prompted the reforms, not a regime start date. (PDF p.162)

section: Reporting and record keeping obligations for OTC derivative transactions - Scope of application
question: Under the OTCD Reporting Rules, which body specifies the OTC derivative transactions that are subject to the transaction information reporting requirements?
options: The SFC
The HKMA
The Court of First Instance
The Hong Kong Trade Repository
explanation: Reporting and record keeping obligations arise in respect of OTC derivative transactions that the HKMA has specified as being subject to the transaction information reporting requirements ("specified OTC derivative transactions").
