The SFC makes the detailed rules under Part IIIA of the SFO but is not the body that specifies the transactions; the Court of First Instance is where breaches are brought; and the HKTR is the electronic reporting system, not a specifying body. (PDF p.162)

question: A licensed corporation may become subject to the OTC derivative reporting obligation in which of the following ways?
options: Only by entering into a specified OTC derivative transaction as a counterparty
By entering into a specified transaction as counterparty, conducting a specified transaction in Hong Kong on behalf of an affiliate, or losing its exempt status
Only by losing its exempt status
By acting as trustee for a CIS in the course of Type 13 regulated activity
explanation: A licensed corporation may be subject to the reporting obligation as a result of (a) entering into a specified OTC derivative transaction as a counterparty, (b) conducting such a transaction "in Hong Kong" on behalf of an affiliate, or (c) losing its exempt status. Options A and C each capture only one of the three triggers, and a licensed corporation that is a counterparty in the course of Type 13 activity as trustee for a CIS is specifically NOT subject to the reporting obligation. (PDF p.163)

question: In general, within what period must reports of OTC derivative transaction events be made to the HKMA?
options: Within one business day (T+1)
Within two business days (T+2)
Within five business days (T+5)
Within three months
explanation: In general, reports must be made on a T+2 basis, i.e. within two business days after any transaction events occur. One business day is the deadline for CLEARING a transaction through a designated CCP under the OTCD Clearing Rules, and three months is the grace period available to a licensed corporation that ceases to be exempt, not the general reporting deadline; T+5 appears nowhere in the text. (PDF p.163)

question: A licensed corporation ceases to be exempt from the OTC derivative reporting obligation. What grace period does it have to submit the required information?
options: No grace period; it must report on a T+2 basis immediately
One month from the day it ceased to be exempt
No later than three months from the day it ceased to be exempt, including transaction events occurring since the grace period commenced
Twelve months from the day it ceased to be exempt
explanation: A licensed corporation that ceases to be exempt benefits from a grace period enabling it to submit information by no later than three months from the day it ceased to be exempt, and that information must include the transaction events that have occurred since the commencement of the grace period. T+2 is the general rule for firms already subject to the obligation, one month is not a stated period, and twelve months is the period a person's position must stay below the CLEARING threshold before an exit notice can be given. (PDF p.163)

question: How often must information concerning the valuation of an outstanding specified OTC derivative transaction be submitted?
options: Daily
Weekly
Monthly
Only when a subsequent event occurs
explanation: Reporting obligations continue while the transaction remains outstanding, and information concerning the valuation of the transaction must be submitted on a daily basis. Weekly and monthly are not frequencies stated in the text, and subsequent events (changes to the terms, conditions or parties of the transaction) trigger separate event reporting in addition to, not instead of, the daily valuation submissions. (PDF p.163)

section: Relief if an affiliate has reported
question: Mr Chan's licensed corporation conducted a specified OTC derivative transaction in Hong Kong on behalf of an affiliate, and the affiliate has made the report instead. What must the licensed corporation obtain to demonstrate compliance with its reporting obligation?
options: Nothing; the affiliate's report automatically discharges the obligation
A written confirmation from the affiliate that the report has been made
An oral assurance from the affiliate's compliance officer
A waiver from the SFC
explanation: Where the transaction was conducted on behalf of an affiliate, the affiliate may make the report instead, but the licensed corporation must obtain from the affiliate a written confirmation that the report has been made in order to demonstrate compliance, because the primary obligation rests with the licensed corporation. Nothing automatic discharges the obligation, an oral assurance does not satisfy the written confirmation requirement, and no SFC waiver mechanism is mentioned. (PDF p.164)

question: Mr Chan's licensed corporation has outstanding specified OTC derivative transactions with aggregate notional amounts of US$25 million in interest rate products and US$10 million in another product class. Is it exempt from the reporting obligation?
options: Yes, because no single product class exceeds US$30 million
No, because its aggregate position across all product classes (US$35 million) exceeds the US$30 million threshold
Yes, because the threshold is US$20 billion
No, because there is no exemption from the reporting obligation
explanation: The exemption applies only if the sum of the notional amounts of ALL outstanding specified OTC derivative transactions to which the licensed corporation is a counterparty does not exceed US$30 million, and firms must consider their aggregate position under all product classes collectively — here US$35 million exceeds the threshold. Option A wrongly applies the test per product class, US$20 billion is the clearing threshold rather than the reporting exemption threshold, and an exemption does exist, so option D is wrong. (PDF p.164)

question: A licensed corporation's outstanding specified OTC derivative positions exceed the US$30 million reporting exemption threshold for one day, then fall back below it. What is its exemption status?
options: It regains the exemption once its position falls back below the threshold
It ceases to be exempt on the day it exceeds the threshold and the exemption is lost permanently
It remains exempt until the SFC issues a notice
It loses the exemption only if it stays above the threshold for 12 consecutive months
explanation: Once the licensed corporation's position exceeds the US$30 million threshold, it ceases to be exempt on the day it exceeds the threshold and the exemption is lost permanently. There is no regaining of the reporting exemption and no SFC notice mechanism; the 12-consecutive-month concept belongs to the clearing threshold exit notice regime, not the reporting exemption. (PDF p.164)

question: For how long must records of specified OTC derivative transactions be kept under the OTCD Reporting Rules?
options: At least two years after the transaction is entered into
At least five years after the termination or maturity date of the transaction, but only if conducted in Hong Kong
At least five years after the termination or maturity date of the transaction, irrespective of whether it was conducted partly or wholly outside Hong Kong
At least seven years after the termination or maturity date of the transaction
explanation: Records must be kept for at least five years after the termination or maturity date of the transaction, irrespective of whether the transaction was conducted partly or wholly outside of Hong Kong, and must be readily accessible and sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the reporting obligations. Option B wrongly limits the duty to Hong Kong transactions, and the two-year and seven-year periods are not stated in the text. (PDF p.164)

question: A licensed corporation breaches the OTCD Reporting Rules. Before which court may the SFC bring it, and what is the maximum penalty the court may impose?
options: The Court of First Instance; HK$5 million
The Court of First Instance; HK$10 million
The Market Misconduct Tribunal; HK$5 million
A magistrates' court; HK$1 million
explanation: The SFC is empowered to bring licensed corporations that breach the rules before the Court of First Instance, and the maximum penalty the Court may impose is HK$5 million. HK$10 million and HK$1 million are not figures stated in the text, and neither the Market Misconduct Tribunal nor a magistrates' court is the forum identified for these breaches. (PDF p.165)

section: Clearing OTC derivative transactions - Mandatory clearing obligation
question: Where a clearing obligation arises in relation to an OTC derivative transaction, within what time must the transaction be cleared, and through what facility?
options: Within two business days, through the Hong Kong Trade Repository
Within one business day after the transaction is entered into, through a designated CCP
Within one business day, through any overseas clearing house
Within three months, through a designated CCP
explanation: The transaction must be cleared through a designated CCP within one business day after the transaction is entered into, unless a relevant exemption applies; only recognised clearing houses under the SFO or persons authorised by the SFC to provide automated trading services are eligible to act as a CCP. Two business days (T+2) is the reporting deadline, the HKTR is the reporting repository rather than a clearing facility, an overseas clearing house only suffices under the separate substituted compliance regime for designated jurisdictions and designated CCPs, and three months is the reporting grace period. (PDF p.166)

section: Product subject to the clearing obligation
question: Which class of product is subject to mandatory clearing in the first phase of the OTCD Clearing Rules?
options: Credit default swaps with specified tenors
Interest rate swaps with specified tenors, comprising fixed to floating IRS, floating vs. floating IRS (basis swaps) and overnight index swaps
All OTC derivative products specified by the HKMA
Equity options and index futures
explanation: The only class of product subject to mandatory clearing in the first phase is interest rate swaps with specified tenors (ranging from a week up to many years), covering the three specified product types: fixed to floating IRS, floating vs. floating IRS (basis swaps), and overnight index swaps. Credit default swaps and equity products are not mentioned as cleared products, and the HKMA-specified transactions concept belongs to the reporting regime, not the first-phase clearing scope. (PDF p.166)

section: Persons subject to the clearing obligation
question: Mr Chan's licensed corporation reached the US$20 billion clearing threshold two years ago. Its trading profile has since changed and its position has remained below the threshold by the specified amount for 12 consecutive months. What can it do to avoid the clearing obligation going forward?
options: Nothing; once the threshold is crossed the clearing obligation applies permanently with no relief
Submit an exit notice to the SFC, its frontline regulator
Submit an exit notice to the HKMA
Apply for substituted compliance
explanation: Persons who reach the clearing threshold are regarded as having crossed it at all times thereafter, but where their position falls below the threshold by a specified amount for 12 consecutive months they may submit an exit notice to their frontline regulator — the SFC for a licensed corporation (the HKMA only for AFIs and AMBs). Option A ignores the exit notice mechanism, and substituted compliance is a different concept relieving the need to clear in Hong Kong where a transaction was cleared under a designated jurisdiction's laws through a designated CCP. (PDF p.167)

question: An open-ended fund company (OFC) established under Part IVA, SFO is a collective investment scheme established in Hong Kong in which legal form?
options: A unit trust constituted by a trust deed
A limited liability company with variable capital
A limited partnership with fixed capital
An unincorporated mutual fund arrangement
explanation: An OFC is a CIS established in Hong Kong in the form of a limited liability company with variable capital, giving flexibility to meet investor applications and redemptions. It is not a unit trust or unincorporated arrangement, and its capital is variable rather than fixed, so the other options are wrong. (PDF p.169)

question: Under section 112ZK, SFO, which body may issue rules (subsidiary legislation) for regulating OFCs and the carrying on of collective investments by OFCs?
options: The Registrar of Companies
The Hong Kong Monetary Authority
The SFC
The Hong Kong Securities and Investment Institute
explanation: Section 112ZK, SFO provides that the SFC may issue rules for regulating OFCs, and accordingly it has made the OFC Rules. The Registrar of Companies undertakes the incorporation of OFCs but does not make the rules; the HKMA's role in this chapter relates to OTC derivative reporting, not OFCs; the HKSII is not a rule-making body. (PDF p.169)

question: Which statement correctly describes the process of incorporating an OFC under Part 2, OFC Rules?
options: The fees and documents required for incorporation are provided to the SFC, which provides them to the Registrar of Companies if the SFC determines to register the OFC; the Registrar then undertakes the incorporation
The fees and documents are filed directly with the Registrar of Companies, which forwards them to the SFC for registration after incorporation
The SFC both registers and incorporates the OFC without any involvement of the Registrar of Companies
The Registrar of Companies both registers and incorporates the OFC and then notifies the SFC
explanation: The incorporation of an OFC is undertaken by the Registrar of Companies, but the relevant fees and documents must be provided to the SFC, which then provides them to the Registrar if the SFC determines to register the OFC. The flow is therefore SFC first, then Registrar — not the reverse — and neither body performs both the registration and incorporation functions alone. (PDF p.169)

question: Prior to incorporating an OFC, an application must be made to the SFC with specified details. Which of the following is NOT among the details listed in Part 2, OFC Rules?
options: The name of the OFC
The identity of the custodian
The identity of the auditor
The address of the registered office
explanation: The pre-incorporation application must include the name of the OFC, the address of the registered office, and the identity of the director(s), the investment manager and the custodian. The auditor is not listed among these details — the power to appoint the auditor rests with the directors. The other three options are all expressly listed. (PDF p.169)

question: An OFC enters into a contract with a third party in breach of a restriction in its instrument of incorporation. The third party dealt with the OFC in good faith. What is the legal position?
options: The contract is automatically void
The legal obligations under the contract are not invalidated in favour of the third party
The contract is valid only if shareholders ratify it by special resolution
The contract is valid only if the SFC approves it retrospectively
explanation: In favour of a third party dealing in good faith with an OFC, an act in breach of a restriction on the OFC's powers does not invalidate any legal obligation, such as those embedded in a contract. The contract is therefore not void, and no shareholder ratification or SFC approval is required for it to stand. Shareholders' remedy is the right to commence proceedings to restrain the OFC from acting in breach of its powers, not to void completed contracts with good-faith third parties. (PDF p.169)

question: Under Part 3, OFC Rules, who is required to maintain a public register of OFCs with specified information for each OFC?
options: The SFC
The Registrar of Companies
Each OFC's investment manager
Each OFC's custodian
explanation: Part 3, OFC Rules deals with the functions of the Registrar of Companies, including the requirement that the Registrar maintains a public register of OFCs. The SFC registers OFCs but the public register is the Registrar's responsibility; the investment manager and custodian are operators of the OFC with no duty to maintain the public register. (PDF p.169)

question: Ms Lee wishes to transfer part of her shareholding in an OFC to her nephew, but the transfer would leave her holding fewer shares than the minimum holding prescribed by the OFC. What may the OFC do?
options: Nothing — shares in an OFC are freely transferable in all circumstances
Refuse to register the transfer
Register the transfer but require the SFC's prior approval
Cancel the shares being transferred and reissue them to the nephew
explanation: Shares in OFCs are transferable subject to limited formalities, but the OFC Rules specify circumstances where the OFC is entitled to refuse to register a transfer — including where the transfer would result in the transferor or transferee holding less than any minimum holding prescribed by the OFC. Transferability is therefore not unconditional, and neither SFC approval of the transfer nor cancellation and reissue is the mechanism described in the OFC Rules. (PDF p.170)

question: Which of the following is NOT among the persons expressly stated as entitled to inspect an OFC's register of shareholders?
options: Any shareholder of the OFC
The investment manager of the OFC
The auditor of the OFC
The SFC
explanation: The register of shareholders must be kept open for inspection by any shareholder of the OFC, the investment manager or custodian of the OFC, or the SFC (among others). The auditor is not expressly named in this list — though the auditor does have separate powers of access to the OFC's accounting records. Shareholders, the investment manager and the SFC are all expressly listed. (PDF p.170)

question: Under Part 5, OFC Rules, shareholders holding what minimum proportion of the total voting rights of all shareholders have the right to require the directors to call a general meeting and to require the inclusion of a proposed resolution at that meeting?
explanation: Shareholders holding at least 10% of the total voting rights of all shareholders may require the directors to call a general meeting and require the inclusion of a proposed resolution. 5% is the threshold for proposing a written resolution, not for requisitioning a meeting; 14 relates to the minimum days of notice for an ordinary general meeting, not a percentage; 75% is the majority needed to pass a special resolution. (PDF p.170)

question: An OFC proposes a resolution at a general meeting to remove its auditor. What is the minimum notice required for the meeting?
options: Not less than 7 days
Not less than 10 days
Not less than 14 days
Not less than 28 days
explanation: Special notice of not less than 28 days is required where the resolution proposed concerns the removal or appointment of a director, or the removal of an auditor (Part 6, OFC Rules). Not less than 14 days is the ordinary general meeting notice period, which does not apply here; 7 and 10 days are not notice periods stated in the OFC Rules. (PDF p.170)

question: How is a special resolution of an OFC passed?
options: By a simple majority of the shareholders who vote
By a majority of at least 75% of the shareholders who vote and cast their votes in favour
By a majority of at least 75% of all shareholders, whether or not they vote
By unanimous approval of all shareholders
explanation: A special resolution is passed by a majority of at least 75% of the shareholders who vote and cast their votes in favour. A simple majority describes an ordinary resolution, not a special resolution; the 75% threshold applies to shareholders who actually vote, not to all shareholders; unanimity is not required. (PDF p.170)

question: A group of shareholders holding 6% of an OFC's shares wants to pass a resolution by way of a written resolution without the required notice period. Which of the following resolutions could NOT be passed this way?
options: A resolution to remove the auditor
A resolution to approve a change of the OFC's registered office
A resolution to vary the rights of a class of shares in accordance with the instrument of incorporation
A resolution to approve the annual report
explanation: Any resolution may be proposed and passed as a written resolution without the required notice period, save in relation to the removal of a director or an auditor — so removal of the auditor cannot be done by written resolution. Either the directors or not less than 5% of shareholders may propose a written resolution, so the 6% holding is sufficient to propose one for the other matters. The other three options fall within the general permission. (PDF p.170)

question: When a written resolution is proposed for an OFC, to whom must a copy of the proposed written resolution be provided?
options: All shareholders only
All shareholders, the auditor, the custodian and the investment manager
The SFC and the Registrar of Companies only
The directors and the SFC only
explanation: A copy of the proposed written resolution must be provided to all shareholders, the auditor, the custodian and the investment manager. Providing it to shareholders alone is insufficient, and the OFC Rules do not state that the copy goes to the SFC or the Registrar of Companies. (PDF p.170)

question: An OFC's instrument of incorporation does NOT require it to hold an annual general meeting. With whom does the power to appoint directors (other than the first directors) reside?
options: The shareholders, by ordinary resolution at a general meeting
The directors of the OFC
The SFC
The investment manager
explanation: Where the instrument of incorporation does not require an annual general meeting, the power to appoint directors resides with the directors of the OFC. Appointment at a meeting applies only where the instrument requires an annual general meeting (in which case directors may still fill a vacancy until the next such meeting). The SFC and the investment manager have no power of appointment of directors under these provisions. (PDF p.171)

question: Mr Chan, a shareholder of an OFC, wants a director removed, but the OFC's instrument of incorporation states that directors may only be removed by special resolution. How can the director be removed?
options: Only by special resolution, because the instrument of incorporation prevails
By ordinary resolution at a general meeting, irrespective of the instrument of incorporation, provided special notice of the meeting has been given
By written resolution proposed by at least 5% of shareholders
Only with the prior approval of the SFC
explanation: Directors can be removed at any time by ordinary resolution at a general meeting irrespective of any provision in the OFC's instrument of incorporation, provided special notice (not less than 28 days) of the meeting has been given — so the instrument cannot impose a higher threshold. A written resolution cannot be used because removal of a director is expressly excluded from the written resolution procedure. Prior SFC approval applies to appointing a new custodian or investment manager, not to removing a director. (PDF p.171)

question: On what terms must an OFC's register of directors be open for inspection?
options: To any shareholder at no cost, and to any other person upon payment of a reasonable fee
To any person at no cost
To shareholders and the SFC only, both upon payment of a reasonable fee
To the directors and auditor only
explanation: An OFC must keep a register of directors that is open for inspection to any shareholder at no cost and to any other person upon payment of a reasonable fee. It is therefore not free to everyone, shareholders cannot be charged, and access is not limited to directors and the auditor. (PDF p.171)

question: A director of an OFC realises she has a significant interest in a transaction the OFC is proposing to enter into. When must she declare her interest to the other directors?
options: Before the transaction is entered into
As soon as reasonably practicable after the transaction is entered into
At the next annual general meeting
Within one business day of becoming aware of the interest
explanation: In the case of a proposed transaction, the declaration of interest must be made before the transaction is entered into. The 'as soon as reasonably practicable' standard applies where the transaction has already been entered into, which is not this scenario. Declaration at the next annual general meeting is not the rule, and the one-business-day deadline relates to reporting client money failures to the SFC, not directors' interests. (PDF p.171)

question: The directors of an OFC wish to terminate the existing custodian and appoint a new one. Which of the following correctly states the requirements?
options: The directors may appoint the new custodian subject to the prior approval of the SFC, and the outgoing custodian must make a statement as to whether there are circumstances that should be brought to the attention of shareholders or creditors, with a copy to the SFC
The directors may appoint the new custodian freely; only the SFC needs to be notified afterwards
The shareholders must approve the new custodian by special resolution, and the outgoing custodian has no further obligations
The Registrar of Companies must approve the new custodian before the appointment takes effect
explanation: Directors may appoint a new custodian subject to receiving the prior approval of the SFC, and a custodian ceasing to act must make a statement as to whether there are any circumstances that should be brought to the attention of the OFC's shareholders or creditors (disclosing them if there are) and provide a copy to the SFC. Mere after-the-fact notification is insufficient because SFC approval must be prior; no shareholder special resolution or Registrar approval is required. (PDF p.171)

question: Which statement about the auditor of an OFC is correct?
options: An OFC may choose not to appoint an auditor if its instrument of incorporation so provides
Every OFC must have an auditor, and the power to make the appointment rests with the directors
The auditor is appointed by the SFC at the time the OFC is registered
The auditor is appointed by the shareholders by special resolution
explanation: Every OFC must have an auditor and the power to make such appointment rests with the directors. Having an auditor is mandatory, so the instrument of incorporation cannot dispense with it; the auditor is not appointed by the SFC or by shareholder special resolution. The OFC Rules also give the auditor powers of access to the OFC's accounting records and to make enquiries of any officer, the custodian or the person responsible for the accounting records. (PDF p.172)

question: What are the obligations of the directors of an OFC regarding the annual report?
options: Prepare and publish an annual report for each financial year, including the financial statements and the auditor's report, and provide a free copy to any shareholder who requests it
Prepare an annual report every six months and file it with the Registrar of Companies
Prepare an annual report and provide copies to shareholders upon payment of a reasonable fee
Prepare an annual report containing the financial statements only; the auditor's report is provided separately to the SFC
explanation: The directors must prepare and publish an annual report for each financial year and provide, free of charge, a copy to any shareholder that requests it; the report must include the financial statements and the auditor's report on those statements. The report is annual, not half-yearly; the copy is free, not for a fee (the reasonable fee applies to non-shareholders inspecting the register of directors); and the auditor's report forms part of the annual report rather than being sent separately to the SFC. (PDF p.172)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, is asked whether every OTC derivative transaction to which his licensed corporation is a party must be cleared under the OTCD Clearing Rules. What is the correct answer?
options: Yes, all OTC derivative transactions of licensed corporations must be centrally cleared
No, the OTCD Clearing Rules only apply to transactions between larger and more significant participants in the OTC derivatives market that pose systemic risks
No, the OTCD Clearing Rules only apply to transactions specified by the HKMA as subject to transaction information reporting
Yes, unless the counterparty is a professional investor
explanation: The OTCD Clearing Rules apply only to transactions between larger and more significant participants in the OTC derivatives market that pose systemic risks, so not every transaction of a licensed corporation is caught. HKMA specification of products relates to the OTCD Reporting Rules, not the Clearing Rules. There is no blanket clearing obligation, and a professional investor exception is not the test stated. (PDF p.175)

question: Which SFC code of conduct applies to asset management (Type 9 regulated activity)?
options: The Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct (CFA Code)
The Fund Manager Code of Conduct (FMCC)
The Code of Conduct for Persons Providing Credit Rating Services (CRA Code)
The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars
explanation: The FMCC applies to asset management, which is Type 9 regulated activity. The CFA Code applies to advising on corporate finance (Type 6), and the CRA Code applies to persons providing credit rating services (Type 10), not asset management. The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars does not apply directly to a regulated activity but is relevant to listed issuers and the Listing Rules. (PDF p.178)

question: Although the OFC Code does not apply directly to a regulated activity, why is it important for licensed persons to understand?
options: It requires that an investment manager holding a Type 9 licence be appointed, who needs to be aware of its provisions
It requires every open-ended fund company to appoint a Type 6 corporate finance adviser
It applies directly to Type 9 regulated activity in the same way as the FMCC
It requires open-ended fund companies to appoint a licensed share registrar
explanation: The OFC Code requires, among other things, that an investment manager holding a Type 9 (asset management) licence be appointed to an open-ended fund company, and that manager will need to be aware of the OFC Code's provisions. It does not require a Type 6 adviser or a share registrar to be appointed. The source states the OFC Code does not apply directly to a regulated activity, so it does not apply directly to Type 9 like the FMCC. (PDF p.178)

question: The CRA Code is applicable to persons carrying on which type of regulated activity?
options: Type 6 regulated activity
Type 9 regulated activity
Type 10 regulated activity
Type 4 regulated activity
explanation: The CRA Code applies to persons providing credit rating services, which is Type 10 regulated activity. Type 6 (advising on corporate finance) is covered by the CFA Code and Type 9 (asset management) by the FMCC, so those are wrong. Type 4 is not linked to any of the codes listed in this Topic. (PDF p.180)

question: The OFC Code is applicable to which of the following?
options: Collective investment schemes managed on a discretionary basis by licensed persons
Funds established in Hong Kong as an incorporated company with limited liability and variable capital, and the key operators of those funds
All unit trusts authorised by the SFC for offer to the public
Companies listed on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
explanation: The OFC Code applies to funds established in Hong Kong as an incorporated company with limited liability and variable capital, and to the key operators of those funds (the directors, the investment manager and the custodian). Discretionary management of CISs is the territory of the FMCC, so option (a) is wrong. Authorised unit trusts and SEHK-listed companies are not what the source says the OFC Code covers. (PDF p.180)

question: Which body published the handbook "Ethics in Practice: A Practical Guide for Financial Practitioners"?
options: The Securities and Futures Commission acting alone
The Independent Commission Against Corruption, in collaboration with a number of organisations including the SFC and the Hong Kong Securities and Investment Institute
The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
The Hong Kong Securities and Investment Institute acting alone
explanation: The ICAC published the handbook in collaboration with a number of organisations, including the SFC and the Hong Kong Securities and Investment Institute, and it is based on ICAC experience with practical examples. The SFC and HKSI were collaborators, not sole publishers, so options (a) and (d) are wrong. The SEHK is not mentioned in connection with the handbook. (PDF p.181)

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the nine general principles on which the Code of Conduct is based?
options: Client assets
Best execution
Responsibility of senior management
Conflicts of interest
explanation: Best execution is not one of the nine general principles; it is a specific practical requirement falling under General Principle 2 (diligence). Client assets, responsibility of senior management and conflicts of interest all appear in the list of nine general principles, alongside honesty and fairness, diligence, capabilities, information about clients, information for clients and compliance. (PDF p.182)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative acting as agent, accepts money from a third party which might induce him not to act in the best interests of his client. Under which provision is this an offence?
options: Section 8, Prevention of Bribery Ordinance
Section 9, Prevention of Bribery Ordinance
The Electronic Transactions Ordinance
Paragraph 3.9, Code of Conduct
explanation: Section 9, POBO makes it an offence for an agent such as a licensed or registered person to solicit or accept unfair advantages which might induce him not to act in the best interests of his client; it also makes it an offence to offer an advantage to an agent. Section 8, POBO deals with bribery of public servants by persons having dealings with public bodies, which is not the situation here. The ETO concerns certification services for client agreements, and paragraph 3.9, Code of Conduct deals with telephone orders. (PDF p.183)

question: Under the Code of Conduct, the requirement of "best execution" means a licensed or registered person should:
options: execute client orders on the best available terms
take all reasonable steps to execute client orders promptly
promptly and fairly allocate executed orders to clients
base advice and recommendations on thorough analysis taking into account available alternatives
explanation: Best execution requires executing client orders on the best available terms. Option (b) describes "prompt execution" and option (c) describes "prompt and fair allocation", which are separate diligence requirements. Option (d) describes the requirement to advise clients with due skill, care and diligence. All four appear under General Principle 2, but only option (a) is best execution. (PDF p.183)

question: Mr Lam, a licensed dealer, holds back a client's order for half a day because executing it immediately would be inconvenient for his firm's own trading. Which Code of Conduct requirement has he breached?
options: Prompt execution, under General Principle 2 (diligence)
Prompt collection of margin, under General Principle 2 (diligence)
Honesty and fairness, under General Principle 1
Suitability, under General Principle 4 (information about clients)
explanation: The prompt execution requirement states that client orders should not be held up or withdrawn for the convenience of the licensed or registered person or persons other than the client placing the order, which is exactly what Mr Lam has done. Prompt collection of margin is a different diligence provision and is not engaged here. General Principle 1 concerns honesty, fairness and accurate information, and suitability concerns recommendations matching client circumstances, neither of which is the specific breach. (PDF p.183)

question: Under paragraph 3.9, Code of Conduct, recordings of client orders received by telephone should be maintained for at least:
options: two business days
three Hong Kong trading days
six months
two years
explanation: A telephone recording system should be used to record orders received by telephone, and recordings should be maintained for at least six months. Two business days is the time limit for providing ultimate client identity information to the SFC, and three Hong Kong trading days is the time allowed for reporting OTC securities transactions, so both are wrong here. Two years is not a period stated in the source. (PDF p.184)

question: Ms Wong, a licensed representative, receives a client's order instruction on her mobile phone while she is away from the office and outside any restricted area. What should she do?
options: Write the order details in her notebook and enter them into the firm's system when she returns to the office
Immediately record the time of receipt and the order details by a call to the office telephone recording system
Refuse to accept the order, as orders may never be accepted by mobile phone
Ask the client to confirm the order by email within six months
explanation: Where orders are accepted by mobile phone outside the restricted areas, the time of receipt and the order details should be recorded immediately by a call to the office telephone recording system. A notebook entry made later does not satisfy this immediate recording requirement. Mobile phone use is strongly discouraged and prohibited in the restricted areas, but not absolutely banned outside them, so option (c) is wrong; six months is the recording retention period, not a confirmation deadline. (PDF p.184)

question: In promoting a specific investment product to a client, what is the only gift a licensed or registered person may offer?
options: A cash rebate paid into the client's bank account
A discount of fees or charges
A shopping voucher of modest value
Any gift, provided it is disclosed to the SFC
explanation: The Code of Conduct states that in promoting a specific investment product to a client, a licensed or registered person should not offer any gift other than a discount of fees or charges. Cash rebates and shopping vouchers are gifts other than fee discounts and so are not permitted. There is no stated exception allowing other gifts upon disclosure to the SFC. (PDF p.184)

question: A licensed person entering into non-centrally cleared over-the-counter derivative (OTCD) transactions should apply risk management standards to financial exposures to its group affiliates, shareholders, directors and employees that are:
options: more lenient than those applied to independent third parties, given the existing relationship
the same as it would apply to independent third parties on an arm's length basis
set individually by the SFC for each licensed person
applicable only where the exposure exceeds a monetary threshold set in the Code of Conduct
explanation: Such a licensed person should implement proper risk mitigation requirements and apply the same risk management standards to exposures to its group affiliates, shareholders, directors and employees as it would apply to independent third parties on an arm's length basis. More lenient treatment for connected parties is precisely what the requirement rules out. The source does not say the SFC sets standards individually or that any monetary threshold applies. (PDF p.184)

question: Under the know your client requirements, a licensed or registered person should take all reasonable steps to establish each of the following about a client EXCEPT the client's:
options: true and full identity
financial situation or strength
tax residency
investment objectives
explanation: The KYC requirement is to establish the client's true and full identity, financial situation or strength, investment experience and investment objectives; tax residency is not one of the four stated items. The other three options are all expressly listed in the source. Tax residency is a plausible-sounding addition but does not appear in the Code's KYC list. (PDF p.185)

question: In the case of a collective investment scheme (CIS) or discretionary account, when does a licensed or registered person need to establish the identities of the ultimate beneficiaries?
options: Always, before executing any transaction for the CIS or account
Only where the ultimate beneficiaries are in fact giving the instructions
Never, since establishing the identity of the manager is always sufficient
Only when the SFC makes a specific request under the Client Identity Rule Policy
explanation: For a CIS or discretionary account, the licensed or registered person must establish the identity of the CIS or account and its manager, and the identities of the ultimate beneficiaries only need to be established where they are in fact giving the instructions. "Always" overstates the requirement and "never" understates it, since beneficiary identity is required when they give instructions. An SFC request triggers an obligation to provide information within two business days, but that is not the test for when beneficiary identity must be established. (PDF p.185)

question: Under the Client Identity Rule Policy, the SFC will consider the client identity requirements complied with if a licensed or registered person provides the necessary information on ultimate clients within what period of the SFC's request?
options: Immediately on request
Two business days
Three Hong Kong trading days
Six months
explanation: The Client Identity Rule Policy states that the SFC will consider the requirements complied with if the necessary information on ultimate clients is provided within two business days of the request; failure to comply will reflect adversely on fitness and properness. Three Hong Kong trading days is the OTC trade reporting deadline and six months is the telephone recording retention period, so both are wrong. Immediate provision is not what the Policy specifies. (PDF p.185)

section: Third party authorisation to operate a client’s account
question: Before accepting orders from a third party in respect of a client account, a licensed or registered person must obtain:
options: a written authorisation from the client for the third party to operate the client's account, having established the third party's true and full identity
an oral confirmation from the client recorded on the telephone recording system
the prior approval of the SFC for the third party arrangement
a written authorisation from the third party's employer
explanation: The Code of Conduct requires the licensed or registered person and its staff to take reasonable steps to establish the true and full identity of the third party, keep a record of the third party's identity information, and obtain a written authorisation from the client for the third party to operate the account. An oral confirmation does not meet the requirement for written authorisation. Neither SFC approval nor an employer's authorisation is mentioned in the source. (PDF p.186)

question: When a licensed or registered person makes a recommendation or solicitation, with whom does the primary obligation relating to suitability rest?
options: With the client, who must satisfy himself the product is suitable
With the licensed or registered person, not the client
With the SFC, which approves the suitability of each recommendation
Jointly and equally with the client and the licensed or registered person
explanation: The source states plainly that the primary obligation relating to suitability rests with the licensed or registered person, not the client; the suitability requirement is a key means of fostering investor protection. Placing the obligation on the client, on the SFC, or jointly contradicts this express statement. The SFC's role described in the source is enforcement and guidance, not pre-approving recommendations. (PDF p.186)

question: Mrs Cheung, without any solicitation or recommendation from her broker, instructs her broker to buy a complex product that is not a derivatives product traded on an exchange in Hong Kong or in a specified jurisdiction. What must the broker do?
options: Execute the order without further steps, since there was no solicitation or recommendation
Ensure the transaction is suitable for her in all the circumstances, and provide sufficient information on the key nature, features and risks of the product and warning statements
Refuse the order, since complex products may only be sold following a recommendation
Execute the order only after obtaining the SFC's prior approval of the transaction
explanation: For a complex product (other than a derivatives product traded on an exchange in Hong Kong or in a specified jurisdiction), suitability must be ensured in all the circumstances even where there has been no solicitation or recommendation, and the client must be given sufficient information on the key nature, features and risks plus warning statements. The absence of solicitation therefore does not remove the obligation, making option (a) wrong. Nothing in the source requires refusal of unsolicited complex product orders or SFC pre-approval. (PDF p.186)

question: How do the Guidelines on Online Distribution and Advisory Platforms (ODAP Guidelines) define a "complex product"?
options: An investment product whose terms, features and risks are not reasonably likely to be understood by a retail investor because of its complex structure
Any derivatives product not traded on an exchange in Hong Kong
Any investment product appearing on the SFC's exhaustive list of complex products
Any investment product sold through a robo-advice platform
explanation: The ODAP Guidelines define a complex product as an investment product whose terms, features and risks are not reasonably likely to be understood by a retail investor because of its complex structure. Off-exchange derivatives are relevant to the scope of the suitability note but are not the definition. The SFC's published list of complex and non-complex products is non-exhaustive and will be revised from time to time, and sale via robo-advice does not itself make a product complex. (PDF p.187)

question: Which of the following statements about "robo-advice" under the ODAP Guidelines is correct?
options: It covers only investment advice that is fully automated with no human involvement
It refers to financial advice provided in an online environment using algorithms or other technology, including fully automated, adviser-assisted, and technology-supported advice
It is excluded from the ODAP Guidelines, which cover only the sale of complex products
It refers solely to the supervision and testing of trading algorithms
explanation: Robo-advice means the provision of financial advice in an online environment using algorithms or other technology, including advice that is fully automated, adviser-assisted, and where the adviser is assisted and supported by technology tools. It is therefore not limited to fully automated advice. The ODAP Guidelines include a part specifically applying to robo-advice (covering information for clients, client profiling and the supervision and testing of algorithms), so it is not excluded, and algorithm testing is one requirement within that part, not the definition. (PDF p.187)

question: Under the investor identification regime, the Broker-to-Client Assigned Number (BCAN) is:
options: a unique and permanent number assigned by the intermediary to each client
a unique central entity number assigned to the intermediary by the SFC
a number assigned by the SEHK's data repository to each on-exchange order
a temporary number reassigned to clients each trading day
explanation: An intermediary trading on behalf of clients must assign to each client a unique and permanent BCAN and maintain up-to-date client identification data (CID) for each client. The number assigned to the intermediary by the SFC is the central entity (CE) number, not the BCAN. The BCAN is assigned by the intermediary, not by the SEHK's data repository, and it is permanent, not reassigned daily. (PDF p.188)

question: An intermediary effects an OTC securities transaction that is not subject to OE Trade Reporting and must report it directly to the SFC. What is the time allowed for reporting?
options: Two business days after the trade
Three Hong Kong trading days after the trade
Six months after the trade
By the end of the same trading day
explanation: The time allowed for reporting OTC securities transactions to the SFC is three Hong Kong trading days after the trade, and the report must include the intermediary's CE number, details of the securities traded, the client's CID and the CE number of any other SFC-licensed intermediary involved. Two business days is the deadline for providing ultimate client identity information on an SFC request, and six months is the telephone recording retention period. Same-day reporting is not what the source specifies. (PDF p.188)

question: Mr Lee wishes to open an account by the mail approach without a face-to-face meeting. Among other steps, the licensed person must obtain a cheque issued by Mr Lee in his name drawn on his account with a licensed bank in Hong Kong for an amount of:
options: not less than HK$10,000
not less than HK$1,000
not less than HK$100,000
any amount, provided the cheque is duly paid
explanation: Under the mail approach, the licensed or registered person must obtain a cheque for an amount not less than HK$10,000 issued by the new client in his name as shown in his identity document, drawn on the client's account with a licensed bank in Hong Kong and duly paid, with the signatures on the cheque and client agreement matching. HK$1,000 and HK$100,000 are wrong amounts, and the requirement sets a minimum of HK$10,000, so any amount will not do. The other mail approach steps include copies of the identity document and the signed client agreement. (PDF p.189)

question: When remotely onboarding a client, a licensed or registered person must successfully receive into its own bank account an initial deposit from a bank account in the client's name maintained with a licensed bank in Hong Kong of not less than:
explanation: The required initial transfer is not less than HK$10,000 from a bank account in the client's name maintained with a licensed bank in Hong Kong; all future deposits and withdrawals must then go through the designated bank account(s) only. HK$5,000, HK$8,000 and HK$100,000 are not figures stated anywhere in the text. The same HK$10,000 minimum (or an equivalent amount in other currencies) applies to remote onboarding of overseas individual clients. (PDF p.190)

question: The Code of Conduct requires every client agreement to contain a clause stating that, if the intermediary solicits the sale of or recommends any financial product to the client, the financial product must be:
options: approved by the SFC for retail distribution before it is offered
reasonably suitable for the client having regard to the client's financial situation, investment experience and investment objectives
guaranteed by the intermediary against loss of principal
offered to the client at the lowest commission rate available in the market
explanation: The mandated clause requires that a solicited or recommended financial product be reasonably suitable for the client having regard to his financial situation, investment experience and investment objectives, and that no other provision or statement derogates from this clause. SFC product approval is not what the clause addresses, and the Code does not require intermediaries to guarantee principal or to charge the lowest commission. (PDF p.191)

question: Which of the following best describes a discretionary account?
options: An account over which the client has authorised the licensed or registered person, or a person employed by it (who must also be licensed or registered), to effect transactions without the client's prior approval for each transaction
An account in which the client must approve every transaction in writing before it is executed
An account that may only be operated under absolute discretion with no conditions attached
An account opened for an employee of the licensed or registered person to trade for his own benefit
explanation: A discretionary account is one where the client has authorised the licensed or registered person, or a person employed by it (who must in turn be licensed or registered), to effect transactions without the client's prior approval for each transaction. Option B describes the opposite of discretion. Option C is wrong because the discretion may be absolute or subject to conditions, such as specified restrictions. Option D describes an employee account, not a discretionary client account. (PDF p.191)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, manages a discretionary account whose written authority is about to expire. His firm sends the client a notice before the expiry date stating that the authority will be automatically renewed unless the client revokes it in writing before that date. Is this acceptable under the Code of Conduct?
options: No, the client must positively re-sign a new written authority every year
No, discretionary authorities cannot be renewed and a new account must be opened
Yes, but only if the SFC approves the renewal in advance
Yes, the authority must be confirmed annually, and pre-expiry notification of automatic renewal unless the client revokes in writing is expressly permitted
explanation: The authority must be confirmed annually, and the Code expressly permits the licensed or registered person to notify the client before the expiry date that it will be automatically renewed unless the client specifically revokes it in writing before expiry. A fresh signature each year is therefore not required, and there is no prohibition on renewal or any requirement for SFC approval of renewals. (PDF p.192)

question: Under the Code of Conduct, the opening of a discretionary account should be approved by:
options: the SFC
senior management of the licensed or registered person
the relevant exchange
the employee who will operate the account
explanation: The Code of Conduct requires senior management to approve the opening of a discretionary account, and internal control systems should ensure the account's operation is properly supervised. Neither the SFC nor any exchange approves the opening of individual discretionary accounts. The operating employee cannot approve it himself; the authority merely specifies who is authorised to operate the account. (PDF p.192)

question: When must a licensed or registered person disclose to a client, in writing, the benefits it receives in relation to transactions executed for the client's discretionary account?
options: At the account opening stage or prior to entering into a discretionary client agreement
Within seven days after each transaction is executed
Only when the client makes a written request for the information
At the end of each financial year of the licensed or registered person
explanation: The disclosure must be made in writing at the account opening stage or prior to entering into a discretionary client agreement, and it covers monetary benefits under an explicit remuneration arrangement as well as any other monetary or non-monetary benefits. It is an up-front requirement, not a post-transaction, on-request or year-end one. (PDF p.192)

question: A client account is opened other than through a face-to-face meeting. With respect to risk disclosure statements, the Code of Conduct requires that:
options: no risk disclosure statements are needed because the client was not met in person
the risk disclosure statements may be provided up to one month after the first trade
the covering correspondence to the client should specifically direct his attention to the appropriate risk disclosure statements
the statements must be read aloud to the client over the telephone before the account is opened
explanation: For non-face-to-face account opening, the covering correspondence must specifically direct the client's attention to the appropriate risk disclosure statements. Risk disclosure statements are still required (they must be in a language of the client's choice, English or Chinese, with the client signing and dating an acknowledgement), so option A is wrong. The text imposes no one-month grace period and no requirement to read statements aloud by telephone. (PDF p.192)

question: The Schedule 1 risk disclosure statement on trading GEM stocks refers to:
options: the danger of losing more than the initial margin deposits placed with the firm
the high volatility of GEM stocks, their shorter track record and the need to seek professional advice
the possibility that assets held overseas may not be protected under Hong Kong law
the need to collect all contract notes and statements of account promptly
explanation: The GEM stocks statement covers their high volatility, shorter track record and the need to seek professional advice. Losing more than margin deposits belongs to the margin trading statement, overseas asset protection belongs to the statement on client assets held outside Hong Kong, and prompt collection of contract notes belongs to the statement on authorities to hold or redirect mail. (PDF p.193)

question: Which of the following is correct regarding a client's authority allowing a licensed or registered person to re-pledge the client's securities collateral?
options: The authority is valid indefinitely once given for all clients
The authority must be renewed monthly for every client
If the licensed or registered person defaults, the client's securities are always fully protected
The authority needs to be renewed annually, unless the client is a professional investor
explanation: The risk disclosure statement on re-pledging securities collateral refers to the need to renew such authorities annually unless the client is a PI (professional investor). It also warns that if the licensed or registered person defaults, the client could lose his securities, so option C is the opposite of the stated risk. The renewal cycle is annual, not monthly, and the authority is not indefinite for ordinary clients. (PDF p.193)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, is explaining the margin trading risk disclosure statement to a new client who plans to place "stop-loss" orders to cap any losses. Which warning is consistent with the statement?
options: The client may lose more than his margin deposits, and stop-loss orders may not be effective in limiting loss due to market conditions
Losses are limited to the margin deposits placed, provided stop-loss orders are used
The firm may never liquidate the client's collateral without the client's prior approval
Stop-loss orders guarantee that losses cannot exceed the level set by the client
explanation: The margin trading statement warns that the client may lose more than his margin deposits, may be called upon to make more deposits, that the firm may liquidate the collateral if a margin call is not met, and that stop-loss orders may not be effective in limiting loss due to market conditions. Options B and D wrongly treat stop-loss orders as a guarantee, and option C contradicts the firm's stated ability to liquidate collateral on a failed margin call. (PDF p.194)

question: A licensed or registered person should confirm with clients promptly the essential information of transactions carried out for them. This requirement does NOT apply to:
options: margin trading accounts
discretionary accounts
accounts of professional investors holding GEM stocks
accounts opened other than face-to-face
explanation: The prompt confirmation requirement is expressly stated as not applicable for a discretionary account. The text gives no such carve-out for margin accounts, professional investors holding GEM stocks, or accounts opened remotely. (PDF p.194)

question: Where a licensed or registered person receives monetary benefits that are NOT quantifiable prior to or at the point of entering into a transaction, it should disclose:
options: nothing, since the benefit cannot be calculated
the exact dollar amount of the benefit once it is eventually received
the existence and nature of the benefits, together with the maximum percentage of the monetary benefit receivable per year
only the average percentage of benefit received over the previous year
explanation: For non-quantifiable monetary benefits, the existence and nature of the benefits must be disclosed together with the maximum percentage of the monetary benefit receivable per year, and this disclosure is made on a transaction-by-transaction basis. Disclosure cannot simply be omitted, the requirement is the maximum percentage per year rather than an exact later amount, and an average historical percentage is not what the Code specifies. (PDF p.195)

question: Which of the following describes a "back-to-back transaction" as explained in the Code of Conduct?
options: The intermediary acts as agent to acquire a product on behalf of a client
The intermediary transacts with a third party as principal to satisfy a client's order, for example acquiring a product as principal and then selling it as principal to the client
The intermediary matches the buy order of one client directly against the sell order of another client
The intermediary borrows the product from the client and returns an equivalent product later
explanation: A back-to-back transaction is where the intermediary transacts with a third party as principal in order to satisfy a client's order — for example, first acquiring the product as principal from a third party and then acting as principal to sell it to the client; any trading profit from it should be disclosed. The text expressly distinguishes this from acting as agent on behalf of the client (option A). Client-to-client matching and stock borrowing are not how the Note defines the term. (PDF p.195)

question: Ms Lee, a licensed representative, concludes a transaction for a client over the telephone and cannot make a required disclosure in writing before the transaction is concluded. What should she do?
options: Cancel the transaction, since written disclosure must always precede execution
Make the disclosure verbally, and provide it in writing to the client as soon as practicable after the conclusion of the transaction
Omit the disclosure for that transaction and include it in the next annual statement
Ask the client to waive the disclosure requirement in writing
explanation: Where a required disclosure cannot be made in writing before the transaction is concluded, it should be made verbally and then provided in writing to the client as soon as practicable after the conclusion of the transaction. Cancelling the trade is not required, deferring to an annual statement does not satisfy the requirement, and the text provides no mechanism for the client to waive it. (PDF p.195)

question: Which of the following would NOT, by itself, cause a licensed or registered person to be regarded as not independent?
options: It receives monetary benefits in relation to the distribution of investment products to clients
It receives non-monetary benefits from a party that is likely to impair its independence in relation to a particular investment product
It charges its clients fees for the advisory services it provides to them
It has close links or other economic relationships with product issuers
explanation: The three factors that make a firm not independent are: receiving monetary benefits (whether or not quantifiable) for distributing investment products, receiving non-monetary benefits likely to impair independence in relation to an investment, product class or issuer, and having close links or other economic relationships with product issuers. Simply charging clients fees for its own services is not one of the listed factors. Options A, B and D each restate one of the disqualifying factors. (PDF p.196)

question: Under the Code of Conduct, client orders must be handled:
options: in order of the size of the order, with the largest orders executed first
fairly and in the order in which they are received, with priority over orders for the account of the licensed or registered person or its employees or agents
at the discretion of the dealing room as to sequence, provided all orders are filled by day end
after the firm's own orders, which take priority because the firm bears market risk
explanation: The Code requires client orders to be handled fairly and in the order in which they are received, and they have priority over orders for the account of the licensed or registered person or for the account of any of its employees or agents. Sequencing by size or at the dealing room's discretion is inconsistent with this, and option D inverts the priority rule. (PDF p.196)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, learns that his firm is about to execute a very large buy order for a client in a particular stock. Before the client's order is executed, he buys the same stock for his own account, expecting the client's order to push the price up. Mr Chan has engaged in:
options: a back-to-back transaction, which is permitted with disclosure
front running, i.e. dealing ahead of transactions pending for other clients, which the Code of Conduct requires firms to prevent
soft dollar practice, which is permitted if the client consents in writing
order aggregation, which is permitted if clients receive priority in allocation
explanation: Dealing ahead of transactions pending for other clients is "front running", and the Code requires a licensed or registered person to ensure that neither it nor its employees deal in this way, whether for the benefit of the firm, an employee or a client. A back-to-back transaction concerns acting as principal with a third party to satisfy a client order, soft dollars concern goods or services received from brokers, and aggregation concerns combining orders — none describes trading ahead of a pending client order. (PDF p.197)

question: A licensed person has aggregated orders for several clients together with an order for its own account, and the combined order cannot be completely filled. In the subsequent allocation it must:
options: allocate pro rata between its own account and the clients' accounts
give priority to its own account because its order was placed first
give priority to the orders of clients, and avoid unfair preference for any one client
cancel all the orders and re-enter them separately the next trading day
explanation: Where aggregated orders cannot all be filled, priority in any subsequent allocation must be given to the orders of clients, and the SFC expects the firm to avoid unfair preference for any one client when allocating among several clients. Pro rata allocation that includes the firm's own account, or giving the house order priority, would breach this; cancellation and re-entry is not what the Code requires. (PDF p.197)

question: Which licensed or registered persons are NOT required to comply with the Financial Dispute Resolution Scheme ("FDRS") administered by the Financial Dispute Resolution Centre Ltd. ("FDRC")?
options: Those carrying on Type 1 regulated activity
Those carrying on Type 9 regulated activity
Those carrying on Type 10 regulated activity under the SFO
None – all licensed or registered persons must comply with the FDRS
explanation: A licensed or registered person, other than those carrying on Type 10 regulated activity under the SFO, should fully comply with the FDRS for managing and resolving disputes and be bound by its dispute resolution processes. The Type 10 carve-out means option D is wrong, and Types 1 and 9 are not excluded by the text. (PDF p.201)

section: Rebates, soft dollars, and connected transactions (paragraph 13)
question: Which of the following goods or services is on the SFC's list of those normally PERMITTED to be received as soft dollars?
options: Travel and accommodation
Clearing and custodial services
Membership fees
General office equipment or premises
explanation: Clearing and custodial services appear on the SFC's permitted list, alongside research and advisory services, economic and political analysis, portfolio analysis, market analysis, data and quotation services, incidental computer hardware and software, and investment-related publications. Travel, accommodation, membership fees and general office equipment or premises are all on the not-permitted list, together with entertainment, general administrative goods or services, employee salaries and direct cash payments. (PDF p.198)

section: Rebates, soft dollars, and connected transactions (paragraph 13)
question: Where a licensed or registered person receives cash rebates from a broker for directing a client's transactions to that broker, the rebates and their approximate values must be disclosed to the client:
options: at least once annually
at least twice annually, or in each contract note
at least quarterly
only if the client requests the information in writing
explanation: Rebates and their approximate values must be disclosed to the client at least twice annually, or in each contract note. "At least annually" is the frequency for the separate disclosure of details of goods and services received (and for soft-dollar practice disclosures), so option A confuses the two requirements. Quarterly and on-request frequencies are not stated in the text. (PDF p.199)

section: Rebates, soft dollars, and connected transactions (paragraph 13)
question: A client of Mr Chan's firm exercises his right under a cooling-off mechanism incorporated in an investment product. The firm wishes to keep the sales commission it retained and to deduct an administrative charge that includes a small profit margin. Which is correct?
options: The firm may keep the sales commission but must waive any administrative charge
The firm must refund everything except the sales commission, and may deduct any administrative charge it chooses

The client must receive a full refund including any sales commission, and an administrative charge may only be deducted if it is reasonable, disclosed at or prior to the point of sale, and contains no profit margin
The firm may delay executing the client's instruction until the end of the calendar month
explanation: On exercise of a cooling-off right, the client's instruction should be executed promptly and the client should receive a full refund including any sales commission (covering commission retained by the firm on that transaction). An administrative charge may be deducted only if it is reasonable, disclosed at or prior to the point of sale, and contains no profit margin — so the firm's proposed margin-bearing charge is not allowed. Options A and B misstate the refund and charge rules, and option D contradicts the prompt execution requirement. (PDF p.199)

question: A firm acting as sponsor of an initial public offering should not issue research on the new listing applicant during the "quiet period" (unless issuing such research is part of its normal course of business). The quiet period for an IPO is:
options: the 10 days following pricing of the IPO
the 30 days following pricing of the IPO
the 40 days following pricing of the IPO
the 3 business days following pricing of the IPO
explanation: The quiet period is the 40 days following pricing of an IPO; for a secondary public offering it is the ten days following pricing, which is why option A is a tempting but wrong answer here. The 30-day and 3-business-day periods relate to the separate restrictions on analysts' own dealings around the issue of research, not to the quiet period. (PDF p.200)

question: An analyst at a licensed firm bought shares in a listed company 20 days before the planned publication date of his research report on that company. Under the firm's required written policies on analyst dealing, what should happen?
options: The research should be withheld, because the analyst dealt within the 30-day period before issue of the research
The research may be published as normal, since only dealings within 3 business days of publication are restricted
The research may be published provided the analyst sells the shares before publication
The research must be delayed until 40 days after the analyst's purchase
explanation: Firms' written policies must include limitations on analysts dealing within 30 days before and three business days after the issue of research, and where an analyst has dealt within the 30-day period, the research should be withheld. A purchase 20 days before publication falls inside that 30-day window. The 3-business-day restriction applies after issue, not as the only restriction; selling the shares is not a stated cure; and 40 days is the IPO quiet period, not the analyst dealing rule. (PDF p.200)

question: Under paragraph 12.2 of the Code of Conduct on employee dealings, the term "related accounts" refers to which of the following?
options: Accounts of minor children and accounts in which employees have a beneficial interest
Accounts of spouses and all adult children of employees
Accounts of any family member living in the same household as the employee
Accounts of other employees of the same licensed or registered person
explanation: The source defines "related accounts" as accounts of minor children and accounts in which employees have a beneficial interest. Spouses and adult children are not part of this definition (the spouse/child pairing belongs to the PI Rules' "associate" definition, a common confusion). Household members and fellow employees' accounts are not mentioned at all. (PDF p.202)

question: Mr Chan, an employee of licensed corporation ABC Ltd, is permitted under ABC's policy to deal in securities on a recognised stock market through another licensed firm, XYZ Ltd. What must be arranged?
options: ABC and Mr Chan must arrange for duplicate trade confirmations and statements of account to be provided to ABC's senior management
Mr Chan must obtain prior approval from the SFC for each trade placed through XYZ
XYZ must send Mr Chan's trade confirmations only to its own compliance department
Mr Chan must transfer all his existing positions to an account held with ABC before dealing through XYZ
explanation: Where employees deal through other licensed or registered persons, the principal and the employee should arrange for duplicate trade confirmations and statements of account to be provided to the senior management of the principal (ABC). SFC pre-approval of individual trades is not a requirement of paragraph 12.2. Reporting only within XYZ defeats the monitoring purpose, and there is no requirement to transfer positions to the principal. (PDF p.202)

question: A licensed corporation discovers that a prospective client is an employee of another licensed person. Under paragraph 12.2 of the Code of Conduct, what is required before it can knowingly accept this person as a client?
options: The written consent of that employee's principal
The verbal consent of that employee's principal, recorded in a file note
A notification to the SFC of the new client relationship
A written undertaking from the employee that he will report his own trades
explanation: A licensed or registered person should not knowingly have another licensed or registered person's employee as a client without the written consent of that employee's principal. Verbal consent does not satisfy the requirement, which is expressly written consent. Notifying the SFC is not the mechanism specified, and an undertaking from the employee himself does not substitute for the principal's consent. (PDF p.202)

question: While reviewing a client complaint, a licensed corporation realises the subject matter also affects other clients who have not filed any complaint. What does the Code of Conduct require?
options: The firm should take steps to investigate and remedy the matter even though the other clients have not complained
The firm need only act on complaints that have actually been filed with it or the FDRC
The firm must refer all affected clients directly to the FDRC for resolution
The firm should wait for direction from the SFC before taking any remedial steps
explanation: If the subject matter of a complaint relates to other clients or raises issues of broader concern, the firm should investigate and remedy the matter even if those other clients have not filed any complaint with the firm and/or the FDRC. Acting only on filed complaints is exactly what the provision rules out. Referral to the FDRC is a step available to a complainant when the firm cannot resolve a dispute, not a substitute for the firm's own investigation, and no SFC direction is required first. (PDF p.203)

question: Under paragraph 12.5 of the Code of Conduct, when must a licensed or registered person report matters such as material breaches or insolvency situations to the SFC?
options: Immediately upon discovery
Within 7 business days of discovery
Within one calendar month of discovery
At the time of its next regular regulatory filing
explanation: The Code of Conduct requires reports to the SFC to be made immediately upon discovery of the listed matters. The 7-business-day, one-month and next-filing options are plausible regulatory timeframes but none is what paragraph 12.5 specifies — the obligation is immediate. (PDF p.203)

question: Which of the following is NOT listed in paragraph 12.5 of the Code of Conduct as a matter requiring immediate report to the SFC?
options: The appointment of a new responsible officer
Any material problems with the firm's business systems or equipment
Any insolvency situation affecting the firm's substantial shareholders
Any determination or settlement of a complaint with the FDRS
explanation: Paragraph 12.5 lists material breaches, insolvency situations affecting the firm, its substantial shareholders or directors, disciplinary actions by regulators, material problems with business systems or equipment, suspected material breaches of market misconduct provisions by clients, and determinations or settlements of complaints with the FDRS. The appointment of a responsible officer is not in that list (the SFO and subsidiary legislation contain other notification provisions, but this question concerns paragraph 12.5). The other three options are all expressly listed. (PDF p.203)

question: Under paragraph 12.7 of the Code of Conduct, unless with reasonable reason, a licensed or registered person should not prohibit its employees from performing expert witness services for which bodies?
options: The SFC and the Hong Kong Monetary Authority
The SFC and the Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
The SFC only
The SFC, the HKMA and the FDRC
explanation: The provision covers expert witness services for the SFC and the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA). It does not mention the exchange, and it is not limited to the SFC alone. The FDRC appears elsewhere in the Code (complaints and the FDRS) but is not part of the expert witness provision. (PDF p.204)

question: General Principle 8 requires that client assets be promptly and properly accounted for and adequately safeguarded. Which subsidiary legislation puts this principle into application?
options: The Securities and Futures (Client Securities) Rules and the Securities and Futures (Client Money) Rules
