The Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules
The Securities and Futures (Professional Investor) Rules
Part IIIA of the Securities and Futures Ordinance
explanation: The source states that General Principle 8 is put into application by the Client Securities Rules and the Client Money Rules made by the SFC (studied in Topic 4). The Financial Resources Rules concern a firm's own capital, not client assets. The PI Rules define additional professional investors, and Part IIIA of the SFO deals with OTC derivatives regulation. (PDF p.204)

section: General Principle 9 – responsibility of senior management
question: According to General Principle 9 of the Code of Conduct, who should bear primary responsibility for ensuring the maintenance of appropriate standards of conduct and adherence to proper procedures by the firm?
options: The senior management of the licensed or registered person
The firm's compliance officer
The SFC
Each individual licensed representative for his own conduct
explanation: General Principle 9 places primary responsibility on the senior management of a licensed or registered person, and the SFC will have regard to an individual's apparent or actual authority in relation to the business. The compliance officer is part of the control framework but is not assigned primary responsibility by this principle. The SFC is the regulator, not the bearer of the firm's conduct responsibility, and the principle addresses firm-level responsibility rather than each representative individually. (PDF p.204)

section: General Principle 9 – responsibility of senior management
question: How many managers-in-charge of core functions ("MICs") does the SFC identify for licensed corporations?
options: Eight
Six
Seven
Ten
explanation: There are eight MIC core functions: overall management oversight; key business line; operational control and review; risk management; finance and accounting; information technology; compliance; and anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing. Six, seven and ten are simply incorrect counts of this list. (PDF p.205)

section: General Principle 9 – responsibility of senior management
question: Ms Wong is principally responsible for the anti-money laundering and counter-terrorist financing function of a licensed corporation, but she is not a licensed representative. Is she regarded by the SFC as senior management?
options: Yes – she is an MIC of a core function, and it is not necessary to be a licensed representative to be regarded as an MIC
No – only licensed representatives can be regarded as senior management
No – only directors and responsible officers are senior management
Yes, but only if the SFC first grants her a formal approval as a responsible officer
explanation: AML/CTF is one of the eight MIC core functions, and the source states it is not necessary for an individual to be a licensed representative to be regarded as an MIC; the SFC considers the person's appointed position, seniority and extent of authority. Senior management comprises all directors (including shadow directors), responsible officers AND MICs, so options limiting it to licensed persons or to directors/responsible officers are wrong. No responsible officer approval is needed for MIC status, though changes to an MIC's appointment must be notified to the SFC as for licensed staff. (PDF p.205)

question: A trust qualifies as a professional investor under the Securities and Futures (Professional Investor) Rules. Into which class of PI does it fall?
options: Corporate Professional Investor
Institutional Professional Investor
Individual Professional Investor
It cannot be a PI, as trusts are excluded from the PI regime
explanation: PIs defined in the PI Rules that are corporates, trusts or partnerships are Corporate PIs. Institutional PIs are those defined in Schedule 1 of the SFO, not in the PI Rules. Individual PIs are individuals defined in the PI Rules, and trusts are expressly capable of being PIs, so the exclusion option is wrong. (PDF p.206)

question: To qualify as a Corporate Professional Investor, a trustee company must be responsible for total assets of not less than:
options: HK$40 million (or its equivalent in any foreign currency)
HK$8 million (or its equivalent in any foreign currency)
HK$10 million (or its equivalent in any foreign currency)
HK$100 million (or its equivalent in any foreign currency)
explanation: Trustee companies qualify as Corporate PIs if responsible for total assets of not less than HK$40 million (or its foreign currency equivalent). HK$8 million is the portfolio threshold for individuals and for corporations/partnerships, not the trustee total-assets test. HK$10 million and HK$100 million are not thresholds used in the PI Rules as described in the source. (PDF p.207)

question: XYZ Partnership has an investment portfolio of HK$9 million and total assets of HK$20 million. Can it qualify as a Corporate Professional Investor?
options: Yes – a corporation or partnership qualifies with either a portfolio of not less than HK$8 million or total assets of not less than HK$40 million
No – it must satisfy both the HK$8 million portfolio test and the HK$40 million total assets test
No – its total assets fall below the required HK$40 million
No – partnerships cannot qualify as Corporate PIs
explanation: Corporations or partnerships qualify as Corporate PIs by having EITHER a portfolio of not less than HK$8 million OR total assets of not less than HK$40 million; XYZ's HK$9 million portfolio satisfies the first limb. The tests are alternatives, not cumulative, so failing the total-assets test alone does not disqualify it. Partnerships are expressly included in the Corporate PI class. (PDF p.207)

question: For the purposes of the Corporate PI and Individual PI tests, "portfolio" means:
options: Securities, certificates of deposit issued by a regulated bank, or money
Securities, real property and money
Securities, futures contracts and insurance policies
Any assets held with a licensed corporation, including artwork and commodities
explanation: The source's note defines "portfolio" as securities, certificates of deposit issued by a regulated bank, or money. Real property, futures contracts, insurance policies, artwork and commodities are not within this definition, however valuable they may be. Only the three stated asset types count towards the HK$8 million threshold. (PDF p.207)

question: Mr Lee holds a joint account containing a HK$21 million portfolio with two business partners who are not his associates. There is no written agreement between the account holders on their shares. Does Mr Lee qualify as an Individual PI on the basis of this account alone?
options: No – on an equal share basis his share is HK$7 million, which is below the HK$8 million threshold
Yes – the total portfolio in the account exceeds HK$8 million
Yes – the full value of a joint account always counts towards each holder's portfolio
No – joint accounts with persons other than associates can never be counted
explanation: Where a joint account is held with non-associates and there is no written agreement, the individual's share is determined on an equal share basis: HK$21 million divided among three holders gives Mr Lee HK$7 million, below the HK$8 million threshold. The total account value is not attributed to one holder, so the second and third options are wrong. Joint accounts with non-associates CAN be counted (to the extent of the individual's share), so the fourth option is also wrong; had there been a written agreement giving him a larger share, that share would count instead. (PDF p.208)

section: Provisions of the SFO not applicable to PIs
question: All categories of professional investor are excluded from which provisions of the SFO?
options: The unsolicited calls provisions of s. 174 and the requirements for offers by intermediaries or representatives in s. 175
The unsolicited calls provisions of s. 174 only
The market misconduct provisions of the SFO
The requirement to enter into a written client agreement
explanation: All PI categories are excluded from both s. 174 (unsolicited calls – meaning PIs may be cold-called) and s. 175 (requirements for offers by intermediaries or representatives). The exclusion is not limited to s. 174 alone. Market misconduct provisions are not waived for PIs – indeed suspected material breaches by clients must be reported to the SFC. The written client agreement requirement is a Code of Conduct provision that may be waived for certain PIs, not an SFO exclusion. (PDF p.208)

section: Provisions of the Code of Conduct
question: Which of the following Code of Conduct requirements is listed as one that MAY be waived in respect of professional investors?
options: The need to ensure the suitability of a recommendation or positive invitation
The need to safeguard client assets under General Principle 8
The need to handle client complaints in a timely and appropriate manner
The need to report material breaches to the SFC immediately upon discovery
explanation: The waivable requirements include information for clients, information about clients (including the need to ensure the suitability of a recommendation or positive invitation), the written client agreement and risk disclosure statements, and certain discretionary account requirements. Safeguarding client assets, complaint handling and SFC notifications are core obligations that do not appear in the list of requirements that may be exempted for PIs. (PDF p.209)

section: Provisions of the Code of Conduct
question: Before applying Code of Conduct exemptions to a Corporate or Individual PI, what must a licensed or registered person do?
options: Inform the client of the consequences, explain that PI treatment relates only to a particular product and market and the right to withdraw at any time, obtain written consent, and confirm PI status with a written risk reminder annually
Obtain the client's verbal consent and record it in a file note, with no further ongoing steps
Notify the SFC of the proposed PI treatment and await a notice of no objection
Obtain the client's written consent once, after which the exemption applies indefinitely without further confirmation
explanation: The firm must inform the client of the consequences of PI treatment, explain that it relates only to a particular product and market, explain the right to withdraw as a PI at any time, and obtain written consent; the confirmation of PI status and a written reminder of the risks and consequences must then be undertaken annually. Verbal consent is insufficient, no SFC notification or no-objection process is involved, and a one-off consent without annual confirmation does not satisfy the requirement. Note these steps are needed only for Corporate and Individual PIs – Institutional PIs are automatically exempted. (PDF p.209)

question: Where a Corporate PI has had no investment activity in a particular product or market, after what period should an additional assessment be undertaken?
options: More than two years
More than one year
More than three years
More than six months
explanation: If there has been no investment activity in a particular product or market for a period of more than two years, an additional assessment should be undertaken. One year, three years and six months are plausible review intervals but are not the period stated in the source. Assessments made for applying PI exemptions must also be recorded in writing with all relevant documentation kept. (PDF p.210)

question: When should a sponsor complete all reasonable due diligence on a listing applicant?
options: Prior to submitting the listing application, except for matters that by their nature can only be dealt with at a later date
At any time before the listing is approved by the exchange
Within one month after submitting the listing application
Only after the public offer has commenced
explanation: A sponsor should complete all reasonable due diligence on a listing applicant prior to submitting a listing application, except for matters that by their nature can only be dealt with at a later date. Completing it before approval, within a month after submission, or after the offer commences would all be too late under this requirement. Sponsors must also keep proper books and records sufficient to demonstrate compliance with the Code of Conduct. (PDF p.210)

section: Conduct requirements in relation to over-the-counter derivative ("OTCD") transactions
question: Which of the following describes Type 12 regulated activity introduced in the SFO in connection with OTC derivatives?
options: Providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions
Dealing in OTC derivative products or advising on OTC derivative products
Asset management of portfolios of OTC derivatives
Operating an alternative liquidity pool for OTC derivatives
explanation: Type 12 is providing client clearing services for OTC derivative transactions; dealing in or advising on OTC derivative products is Type 11. Asset management is Type 9, and operating an ALP is governed by paragraph 19 of the Code of Conduct, not by a new OTCD regulated activity. Both Type 11 and Type 12 will be implemented when the relevant commencement notice is issued. (PDF p.211)

section: Mitigating risk in OTCD transactions that are not centrally cleared
question: The SFC's risk mitigation requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions revolve around four key areas. Which of the following is NOT one of them?
options: Margin requirements
Documentation
Valuation
Handling of disputes
explanation: The four key areas are documentation, valuation, portfolio matters and handling of disputes; margin requirements are not listed. These requirements, set out in Part I, Schedule 10 of the Code of Conduct, apply only to OTCD transactions that are not centrally cleared and apply to licensed corporations, not registered persons. The other three options are all expressly among the four key areas. (PDF p.212)

section: Disclosures when dealing with unlicensed persons
question: Licensed corporation LMN Ltd recommends that a client (which is not a group affiliate) enter into an OTCD transaction with LMN's group affiliate, which is not licensed by the SFC. What should LMN do?
options: Include additional risk disclosures in the written client agreement stating that the affiliate is not licensed by the SFC and that the regulatory oversight protection might not be the same as if it were SFC-licensed and regulated
Refuse to arrange the transaction, as dealings with unlicensed group affiliates are prohibited
Give the client a verbal warning about the affiliate's unlicensed status before execution
Proceed without disclosure, provided LMN acts in the best interests of the client
explanation: Where a licensed corporation solicits, recommends or arranges for a client to enter an OTCD transaction with a group affiliate not licensed by the SFC, additional risk disclosures must be included in the written client agreement bringing the unlicensed status and the potentially different regulatory protection to the client's attention. Such transactions are not prohibited, so refusal is not required. A verbal warning does not meet the written client agreement disclosure requirement, and while licensed persons must always act in the best interests of the client when arranging affiliate OTCD transactions, that duty does not replace the disclosure obligation. (PDF p.213)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: The international framework establishing minimum margin standards for non-centrally cleared (NCC) OTC derivatives was developed as part of reforms following the 2008 financial crisis. Which of the following are stated as important objectives of this framework?
options: To reduce systemic risk and to promote central clearing
To promote investor education and to harmonise global tax treatment of derivatives
To eliminate OTC derivatives trading and move all trading onto exchanges
To increase market liquidity and to reduce transaction costs
explanation: The source states the framework's important objectives are to reduce systemic risk and to promote central clearing. Liquidity, transaction costs, investor education and tax harmonisation are not mentioned as objectives, and the framework aims to promote central clearing of OTCDs, not to eliminate OTC derivatives trading altogether. (PDF p.214)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: What is the key difference between initial margin (IM) and variation margin (VM) in respect of an NCC OTCD transaction?
options: IM reflects the potential future exposure at the time of entering into the transaction, while VM reflects the current exposure already incurred as the mark-to-market value changes
IM is posted only by the licensed person, while VM is posted only by the counterparty
IM reflects the current mark-to-market exposure, while VM reflects the potential future exposure at inception
IM is calculated daily, while VM is calculated only once at the start of the transaction
explanation: IM reflects the size of the potential future exposure at the time of entering into the OTCD transaction, whereas VM reflects the current exposure already incurred as the mark-to-market value changes over time. The option swapping the two definitions reverses the rule. The option splitting posting between the parties is wrong because IM and VM are normally posted and collected by both parties (collateral is exchanged). The option on calculation timing is wrong because it is VM that must be calculated at least daily, not IM, and VM is calculated over time rather than only at the start. (PDF p.214)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: To whom do the IM and VM requirements in the Code of Conduct NOT apply?
options: Registered institutions, which will be regulated by the HKMA
Licensed persons that are contracting parties to relevant NCC OTCD transactions
Financial counterparties exceeding the relevant notional amount thresholds
Licensed corporations transacting with covered entities
explanation: The Code of Conduct IM and VM requirements do not apply to registered institutions, which will be regulated by the HKMA instead. Licensed persons that are contracting parties to relevant NCC OTCD transactions and licensed corporations transacting with covered entities are precisely the persons to whom the requirements do apply. A financial counterparty exceeding the thresholds describes a category of covered entity counterparty, not an exempted party. (PDF p.214)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: Which of the following is specifically EXCLUDED from the definition of "covered entity" for the purposes of the NCC OTCD margin requirements?
options: A non-financial counterparty whose group's average aggregate notional amount of NCC OTCDs exceeds the relevant thresholds
A financial counterparty whose group's average aggregate notional amount of NCC OTCDs exceeds the relevant thresholds
The Bank for International Settlements
A registered institution regarded as a financial counterparty
explanation: Sovereigns, public sector entities, multilateral development banks as specified by the HKMA, and the Bank for International Settlements are specifically excluded from the definition of covered entity. Financial and non-financial counterparties whose groups exceed the relevant thresholds are not excluded — they are exactly what covered entity includes. A registered institution is also not excluded from this analysis, since it may be regarded as a financial counterparty for a licensed person determining its own IM/VM obligations. (PDF p.214)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: ABC Securities, a licensed corporation, enters into an NCC OTCD transaction with an overseas counterparty subject to margin requirements of a jurisdiction the SFC regards as comparable. ABC wishes to adhere to that other jurisdiction's margin requirements instead of the Code of Conduct requirements. What must ABC do?
options: Apply to the HKMA for an exemption from the margin requirements
Obtain the prior written consent of the counterparty
Obtain a written legal opinion confirming the enforceability of its netting agreement
Notify the SFC that it will perform "substituted compliance"
explanation: "Substituted compliance" refers to adhering to the margin requirements of another jurisdiction regarded by the SFC or HKMA as having comparable margin requirements, and it applies where the licensed person has notified the SFC. A written legal opinion is relevant to the separate exception concerning reasonable doubt about the enforceability of netting agreements, not substituted compliance. Counterparty consent relates to reuse of received IM, and no HKMA exemption application is described for licensed persons. (PDF p.215)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: Where variation margin (VM) needs to be exchanged for NCC OTCD transactions, how frequently must it be calculated?
options: At least monthly, with collection within 30 days
At least on a weekly basis, and collected within five trading days
Only at the time of entering into the transaction
At least on a daily basis, and collected as soon as practicable
explanation: VM must be calculated at least on a daily basis and collected as soon as practicable. Weekly and monthly frequencies are not stated anywhere in the source; five trading days and 30 days are figures from the FMCC personal account dealing rules, not VM collection. Calculation only at inception describes the timing focus of IM (potential future exposure at entry), not VM. (PDF p.215)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: In which of the following situations does neither IM nor VM need to be exchanged?
options: Where the counterparty is a significant non-financial counterparty that has declared its use of NCC OTCDs is predominantly for hedging purposes
Where the transaction is an NCC OTCD with any financial counterparty
Where the licensed person has no counterparty risk
Where the counterparty is a registered institution
explanation: Neither IM nor VM needs to be exchanged where the counterparty is a significant non-financial counterparty that has declared its use of NCC OTCDs is predominantly for hedging purposes. Where the licensed person has no counterparty risk, only IM does not need to be exchanged — that qualification does not extend to VM. A counterparty being a registered institution does not remove the licensed person's own obligations (a registered institution may still be regarded as a financial counterparty), and NCC OTCD transactions with financial counterparties exceeding the thresholds are precisely where exchange is required. (PDF p.215)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: Mr Chan is a responsible officer of a licensed corporation that has received IM from an OTCD counterparty. The firm wishes to re-pledge the IM. Under the Code of Conduct, the firm may do so only if:
options: The compliance officer gives prior written consent and the reasons are documented
The IM is held with a third party custodian independent of the counterparty
The counterparty has given written consent, and the re-pledge is solely for the purpose of hedging the firm's exposure to the position established with that counterparty
The firm gives the counterparty prior written notice of the re-pledge
explanation: IM received must be treated as client assets and may not be reused (e.g. re-pledged) save with the written consent of the counterparty, and even then only for the purpose of hedging the firm's exposure to the position established with that counterparty. Mere notice is insufficient — consent is required. Compliance officer written consent with documented reasons is the rule for a Fund Manager dealing ahead of funds, not IM reuse. The independent third party custodian requirement applies to IM the licensed person posts, and does not itself permit reuse of IM received. (PDF p.216)

section: Margin requirements for non-centrally cleared OTCD transactions
question: Which of the following securities would be ELIGIBLE as IM or VM collateral under the Code of Conduct?
options: Securities that are not associated with a credit quality of investment grade
Listed shares, subject to haircuts as set out in the Code of Conduct
Securities issued by a company in the same consolidated group as the licensed person
Securities that exhibit significant correlation with the creditworthiness of the counterparty
explanation: Cash, marketable debt securities, gold and listed shares may be accepted as collateral, subject to haircuts set out in the Code of Conduct. The other three options are each expressly identified as ineligible: securities issued by a company in the same consolidated group as the licensed person, securities significantly correlated with the counterparty's creditworthiness or the value of the relevant NCC OTCD, and securities not associated with investment grade credit quality. (PDF p.216)

section: Bookbuilding and placing activities in equity capital market and debt capital market transactions
question: A licensed or registered person that engages in bookbuilding or placing activities in connection with the offering of shares or debt securities is known as a:
options: Discretionary Account Manager
Capital market intermediary
Securities margin financier
Covered entity
explanation: Such a person is known as a capital market intermediary and should comply with paragraph 21 of the Code of Conduct, which aims to ensure bookbuilding and placing activities are conducted in a fair and orderly manner consistent with preserving market integrity. A Discretionary Account Manager is a Fund Manager of a Discretionary Account under the FMCC, a securities margin financier conducts Type 8 regulated activity, and a covered entity is a counterparty category under the NCC OTCD margin requirements. (PDF p.217)

section: Schedules specifying additional conduct requirements for dealings on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited and the Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited, in securities margin financing, leveraged foreign exchange trading, electronic trading and ALPs
question: Schedule 5 to the Code of Conduct specifies conduct and operational requirements for which persons?
options: Persons licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity (providing depositary services for relevant CISs)
Securities margin financiers (Type 8 regulated activity), securities dealers conducting margin financing (Type 1 regulated activity) and their representatives
Leveraged foreign exchange traders (Type 3 regulated activity)
Licensed or registered persons conducting electronic trading
explanation: Schedule 5 deals with margin lending and applies to securities margin financiers (Type 8), securities dealers conducting margin financing (Type 1) and their representatives. Leveraged foreign exchange traders (Type 3) are covered by Schedule 6, electronic trading by Schedule 7, and Type 13 depositary services by Schedule 11. (PDF p.217)

section: Schedules specifying additional conduct requirements for dealings on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited and the Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited, in securities margin financing, leveraged foreign exchange trading, electronic trading and ALPs
question: Schedule 3 to the Code of Conduct, on additional requirements for dealing or trading on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited, principally deals with:
options: Securities margin financing on the SEHK
Options trading on the SEHK
Trading through alternative liquidity pools
Futures contracts and options contracts on the HKFE
explanation: Schedule 3 principally deals with options trading on the SEHK. Futures and options contracts on the HKFE are covered by Schedule 4, alternative liquidity pools by Schedule 8, and margin lending by Schedule 5. (PDF p.217)

question: The Fund Manager Code of Conduct (FMCC) applies to persons licensed by or registered with the SFC whose business involves the management of:
options: CISs and discretionary accounts, but only where the CIS is authorised by the SFC
CISs (whether or not authorised) and/or discretionary accounts, whether in the form of an investment mandate or a pre-defined model portfolio
Discretionary accounts only, in the form of investment mandates
Authorised CISs only
explanation: The FMCC applies to Fund Managers whose business involves managing CISs, whether or not the CIS is authorised, and/or discretionary accounts in the form of an investment mandate or a pre-defined model portfolio. Options B and D wrongly limit application to authorised CISs, and option C wrongly excludes CIS management and pre-defined model portfolios. (PDF p.219)

question: In assessing the effect of a breach of the FMCC on a Fund Manager's fitness and properness, the SFC will adopt a pragmatic approach taking into account all relevant circumstances, including:
options: The profitability of the Fund Manager and the number of its fund investors
Whether the Fund Manager manages authorised or unauthorised CISs only
The size of the Fund Manager and any compensatory measures implemented by its senior management
The length of time the Fund Manager has been licensed and its capital reserves
explanation: The FMCC states that the SFC will take into account all relevant circumstances, specifically including the size of the Fund Manager and any compensatory measures implemented by its senior management. Profitability, number of investors, the authorised status of the CISs managed, length of licensing and capital reserves are not the factors identified in the source. (PDF p.219)

question: Under the FMCC, in which situation is a Fund Manager permitted to charge a mark-up?
options: Where it is acting as a principal, provided the circumstances in which mark-ups may be made are disclosed to the fund and transactions are reported in regular statements or transaction advice
In any capacity, provided the mark-up does not exceed customary institutional rates
Mark-ups are prohibited in all circumstances under the FMCC
Where it is acting as an agent, provided the mark-up is fair and reasonable
explanation: Mark-ups are prohibited where a Fund Manager is acting as an agent, but are allowed where it acts as a principal, in which case the circumstances must be disclosed to the fund (and to fund investors where the Fund Manager is responsible for the overall operation of a fund) and transactions reported in regular statements or transaction advice. The agent option reverses the rule, the blanket-prohibition option ignores the principal exception, and the customary institutional rates standard relates to connected person transactions, not mark-ups. (PDF p.220)

question: Regarding gifts and benefits, a Fund Manager should maintain written guidelines for staff and persons accredited to it which include monetary limits, and should also maintain:
options: A record of allocation intentions before placing orders
A register of any benefits received in excess of specified limits
Written approval from the SFC for each benefit accepted
A register of all benefits received, regardless of amount
explanation: The FMCC requires a Fund Manager to maintain written guidelines (including monetary limits) regarding the offer and acceptance of gifts, rebates or other benefits, and to maintain a register of any benefits received in excess of specified limits. Registering all benefits regardless of amount is too broad — only benefits exceeding the specified limits need to be registered. Recording allocation intentions concerns the separate fair allocation of orders requirement, and there is no requirement for SFC approval of each benefit. (PDF p.220)

question: Ms Wong, a Discretionary Account Manager, will receive a management fee and a performance fee for managing a client's discretionary account. Before providing such services, she must:
options: Obtain a written legal opinion on the enforceability of the agreement
Enter into a Discretionary Client Agreement, in a language understood by the client, setting out the terms and conditions applying to the exercise of the discretion
Notify the SFC that she will perform substituted compliance
Obtain SFC authorisation of the discretionary account's marketing materials
explanation: Before providing discretionary account management services for which a management fee and/or performance fee is received, a Discretionary Client Agreement must be entered into in a language understood by the client; it must set out the terms applying to the exercise of the discretion, comply with the minimum content requirements of paragraph 6.2 of the Code of Conduct, and meet the additional requirements in Appendix 1 of the FMCC. SFC authorisation relates to advertisements and marketing materials where required, not to commencing discretionary services. Substituted compliance and written legal opinions belong to the NCC OTCD margin regime, not discretionary account agreements. (PDF p.221)

question: Under the FMCC, what should a Fund Manager disclose regarding climate-related risks?
options: Only the historical financial losses caused by climate-related events
The governance arrangements for the oversight of such risks and how such risks are factored into its investment and risk management processes
The carbon footprint of each investment held by the fund
Climate-related risks need only be disclosed to the SFC on request
explanation: A Fund Manager should disclose information about climate-related risks, in particular the governance arrangements for the oversight of such risks and how such risks are factored into its investment and risk management processes. Historical losses and carbon footprints of individual investments are not the disclosures specified. The on-request disclosure to the fund concerns the financial information of the Fund Manager's business, not climate-related risks. (PDF p.221)

question: When a Fund Manager participates in IPOs on behalf of funds, the FMCC requires that:
options: Preferential allocations are prohibited and records of the intended basis of allocation and the actual allocation are made
IPO participation by Fund Managers is prohibited entirely
Allocations may be preferential provided fund investors give written consent
Only the actual allocation needs to be recorded, after the orders are placed
explanation: The fair allocation requirement is extended specifically to IPO participation: preferential allocations are prohibited and records of both the intended basis of allocation and the actual allocation must be made. Written consent does not cure preferential allocation — the prohibition is absolute. Recording only the actual allocation is insufficient since the intended basis must also be recorded, and IPO participation itself is not prohibited. (PDF p.222)

question: Under the FMCC, a Fund Manager should not carry out fund transactions with a connected person unless the transactions are:
options: Carried out at arm's length, with standard commission rates not higher than customary institutional rates and on best-execution terms
Carried out at commission rates above customary institutional rates to compensate for the conflict
Approved in advance by the SFC and reported within five trading days
Disclosed in the fund's offering document, with no other conditions
explanation: Fund transactions with a connected person must be carried out at arm's length, with standard commission rates not higher than customary institutional rates and on best-execution terms; borrowing or depositing funds with a connected person should be at standard interest terms or better from the fund's point of view. No prior SFC approval is required, the five trading days figure relates to personal account dealing permissions, and rates above customary institutional rates would breach the requirement rather than satisfy it. Disclosure alone is not the stated condition. (PDF p.222)

question: Mr Chan, a fund manager at a licensed corporation, wants to execute a cross trade between the firm's house account and a client account. Under the FMCC, this is permitted only:
options: Never — cross trades involving the house account are prohibited in all cases
If the trade is conducted fairly and the client is informed afterwards
With the prior written consent of the compliance officer, with reasons documented
With the prior written consent of the client, to whom any actual or potential conflicts of interest should be disclosed
explanation: Cross trades between the house account and client accounts may only be carried out with the prior written consent of the client, to whom any actual or potential conflicts of interest should be disclosed. Compliance officer consent with documented reasons is the rule for dealing ahead of funds, not house-client cross trades. Conducting the trade fairly and informing the client applies to cross trades between client accounts, which do not need prior consent. It is cross trades between staff personal accounts and client accounts that are prohibited outright, not house-client cross trades. (PDF p.222)

question: A Fund Manager wishes to execute an order for its house account ahead of orders for the funds it manages. Under the FMCC house trade rules, this is permitted only:
options: Where the order relates to information already contained in unpublished research reports
With the prior written consent of the affected fund investors
With the prior written consent of the compliance officer or other persons designated by senior management, with the reasons for such consent being documented
Where the house order is smaller than the aggregate fund orders
explanation: A Fund Manager should not deal ahead of funds except with the prior written consent of the compliance officer or other persons designated by senior management, and the reasons for such consent must be documented. Fund investor consent is the requirement for house-client cross trades, not dealing ahead. Order size is not a stated criterion, and using advance knowledge of unpublished recommendations, research reports or analyses to deal before clients have had a reasonable chance to act is expressly prohibited. (PDF p.223)

question: Ms Lee is a relevant person at a Fund Manager and obtains written permission from the compliance officer for a personal account dealing. For how long may that permission remain valid, and how long must she hold the personal investment?
options: Permission valid for no more than five trading days; investment held for at least 90 days in all cases
Permission valid for no more than five trading days; investment held for at least 30 days unless prior written approval is obtained
Permission valid for no more than 30 days; investment held for at least five trading days unless prior written approval is obtained
Permission valid for no more than ten trading days; investment held for at least 30 days unless prior written approval is obtained
explanation: Relevant persons must obtain prior written permission for personal account dealings, which may be valid for no more than five trading days, and must hold personal investments for at least 30 days unless prior written approval is obtained. The 30-day-permission option swaps the two periods, the 90-day option invents a holding period not in the source and removes the approval exception, and the ten-trading-day option uses the wrong permission validity instead of the correct five trading days. (PDF p.224)

section: Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct
question: Which of the following is NOT included in the activity of advising on corporate finance (Type 6 regulated activity) as described in the CFA Code?
options: Advising on the Listing Rules
Advising on the Code on Unit Trusts and Mutual Funds
Advising on the Code on Takeovers and Mergers
Advising on corporate restructurings
explanation: Type 6 regulated activity includes advising on the Listing Rules, the Takeovers Code, the Share Buy-backs Code, giving advice concerning offers to dispose of or acquire securities to or from the public, and corporate restructurings. Advising on the UT Code is not listed as part of this activity. The other three options are all expressly mentioned in the description of the activity. (PDF p.226)

section: Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct
question: If there is an inconsistency between the CFA Code and another code or guideline applicable to a corporate finance adviser, which provision prevails?
options: The CFA Code always prevails
The other code or guideline always prevails
The provision requiring the higher standard prevails
The SFC decides which prevails on a case-by-case basis
explanation: The CFA Code supplements and is applied in conjunction with relevant laws, codes, regulations and guidelines, and where there is any inconsistency, the provision requiring a higher standard prevails. Neither the CFA Code nor the other instrument automatically prevails, so the first two options are wrong. The source does not state that the SFC resolves inconsistencies case by case. (PDF p.226)

question: Under the CFA Code, which statement about a corporate finance adviser's compliance function is correct?
options: It must be headed by a designated compliance officer who is independent of other business functions and reports directly to senior management
It must be headed by a compliance officer who reports directly to the SFC
It may never be assumed by senior management
It is only required where the adviser also acts as a sponsor
explanation: A corporate finance adviser must maintain an effective compliance function headed by a designated compliance officer who is independent of other business functions and reports directly to senior management. The officer reports to senior management, not the SFC. Where necessary, the function may in fact be assumed by senior management, so the third option is wrong, and the requirement applies generally, not only to sponsors. (PDF p.226)

question: Ms Wong's corporate finance advisory firm discovers a material conflict of interest with a client that cannot be resolved through the client giving informed consent. Under the CFA Code, the firm should:
options: Proceed with the mandate after establishing Chinese walls
Proceed with the mandate after disclosing the conflict to the SFC
Proceed with the mandate at a reduced fee
Withdraw from, or not take on, the mandate
explanation: The CFA Code requires an adviser to withdraw from, or not take on, a mandate where there is a material conflict of interest with its client that cannot be resolved through the client giving informed consent. Chinese walls address leakage of confidential information within a group, not an unresolvable client conflict. Disclosure to the SFC or reducing fees are not stated as ways to cure such a conflict. (PDF p.227)

question: A corporate finance adviser is part of a group conducting other business activities. According to the CFA Code, its system of Chinese walls should include:
options: Confidentiality undertakings signed by staff shared across business activities
Physical separation between, and different staff employed for, the various business activities
Quarterly reporting of information flows to the SFC
Disclosure of all price-sensitive information to clients of each business line
explanation: The CFA Code states that the system of functional barriers should include physical separation between, and different staff employed for, the various business activities, to prevent leakage of confidential or price-sensitive information. Shared staff with undertakings contradicts the requirement for different staff. Quarterly SFC reporting and disclosure of price-sensitive information to clients are not stated and the latter is the opposite of the objective. (PDF p.227)

question: How does the CFA Code treat the recording of the terms of a corporate finance adviser's engagement with its client in writing?
options: It is a mandatory requirement, as with the other codes
It is encouraged but not a requirement, unlike the other codes
It is required only when acting as a sponsor
It is required only for engagements involving public documents
explanation: The CFA Code only encourages a corporate finance adviser to record the terms of its engagement in writing; the source expressly notes this is a matter of encouragement and not a requirement as with the other codes. The first option reverses this distinction. The sponsor and public-document qualifications are not stated in the source. (PDF p.228)

question: Mr Chan's firm is acting as financial adviser to a listing applicant, while another firm is acting as the sponsor. Under the CFA Code, Mr Chan's firm should:
options: Co-operate fully with the sponsor and not conduct itself in a way that would unreasonably or adversely affect the sponsor in discharging its duties
Take over the sponsor's duties regarding the listing application
Deal only with the listing applicant and avoid contact with the sponsor
Report the sponsor's work directly to the SFC for review
explanation: A corporate finance adviser acting as a financial adviser to a listing applicant should co-operate fully with the sponsor regarding the listing application and not conduct itself in a way that would unreasonably or adversely affect the sponsor in discharging its duties. It does not take over the sponsor's duties, and avoiding the sponsor would contradict the duty of full co-operation. Reporting the sponsor's work to the SFC is not stated. (PDF p.228)

question: A corporate finance adviser asks its client to report a non-compliance with relevant regulations to the regulators, but the client refuses without valid reasons. Under the CFA Code, the adviser should:
options: Report the non-compliance to the police
Continue acting for the client as normal, since the duty rests with the client
Consider the need to cease to act for the client
Immediately publish details of the non-compliance
explanation: Where a client refuses without valid reasons to report non-compliance to the regulators, the adviser should consider the need to cease to act for the client; the adviser should also co-operate with the regulator if asked about possible breaches. Reporting to the police or publishing the details is not stated in the CFA Code. Simply continuing as normal ignores the consequence the Code prescribes. (PDF p.228)

section: Code of Conduct for Persons Providing Credit Rating Services
question: The CRA Code is primarily based on which document?
options: The SFC's Fund Manager Code of Conduct
The Basel Committee's principles for credit risk management
The International Organization of Securities Commission's Code of Conduct Fundamentals for Credit Rating Agencies
The Listing Rules of the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong
explanation: The CRA Code is primarily based on the International Organization of Securities Commission's Code of Conduct Fundamentals for Credit Rating Agencies, and sets out the specific requirements for the way a CRA engages in Type 10 regulated activity. The FMCC, Basel principles and Listing Rules are not stated as the basis of the CRA Code. (PDF p.230)

question: Under the CRA Code, how often should a CRA review a rating target's creditworthiness, in any event?
options: At least quarterly
At least semi-annually
At least annually
At least once every two years
explanation: A CRA should monitor issued ratings on an ongoing basis and update them as necessary, but in any event it should review a rating target's creditworthiness at least annually. Quarterly, semi-annual and two-yearly intervals are not the periods stated in the CRA Code. (PDF p.230)

question: A CRA introduces changes to its rating methodologies, models or key assumptions. Under the CRA Code, which statement is correct?
options: The changes should be applied to initial ratings only
The changes should be applied to subsequent ratings only
The changes should be applied to both initial and subsequent ratings, and should be fully and publicly disclosed
The changes need only be disclosed privately to rated entities
explanation: Changes to rating methodologies, models or key assumptions should be applied to both initial and subsequent ratings, and any changes introduced should be fully and publicly disclosed. Applying changes only to initial or only to subsequent ratings is therefore wrong, and disclosure must be public rather than private to rated entities. (PDF p.231)

question: Under the CRA Code, a CRA's methodologies and models, and any significant changes made to them, should be subject to a rigorous and formal review:
options: At least annually, with the findings recorded in writing and copied to the SFC upon completion
At least quarterly, with the findings published on the CRA's webpage
At least annually, with the findings kept confidential within the CRA
Only when the SFC requests a review
explanation: The review must take place at least annually, and the findings should be recorded in writing and copied to the SFC upon its completion. Quarterly review and webpage publication are not what the source states. Keeping the findings internal omits the requirement to copy them to the SFC, and the review is a standing requirement rather than one triggered by an SFC request. (PDF p.231)

question: Mr Chan is a representative of a licensed CRA. His sister currently works for a company that the CRA has been engaged to rate. Under the CRA Code, Mr Chan:
options: May work on the rating provided he discloses the relationship to his supervisor
Should not be involved in or in any way influence the rating of that rating target
May work on the rating provided his sister is not employed in the company's finance department
May work on the rating provided the compliance officer pre-approves his involvement
explanation: Representatives who have some form of relationship with a rated entity should not be involved in or in any way influence the rating of the particular rating target; having an immediate relation (a spouse, partner, parent, child, or sibling) who currently works for the rated entity is an example given. The CRA Code does not provide carve-outs based on disclosure, the relative's department, or compliance pre-approval. (PDF p.231)

question: Ms Lee is a CRA representative directly involved in the rating process, with bank ABC within her area of primary analytical responsibility. Which of the following is permitted under the CRA Code?
options: Ms Lee buying bonds issued by bank ABC
Ms Lee's spouse buying shares guaranteed by bank ABC
Ms Lee holding units in a CIS
An account controlled by Ms Lee, in which she has a beneficial interest, buying derivatives based on bank ABC's securities
explanation: Representatives directly involved in the rating process must not deal in securities (or derivatives based on such securities) issued, guaranteed or otherwise supported by any entity within their area of primary analytical responsibility, other than holdings in CISs. The restriction also applies to the representative's spouse, partner, minor children and any account controlled by the representative in which she has a beneficial interest, so the other three options are all prohibited. (PDF p.232)

question: Which statement about a CRA's compensation arrangements with rated entities is correct under the CRA Code?
options: The general nature of the arrangements must be publicly disclosed, and contingent fee arrangements for providing credit rating services are prohibited
The arrangements may be kept confidential provided they are disclosed to the SFC
Contingent fee arrangements are permitted if publicly disclosed
Compensation arrangements need only be disclosed to investors who request them
explanation: The general nature of a CRA's compensation arrangements with rated entities must be publicly disclosed, and a CRA is prohibited from entering into any contingent fee arrangement for providing credit rating services. Disclosure to the SFC alone or only on investor request does not satisfy the public disclosure requirement, and contingent fees are prohibited outright rather than permitted with disclosure. (PDF p.232)

section: Responsibilities to the investing public and rated entities
question: Under the CRA Code, before a rating is issued or revised, the CRA must:
options: Publish the proposed rating to the investing public for comment
Communicate the rating to the rated entity, provided it is feasible and appropriate to do so, giving it an opportunity to clarify any possible factual misperceptions
Obtain the SFC's approval of the rating
Obtain the rated entity's written consent to the rating
explanation: To facilitate more accurate and fully informed ratings, a rating must be communicated to the rated entity before it is issued or revised, provided it is feasible and appropriate to do so, giving the entity an opportunity to clarify possible factual misperceptions or other considerations relevant to the rating's accuracy. There is no requirement for public comment, SFC approval, or the rated entity's consent. (PDF p.232)

question: Which of the following is NOT a key operator of an open-ended fund company (OFC) under the OFC Code?
options: A director of the OFC
The investment manager
The custodian
The auditor
explanation: The key operators of an OFC are its director(s), investment managers and custodian. The auditor, although an OFC must appoint one that is independent of the investment manager, custodian and directors, is not listed as a key operator. (PDF p.234)

question: Under the OFC Code, an OFC is a public OFC if it:
options: Is authorised by the SFC under s. 104, SFO, or intends to apply for such authorisation
Is listed on the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong
Has more than 50 shareholders
Is registered with the SFC under Part IVA, SFO
explanation: An OFC is a public OFC if it is authorised by the SFC under s. 104, SFO, or intends to apply for such authorisation; an OFC that is not a public OFC is a private OFC. Listing status and shareholder numbers are not the test stated in the source. Part IVA, SFO gives the SFC regulatory oversight of OFCs generally and does not distinguish public from private OFCs. (PDF p.234)

question: Which statement about the consequences of a breach of the OFC Code is correct?
options: A breach in itself gives rise to judicial proceedings against the OFC
A breach could lead to the OFC being deregistered, and the Code's provisions can be taken into account in a court where relevant to a judicial proceeding
A breach has no consequences for persons proposing new OFCs
A breach automatically results in the revocation of the investment manager's licence
explanation: For the OFC itself, a breach could lead to deregistration, and although the OFC Code does not have the force of law and breaches cannot in themselves give rise to judicial proceedings, its provisions can be taken into account in court where relevant. A breach may also adversely affect a person's ability to be granted registration of further OFCs, so the third option is wrong, and automatic licence revocation is not stated — for licensed or registered persons a breach may affect fitness and properness and give rise to disciplinary measures. (PDF p.234)

question: A fund whose investment objective is to invest solely in bonds applies to register as an OFC under the name "Global Equity Growth OFC". Under the OFC Code, this name is most likely:
options: Acceptable, because it ends with "OFC"
Acceptable, provided the offering documents describe the bond strategy
Unacceptable, because a name inconsistent with the investment objectives of the OFC may be regarded as misleading
Unacceptable, because OFC names must not refer to asset classes
explanation: An OFC's name must not be misleading, and the source gives the example that a name inconsistent with the investment objectives of the OFC or its other characteristics may be regarded as misleading and therefore unacceptable. Ending with "OFC" satisfies only one of the naming requirements and does not cure a misleading name, and disclosure in the offering documents is not stated as a cure. There is no stated rule against referring to asset classes. (PDF p.235)

question: An OFC proposes to appoint Ms Cheung as its independent director. Which of the following would disqualify her under the OFC Code?
options: She is an employee of the investment manager of the OFC
She is an employee of the custodian of the OFC
She is a shareholder of the OFC
She is a director of an unrelated listed company
explanation: An OFC must have at least one independent director, who must not be a director or employee of the custodian. However, the independent director does not need to be independent of the investment manager, so the first option is not disqualifying under the Code. Being a shareholder of the OFC or a director of an unrelated company is not stated as a disqualification. (PDF p.236)

question: Under the OFC Code, an OFC must delegate its investment management function to an investment manager that is:
options: Licensed by or registered with the SFC for Type 1 (dealing in securities) regulated activity
Licensed by or registered with the SFC for Type 6 (advising on corporate finance) regulated activity
Licensed by or registered with the SFC for Type 9 (asset management) regulated activity
Authorised by the SFC under s. 104, SFO
explanation: An OFC must delegate the investment management function to an investment manager licensed by or registered with the SFC to engage in Type 9 (asset management) regulated activity, so that it is subject to the SFC's oversight; an OFC may appoint more than one investment manager. Type 1 is relevant to an alternative eligibility route for a private OFC's custodian, not the investment manager, Type 6 relates to corporate finance advice, and s. 104, SFO authorisation is the test for a public OFC. (PDF p.236)

question: Under the OFC Code, the custodian of an OFC may hold scheme property in an omnibus account provided that:
options: The scheme property is properly recorded, is subject to frequent reconciliations, and is otherwise held subject to adequate safeguards in line with international standards and best practices
The investment manager gives written consent
The omnibus account contains only assets of the custodian's other clients
The SFC grants a specific waiver from the segregation requirement
explanation: Although the custodian must keep the OFC's assets segregated from assets belonging to others (including the investment manager and its affiliates, and the custodian and its other clients), it may hold scheme property in an omnibus account provided it is properly recorded, subject to frequent reconciliations, and otherwise held subject to adequate safeguards in line with international standards and best practices. Investment manager consent and SFC waivers are not the stated conditions, and mixing scheme property with other clients' assets is exactly what segregation is meant to prevent unless the stated safeguards apply. (PDF p.237)

question: What restriction applies to the investments scope of a private open-ended fund company (OFC)?
options: It should not operate as a corporate entity for general commercial business or trade
It may only invest in securities listed in Hong Kong
It must restrict its investments to Type 1 regulated products
It may only invest with the prior approval of the SFC for each transaction
explanation: Private OFCs are designed to operate as investment vehicles and so should not be operating as a corporate entity for general commercial business or trade. The source imposes no requirement that investments be Hong Kong-listed, limited to Type 1 products, or individually pre-approved by the SFC — those options borrow language from other parts of the regime (the Type 1 custodian alternative and SFC filing requirements) but are not stated as scope restrictions. (PDF p.238)

question: In which of the following circumstances can the instrument of incorporation of an OFC be changed WITHOUT shareholders' approval?
options: Where the change is necessary to correct a manifest error
Where the change affects the distribution policy only
Where the custodian consents in writing to the change
Where the SFC has been notified seven days in advance
explanation: In general the instrument of incorporation can only be changed with shareholders' approval, save in very limited circumstances, for example where the change is necessary to correct a manifest error. Changes to the distribution policy are part of fund operations disclosure, not an exemption; custodian consent is not mentioned as a basis for change; and the seven-day period relates to filing revised offering documents with the SFC, not to dispensing with shareholder approval. (PDF p.238)

question: After issuing a revised offering document, within what period must an OFC file the changes with the SFC?
options: Seven days of issuing the revised offering document
As soon as practicable, with no fixed time limit
Fourteen days of issuing the revised offering document
Seven years of issuing the revised offering document
explanation: Any subsequent changes to the offering document must be filed within seven days of issuing the revised document. 'As soon as practicable' applies to the initial filing of the offering document following its issuance, not to subsequent changes. Fourteen days is not stated anywhere in the text, and seven years is the custodian's record-keeping period, not a filing deadline. (PDF p.238)

question: As an alternative to meeting the eligibility requirements of the UT Code, a custodian of a private OFC may instead be licensed or registered for which regulated activity?
options: Type 1 regulated activity (dealing in securities)
Type 9 regulated activity (asset management)
Type 4 regulated activity (advising on securities)
Type 2 regulated activity (dealing in futures contracts)
explanation: The custodian may instead be licensed or registered to engage in Type 1 regulated activity (dealing in securities), subject to certain requirements, at all times. Types 9, 4 and 2 are other regulated activities but the source specifies only Type 1 as the alternative to the UT Code eligibility requirements. (PDF p.238)

question: Mr Chan works for a custodian of a private OFC. The custodian, which is not a bank, receives money on behalf of the OFC. How should this money be held?
options: In a segregated bank account in Hong Kong
In any bank account in the custodian's name, whether in Hong Kong or overseas
Registered in the name of the private OFC as soon as reasonably practicable
It must be returned to the OFC's investment manager within seven days
explanation: Where money is received on behalf of a private OFC by a custodian that is not a bank, it should be held in a segregated bank account in Hong Kong. A general account in the custodian's own name, or one held overseas, does not satisfy this. Registration in the name of the private OFC as soon as reasonably practicable is the treatment for securities, not money, and there is no requirement to return money to the investment manager within seven days. (PDF p.239)

question: Money received by a custodian on behalf of a private OFC may be paid out for all of the following purposes EXCEPT:
options: Making loans to the directors of the private OFC
Acting in accordance with written directions or standing authority of the private OFC
Meeting settlement or margin obligations arising from the OFC's investment transactions
Settling the custodian's fees
explanation: The source permits money to be paid out in accordance with written directions or standing authority of the private OFC, to meet settlement or margin obligations arising in respect of the OFC's investment transactions, or to settle the custodian's fees. Lending to directors is not among the permitted purposes — and a private OFC should not operate as a vehicle for general commercial business in any case. (PDF p.239)

question: For how long must the custodian of a private OFC keep accounting and other records, and where should they be kept?
options: Not less than seven years, in designated premises approved by the SFC
Not less than five years, in designated premises approved by the SFC
Not less than seven years, at any premises chosen by the custodian
Not less than seven days, in a segregated facility in Hong Kong
explanation: The custodian must keep accounting and other records for not less than seven years, and such records should be kept in the designated premises approved by the SFC. Five years is not the stated period; premises cannot simply be chosen by the custodian because the SFC must approve the designated premises; and seven days is the deadline for filing revised offering documents, not a record-retention period. (PDF p.239)

question: A custodian of a private OFC must adopt internal policies, procedures, systems and controls substantially the same as those for safekeeping client assets in its Type 1 regulated activities. Which of the following is among the laws and regulations that apply as if its custodian role were an integral part of those activities?
options: The Securities and Futures (Client Money) Rules
The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars
The Listing Rules
The UT Code eligibility requirements
explanation: The listed obligations include the Securities and Futures (Client Securities) Rules, the Securities and Futures (Client Money) Rules, the Securities and Futures (Keeping of Records) Rules, the Code of Conduct, the ICG, the GAML and the Guideline on Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Financing of Terrorism (For Authorized Institutions). The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars applies to approved share registrars, the Listing Rules govern listed companies' use of share registrars, and the UT Code eligibility requirements are the alternative route a Type 1 custodian is dispensing with — none appears in this list. (PDF p.239)

section: The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars
question: Under the Listing Rules, what must a share registrar be in order to be approved to maintain a register of shareholders or warrant-holders of a listed company in Hong Kong?
options: A member of the Federation of Share Registrars Limited
A corporation licensed for Type 1 regulated activity (dealing in securities)
A company authorised by the SFC under s. 104, SFO
A registered institution supervised by the HKMA
explanation: To be approved, a share registrar must be a member of the Federation of Share Registrars Limited, and the Listing Rules state that only an approved share registrar may be employed for this role. A Type 1 licence is the alternative eligibility route for private OFC custodians, not share registrars; s. 104, SFO authorisation is what distinguishes a public OFC from a private one; and HKMA supervision of registered institutions is not mentioned as a condition for share registrar approval. (PDF p.241)

section: The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars
question: Who issued The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars, and in consultation with whom?
options: The SFC, in consultation with the Federation of Share Registrars Limited
The Federation of Share Registrars Limited, in consultation with the SFC
The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong, in consultation with the SFC
The SFC, in consultation with the Stock Exchange of Hong Kong
explanation: The Code of Conduct for Share Registrars was issued by the SFC in consultation with the Federation of Share Registrars Limited, and it is similar to the Code of Conduct, containing provisions on honesty and fairness, diligence, capabilities, conflicts of interest, safeguarding of client assets, compliance and responsibility of senior management. The roles in option B are reversed, and the Stock Exchange is not stated as the issuer or the consulted party — the Listing Rules connection is only that approved registrars must be used by listed companies. (PDF p.241)

question: Mr Chan, an employee of a licensed corporation, wishes to deal through another licensed person rather than his own firm. Which statement is correct?
options: He may do so if his principal consents, and he must arrange for duplicate trade confirmations and statements of account to be provided to the senior management of his own principal
He may never deal through another licensed or registered person
He may do so freely without consent, provided he reports the trades to the SFC
He may do so if his principal consents, and the confirmations need only be sent to the executing firm's senior management
explanation: An employee may deal through other licensed or registered persons if his principal consents, and he must arrange for duplicate trade confirmations and statements of account to be provided to the senior management of his own principal. It is therefore not an absolute prohibition, consent of the principal (not a report to the SFC) is the precondition, and the duplicates go to his own principal's senior management, not merely to the executing firm. (PDF p.243)

question: A broker offers an inducement to a fund manager that might induce the fund manager not to act in the best interests of his client, and the fund manager accepts it. What is the position under the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance (POBO)?
options: Both the broker and the fund manager are guilty of offences under the POBO
Only the fund manager who accepted the inducement is guilty of an offence
Only the broker who offered the inducement is guilty of an offence
Neither is guilty unless the client actually suffers a loss
explanation: The POBO makes it an offence for an agent to solicit or accept unfair advantages which might induce him not to act in his client's best interests, and it also makes it an offence to offer an advantage to an agent — so if the inducement is accepted, both are guilty of offences. Options blaming only one party ignore that both offering and accepting are offences, and the source does not condition liability on the client suffering an actual loss. (PDF p.243)

question: The Management, Supervision and Internal Control Guidelines ("ICG") identify how many key areas of internal control and management systems?
options: Seven
Eight
Nine
Ten
explanation: The ICG identify eight key areas of internal control and management systems, collectively referred to as "internal controls". "Nine" is the number of general principles in the Code of Conduct that the eight areas are designed to match, making it the classic confusion; seven and ten are simply incorrect counts. (PDF p.248)

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the eight key areas identified in the ICG?
options: Segregation of duties and functions
Information management
Conflicts of interest
Personnel and training
explanation: The eight key areas are management and supervision, segregation of duties and functions, personnel and training, information management, compliance, audit, operational controls and risk management. "Conflicts of interest" is not a key area heading, although conflicts are addressed within areas such as segregation of duties and operational controls. The other three options are all expressly listed key areas. (PDF p.248)

question: What is the legal effect of a failure by a licensed person to observe the ICG?
options: It is an offence leading to prosecution
It automatically results in revocation of the licence
It is not an offence, but may reflect adversely on the person's fitness and properness to remain licensed or registered
It has no consequences because the ICG are purely advisory
explanation: Like the codes, the ICG do not have the force of law, so a failure to observe them is not an offence leading to prosecution; however, failing to follow their substance may reflect adversely on the fitness and properness of the licensed or registered person to continue to be licensed or registered. Prosecution and automatic revocation overstate the effect, while "no consequences" understates it because fitness and properness can be affected. (PDF p.248)

question: Mr Chan is a responsible officer of a small licensed brokerage with only six staff. The firm cannot maintain separate compliance and internal audit departments. How will the SFC apply the ICG to the firm?
options: It will require the firm to hire enough staff to establish full compliance and internal audit departments
It will apply the ICG pragmatically and be satisfied if other controls compensate for the reduced size and staff
It will exempt the firm from the ICG entirely because of its size
It will require the firm to outsource all of its operations
explanation: The SFC has indicated it uses a pragmatic approach, looking at the reality and substance of a situation rather than applying the ICG rigidly; for a small business it will not expect a complicated system of segregation of functions or large compliance and internal audit departments, and will be satisfied if other controls compensate. Forcing extra hiring contradicts this pragmatic approach, no exemption from the ICG is given, and outsourcing all operations is not mentioned. (PDF p.249)

section: Internal control and internal management systems
question: Internal controls are used by a licensed or registered person to provide reasonable assurance of all of the following EXCEPT:
options: Operating its business in an orderly and efficient manner
Safeguarding the assets of its clients and its own
Maximising the profitability of the business
Complying with all applicable laws and regulatory requirements
explanation: The ICG state that internal controls give reasonable assurance that the firm can operate in an orderly and efficient manner, safeguard the assets of its clients and its own, maintain proper records and reliable financial and other information, and comply with all applicable laws and regulatory requirements. Maximising profitability is a commercial aim, not one of the stated purposes of internal controls. The other three options are all expressly listed. (PDF p.249)

question: Under the ICG, what is the stated objective of the "management and supervision" key area?
options: Senior management should establish and operate an effective management and organisational structure ensuring the business is conducted in a sound, efficient and effective manner
Senior management should delegate full responsibility for internal controls to the compliance department
Senior management should personally execute all client orders
Senior management should ensure incompatible duties are performed by the same person for efficiency
explanation: The objective is that senior management should establish and operate an effective management and organisational structure which ensures the business is conducted in a sound, efficient and effective manner. Delegating full responsibility away is wrong because senior management must assume full responsibility for the operations and internal controls; personally executing orders is not required; and combining incompatible duties is the opposite of what the segregation area requires. (PDF p.249)

question: For a licensed corporation, senior management includes which of the following?
options: Only the board of directors
Directors, responsible officers and managers-in-charge of core functions ("MICs")
Only the responsible officers approved by the SFC
All licensed representatives of the corporation
explanation: Senior management of a licensed corporation includes directors and responsible officers as well as managers-in-charge of core functions ("MICs"). Limiting senior management to directors only or to responsible officers only omits the other categories expressly mentioned, and ordinary licensed representatives are not part of senior management. (PDF p.248)

question: Ms Wong works at a licensed corporation where she both executes client trades (dealing) and processes the settlement of those trades. Under the ICG, what is the concern with this arrangement?
options: There is no concern, as combining dealing and settlement improves efficiency
Dealing and settlement are incompatible functions which, where practical, should be segregated because combining them may create opportunities for abuse or the overlooking of errors
The arrangement is acceptable provided Ms Wong is a responsible officer
The arrangement only matters if Ms Wong also performs research functions
explanation: The ICG require that, where practical, functions such as sales, dealing, accounting and settlement be segregated from each other, because incompatible duties performed by the same person may provide opportunities for abuse or result in errors being overlooked, exposing the intermediary and its clients to risks. Efficiency does not override this concern, being a responsible officer does not cure it, and the problem exists independently of any research function. (PDF p.250)

question: According to the ICG, why should research functions be segregated from sales and dealing?
options: To reduce staffing costs
To avoid conflicts of interest
To comply with the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance
Because research staff are not permitted to hold licences
explanation: The ICG state that research functions should be segregated from sales and dealing to avoid conflicts of interest, and where practical research should also be segregated from corporate finance. Cost reduction is not the rationale given; the Prevention of Bribery Ordinance is referenced in the compliance area in relation to anti-bribery policies, not segregation; and nothing in the text says research staff cannot hold licences. (PDF p.250)

question: Under the ICG, where possible, compliance and internal audit functions should be:
options: Combined with line operational duties to keep them informed of the business
Separate from and independent of the operational functions, and report directly to senior management
Outsourced to the SFC
Subordinate to the sales and dealing departments
explanation: The ICG state that, where possible, compliance and internal audit should be separate from and independent of the operational functions and report directly to senior management. Combining them with line operational duties is expressly what should be avoided where practicable; the SFC is the regulator and does not take on a firm's compliance work; and making them subordinate to sales and dealing would destroy their independence. (PDF p.250)

question: According to the ICG, the failure to separate incompatible functions has often led to:
options: Manipulation, misappropriation and fraud
Increased staff turnover
Lower commission income
Delays in licence applications
explanation: The ICG note that the failure to separate incompatible functions has often led to manipulation, misappropriation and fraud. Staff turnover, commission income and licensing delays are not the consequences identified in the text. (PDF p.250)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, has just been recruited by a licensed corporation. Under the ICG's personnel and training guidelines, what must the firm provide him?
options: Only an employment contract, since training is his personal responsibility

Adequate training suitable for his specific duties and to meet the continuous professional training ("CPT") requirements, plus comprehensive and up-to-date information on the firm's policies and procedures
A copy of the SFO only
Training only after he has worked at the firm for one year
