Accuracy is a Principle 2 matter and does not authorise new uses, and publishing policies relates to Principle 5's transparency requirement, not consent. (PDF p.284)

question: Which of the following is a data subject entitled to under Principle 6?
options: To inspect the personal data that the data user holds about other clients
To require the data user to destroy his personal data immediately on request
To access his personal data free of charge within 24 hours
To request access to his personal data within a reasonable time, at a reasonable fee and in an intelligible form
explanation: Principle 6 entitles a data subject to ascertain whether his data is held, request access within a reasonable time, at a reasonable fee and in an intelligible form, request corrections, be given reasons for refusals, and object to the Privacy Commissioner. A right to immediate destruction is not stated; retention is governed by Principle 2's no-longer-than-necessary standard. Access may carry a reasonable fee and a reasonable time, not free within 24 hours, and the right covers only data of which he is the subject. (PDF p.284)

question: Mrs Lee discovers her intermediary holds incorrect personal data about her. She asks for it to be corrected and the intermediary refuses. What can she do?
options: Apply to the SEHK for the data to be rectified
Report the matter to the SFC's licensing department
Nothing further, as the data user's decision on corrections is final
Report the matter to the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data
explanation: A data subject is entitled to ask for incorrect data to be corrected, and if this is refused, can report the matter to the Privacy Commissioner for Personal Data, who is the independent public officer enforcing the PDPO. The SFC and SEHK regulate securities matters and are not the recourse stated for PDPO correction refusals. The data user's refusal is not final, since reasons must be given for refusals and an objection lies to the Commissioner. (PDF p.290)

question: Which of the following is among the things senior management must install to promote good compliance practices?
options: Unwritten policies and procedures to preserve flexibility
Distinctions between supervisory and review functions, and operational and line functions
Restricted access for complaints so they are handled only by senior management
A combined Chairman and chief executive officer role to streamline decisions
explanation: Senior management must install distinctions between supervisory and review functions, and operational and line functions, among other items such as good reporting structures and effective communications. Good governance involves separating, not combining, the Chairman and CEO functions. Complaints require open access and prompt, satisfactory handling, and policies, practices and procedures must be well-defined and set out in writing, not unwritten. (PDF p.285)

question: The OECD's core principles of corporate governance practices include which set of concepts?
options: Liquidity, capital adequacy, transparency and disclosure
Integrity, suitability, best execution and fair allocation
Fairness, transparency, accountability and responsibility
Honesty, diligence, competence and solvency
explanation: The OECD has issued core principles of corporate governance comprising fairness, transparency, accountability and responsibility. The other options mix in concepts from conduct of business and prudential regulation — honesty and diligence echo general conduct standards, capital adequacy is a financial resources concept, and suitability and best execution are client-dealing obligations — none of which is the OECD set stated in the text. (PDF p.286)

question: According to the text, corporate governance is primarily concerned with the proper relationship between which parties?
options: A company's management, its board and its shareholders, and possibly also its stakeholders
The SFC and the SEHK
A company and its regulators only
An intermediary and its qualified investor clients
explanation: Corporate governance is primarily concerned with the proper relationship between a company's management, its board and its shareholders, and possibly also its stakeholders such as employees, creditors and customers. Regulators are external agencies with which firms should maintain good relationships, not the core governance relationship. The SFC and SEHK are regulators increasingly concerned with governance, not parties to it, and qualified investors relate to ALP access. (PDF p.286)

question: Which of the following is an example of installing appropriate checks and balances to improve a company's governance?
options: Reducing disclosure to shareholders to protect commercially sensitive information
Allowing executive directors to set their own remuneration
Separating the functions of Chairman and chief executive officer
Combining the audit committee with the operational line functions
explanation: Examples of checks and balances include separating the Chairman and chief executive officer functions, appointing independent non-executive directors, establishing independent audit committees, and setting up committees to control the remuneration and benefits of directors and senior management. Letting executives set their own pay defeats the remuneration committee control, audit committees should be independent, and good governance calls for increasing, not reducing, transparency and disclosure. (PDF p.286)

section: Some effects of deficiencies in corporate governance
question: A listed company transfers one of its assets to a connected person at a price well below its value. The text gives this as an example of what?
options: A proprietary order placed into an ALP
A permitted transaction provided it is disclosed to shareholders
An undervalued connected transaction resulting from deficiencies in corporate governance
An overvalued connected transaction resulting from deficiencies in corporate governance
explanation: Transferring a company asset to a connected person at a low price is the text's example of an undervalued connected transaction, one of the results of deficient corporate governance alongside market misconduct and fraud by directors and managers. An overvalued connected transaction is the reverse case, where the company acquires an asset from a connected person at an excessively high price. Proprietary orders concern ALPs, and the text presents these transactions as effects of governance deficiencies, not permitted practice. (PDF p.287)

question: Under s. 118, SFO, what is a necessary condition for the SFC granting a licence to a corporation?
options: Membership of both the SEHK and the Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited
The appointment of an independent audit committee
Registration with the Inland Revenue Department under the CRS
The maintenance of insurance cover, or the deposit of security with the SFC
explanation: The maintenance of insurance cover by a licensed corporation, or the deposit of security with the SFC, is a necessary condition for the granting of a licence under s. 118, SFO. Exchange participation is relevant only to determining which firms are exempt from the Insurance Rules, not a licensing condition stated here. Audit committees are a corporate governance measure, and CRS due diligence and reporting to the Inland Revenue Department is a separate tax-transparency obligation. (PDF p.288)

question: Under Schedule 2 of the Insurance Rules, what is the insured amount applicable to dealing in securities, and the maximum deductible?
options: HK$3 million insured, with a deductible not exceeding HK$15 million
HK$15 million insured, with a deductible not exceeding HK$5 million
HK$10 million insured, with no deductible permitted
HK$15 million insured, with a deductible not exceeding HK$3 million
explanation: Schedule 2, Insurance Rules sets the insured amount for dealing in securities, dealing in futures contracts or securities margin financing at HK$15 million each, with a deductible amount not exceeding HK$3 million in each case. The HK$3 million insured/HK$15 million deductible option reverses the two figures, HK$5 million is a wrong deductible, and HK$10 million is a wrong insured amount; there is no specified amount for other regulated activities. (PDF p.288)

question: Under the Inland Revenue (Amendment) (No.3) Ordinance 2016 implementing the CRS in Hong Kong, what must licensed corporations and registered institutions do?
options: Undertake due diligence to identify financial accounts held by tax residents of reportable jurisdictions and report to the Inland Revenue Department annually
Withhold tax from accounts held by tax residents of reportable jurisdictions
Report financial accounts of overseas tax residents to the SFC every six months
Report all client accounts to the OECD on a quarterly basis
explanation: The Ordinance requires licensed corporations and registered institutions, among others, to undertake the due diligence necessary to identify financial accounts held by tax residents of reportable jurisdictions and to report to the Inland Revenue Department on an annual basis. The OECD developed the CRS in 2014 but is not the reporting recipient. No tax withholding obligation is stated, and the report goes annually to the Inland Revenue Department, not six-monthly to the SFC. (PDF p.288)

section: Topic 7: Participating in the Hong Kong exchanges - Table of contents
question: According to the table of contents of Topic 7 ("Participating in the Hong Kong exchanges"), which entity is the subject of section 2, covering exchanges, trading systems and clearing houses, participants and mutual market access?
options: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited (SEHK)
Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited
Hong Kong Securities and Investment Institute
explanation: Section 2 of Topic 7 is headed "Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited" and covers exchanges, trading systems and clearing houses, participants and mutual market access. The SEHK is instead the subject of section 3 (dealing in securities listed on the SEHK), and Hong Kong Futures Exchange Limited appears under section 5 (dealing in futures contracts). The Hong Kong Securities and Investment Institute is the publisher named in the page footer, not a section subject. (PDF p.292)

section: Dealing in securities listed on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited ("SEHK")
question: In the Topic 7 table of contents, the headings "Regulated short selling on the SEHK", "Securities borrowing and lending" and "Short position reporting" all fall under which section?
options: Section 2: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
Section 3: Dealing in securities listed on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited ("SEHK")
Section 4: Traded options on the SEHK
Section 5: Dealing in futures contracts
explanation: These three headings are listed under section 3, "Dealing in securities listed on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited (SEHK)", alongside related items such as short-selling requirements additional to those in the Securities and Futures Ordinance. Section 2 covers HKEX and the mutual market access scheme (where only "Short selling via the Northbound trading links" appears), section 4 covers traded options, and section 5 covers futures contracts. (PDF p.292)

section: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
question: Per the Topic 7 table of contents, "Additional restrictions on Northbound trading" and "Short selling via the Northbound trading links" are sub-headings of which section?
options: Section 6: Trading and marketing
Section 3: Dealing in securities listed on the SEHK
Section 2: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
Section 5: Dealing in futures contracts
explanation: Both Northbound trading headings sit within section 2, "Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited", which also covers mutual market access and updates to the mutual market access scheme. Section 3 deals with short selling on the SEHK itself, not the Northbound links; section 5 concerns futures contracts; and section 6 covers trading position limits, reporting requirements and marketing listed structured products. (PDF p.292)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, wants to review the study notes on trading position limits and reporting requirements, as well as the marketing of listed structured products. According to the Topic 7 table of contents, which section should he read?
options: Section 4: Traded options on the SEHK
Section 6: Trading and marketing
Section 5: Dealing in futures contracts
Section 3: Dealing in securities listed on the SEHK
explanation: Section 6, "Trading and marketing", contains both sub-headings: "Trading position limits and reporting requirements" and "Marketing listed structured products". Section 4 covers traded options (trading, clearing participants, clearing and margin, default); section 5 covers dealing in futures contracts including settlement; and section 3 covers dealing in SEHK-listed securities. (PDF p.293)

question: Under the SFO classification of regulated activities, dealing in futures contracts traded on the exchange is:
options: Type 1 regulated activity
Type 2 regulated activity
Type 3 regulated activity
Type 4 regulated activity
explanation: Dealing in futures contracts traded on the exchange is Type 2 regulated activity. Type 1 is wrong because it covers dealing in listed securities and Traded Options, both of which are Type 1 regulated activities. Types 3 and 4 are not mentioned in this Topic at all. (PDF p.296)

section: Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
question: Which body PRINCIPALLY monitors the regulation of intermediaries engaged in trading on the exchanges and their relationships with clients?
options: The HKEX
The SEHK
The SFC directly
The HKMA
explanation: The regulation of intermediaries trading on the exchanges, and their relationships with each other and their clients, is principally monitored by the SFC directly. The HKEX is wrong because it regulates listed companies, trading on the exchanges and related clearing and settlement functions, not intermediaries' conduct. The SEHK is one of the exchanges controlled by the HKEX, and the HKMA is only mentioned in this chunk as the regulator of registered institutions participating in the Stock Connects. (PDF p.297)

section: Exchanges, trading systems and clearing houses
question: The SEHK and the HKFE were independent exchanges until they were made subsidiaries of the HKEX under a scheme of arrangement. When did this happen?
options: 6 March 2000
6 March 2003
When the Stock Connects were launched
explanation: The two exchanges were independent until 6 March 2000, when they became subsidiaries of the HKEX under a scheme of arrangement. 2013 is wrong as that is the year OTC Clear started operation. 6 March 2003 is a plausible-sounding but incorrect date, and the Stock Connects launch is unrelated to the corporate restructuring of the exchanges. (PDF p.297)

question: Which of the following products is processed through OTP-C, the trading system currently used by the SEHK?
options: Futures contracts traded on the HKFE
Traded Options of the SEHK
Callable bull/bear contracts
OTC derivatives transactions
explanation: OTP-C processes products traded through the facilities of the HKEX including stocks, derivative warrants, bonds, exchange traded products and callable bull/bear contracts. HKFE futures and Traded Options are wrong because both are traded on HKATS (with the Genium INET platform), not OTP-C. OTC derivatives are cleared by OTC Clear and are not exchange-traded products processed by OTP-C. (PDF p.297)

question: Traded Options are products of the SEHK. On which system are they traded?
options: OTP-C, because they are SEHK products
HKATS, with the Genium INET platform
DCASS, the common derivatives platform
CCASS, operated by the HKSCC
explanation: The Hong Kong Futures Automated Trading System (HKATS), with a platform called Genium INET, is used for trading HKFE products as well as Traded Options, even though Traded Options are products of the SEHK. OTP-C is wrong because it is the SEHK's system for the securities market (stocks, warrants, bonds, ETPs, CBBCs), not Traded Options. DCASS and CCASS are wrong because they are clearing and settlement systems, not trading systems. (PDF p.298)

question: Which clearing house clears the options business of the SEHK?
options: The HKSCC
The SEOCH
The HKCC
OTC Clear
explanation: The SEHK Options Clearing House Limited (SEOCH), a wholly owned subsidiary of the HKEX, clears the options business of the SEHK. The HKSCC is wrong because it is the clearing house for the cash business and securities settlement of the SEHK. The HKCC clears the futures and options business of the HKFE, and OTC Clear clears and settles OTC derivatives transactions. (PDF p.298)

question: Which of the following statements about OTC Clear is CORRECT?
options: It is a wholly owned subsidiary of the HKEX and started operation in 2013
It is a majority-owned subsidiary of the HKEX and started operation in 2013
It is a majority-owned subsidiary of the HKEX and started operation in 2000
It clears the futures and options business of the HKFE
explanation: OTC Clear is a majority-owned subsidiary of the HKEX that clears and settles OTC derivatives transactions, and it started operation in 2013. The first option is wrong because, unlike the HKSCC, SEOCH and HKCC, which are wholly owned, OTC Clear is only majority-owned. The year 2000 is wrong as that is when the SEHK and HKFE became HKEX subsidiaries, and the futures and options business of the HKFE is cleared by the HKCC. (PDF p.298)

question: The Derivatives Clearing and Settlement System (DCASS) is a common platform for clearing all derivatives products traded on the HKEX. Who operates DCASS for the clearing of Traded Options?
options: The HKSCC
The HKCC
The SEOCH
The SEHK
explanation: DCASS is operated by the SEOCH for the clearing of Traded Options, and by the HKCC for clearing of futures contracts and option contracts traded on the HKFE. The HKSCC is wrong because it operates CCASS for clearing and settlement of securities transactions on the SEHK. The SEHK is an exchange, not a clearing house, and does not operate DCASS. (PDF p.298)

question: Which of the following statements about trading rights is CORRECT?
options: They are transferable and available from the SEHK for a fee of HK$500,000
They are non-transferable and available from the SEHK for a fee currently set at HK$500,000
They are non-transferable and available from the SFC for a fee of HK$500,000
They are non-transferable and available from the SEHK for a fee of HK$5,000,000
explanation: Trading rights are non-transferable and are only available from the SEHK for a fee, currently set at HK$500,000. The first option is wrong because trading rights cannot be transferred. The third option is wrong because trading rights are obtained from the SEHK, not the SFC (the SFC issues licences for regulated activities), and HK$5,000,000 is an incorrect figure. (PDF p.299)

question: How often must participants provide the HKEX with a form of attestation that they have complied with the relevant rules and regulations applicable to their participant activities?
options: Monthly
Quarterly
Annually
Only upon admission as a participant
explanation: Participants are required to provide the HKEX with a form of attestation on an annual basis, which the HKEX uses with the participant's compliance history and activity levels to assess its risk profile and decide whether further investigation or an on-site inspection is needed. Monthly and quarterly are wrong frequencies not stated in the text. The attestation is an ongoing annual obligation, not a one-off requirement on admission. (PDF p.299)

question: Under the Stock Connects, the 'Northbound trading links' allow:
options: Investors in the Chinese Mainland to trade eligible securities listed on the SEHK
Investors in Hong Kong to trade eligible securities listed on the SSE and the SZSE
Investors in Hong Kong to trade eligible securities listed on the SEHK
Investors in the Chinese Mainland to trade eligible securities listed on the SSE and the SZSE
explanation: Northbound trading links allow investors in Hong Kong to trade eligible securities listed on the Shanghai Stock Exchange (SSE) and the Shenzhen Stock Exchange (SZSE). The first option describes the Southbound trading links, the reverse direction. The third and fourth options are wrong because each describes investors trading on their own home market, which is ordinary trading and not what the Stock Connect links provide. (PDF p.299)

question: The SEHK considers it necessary, for ensuring a fair and orderly market, to suspend trading via the Stock Connects in respect of a specific security. What must happen before the suspension can take effect?
options: The CSRC must give its prior approval
The SFC must give its prior approval
The HKEX board must give its prior approval
Nothing; the SEHK may suspend trading at any time without approval
explanation: Although either of the relevant exchanges may suspend trading via the Stock Connects at any time, generally or for specific securities, any such suspension is subject to the prior approval of the relevant regulator: the SFC in respect of the SEHK, and the CSRC in respect of the SSE/SZSE. The CSRC option is wrong because the CSRC approves suspensions by the SSE/SZSE, not the SEHK. HKEX board approval is not the stated requirement, and the last option ignores the prior-approval condition entirely. (PDF p.300)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, receives an order from a Hong Kong retail client to buy shares listed on the ChiNext Board of the SZSE via the Northbound trading link. The client is able to trade on the SEHK but is not an institutional professional investor. What should Mr Chan advise?
options: The client may trade ChiNext shares because anyone able to trade on the SEHK can trade all eligible Northbound securities
The client may not trade ChiNext shares, because only institutional professional investors are permitted to trade eligible securities listed on the ChiNext Board of SZSE and the STAR Market of SSE
The client may trade ChiNext shares only if he holds at least RMB500,000 in his securities and cash accounts
The client may not trade ChiNext shares because retail investors are prohibited from all Northbound trading
explanation: Only institutional professional investors are permitted to trade eligible securities listed on the ChiNext Board of the SZSE and the Sci-Tech Innovation Board (STAR Market) of the SSE; this is one of the two main exceptions to the general position. The first option ignores that exception. The RMB500,000 threshold is wrong because it is the eligibility criterion for Mainland individual investors trading Southbound, not for ChiNext access. The last option overstates the restriction, as persons able to trade on the SEHK can generally trade other eligible Northbound securities. (PDF p.300)

question: A Mainland individual investor wishes to buy SEHK-listed stocks via the Southbound trading links. Under the present eligibility criteria, what must the investor hold?
options: At least RMB500,000 in his securities and cash accounts
At least RMB5 billion in his securities and cash accounts
At least HKD550 million in his securities and cash accounts
At least RMB30 million in his securities and cash accounts
explanation: The only Mainland investors able to participate in Southbound trading are institutional investors and individual investors meeting the eligibility criteria, presently that an individual must hold at least RMB500,000 in his securities and cash accounts. RMB5 billion is the minimum daily average market capitalisation criterion for SSE A share index constituents, RMB30 million is the daily average turnover criterion for those shares, and HKD550 million is the minimum daily average AUM for an SEHK-listed ETF, so all three are figures from nearby eligibility rules, not the investor threshold. (PDF p.301)

question: Which of the following persons is NOT regarded as a Mainland investor for the purposes of the Stock Connects?
options: An individual who possesses a Mainland Resident Identity Card
A holder of a joint account where one of the holders is considered a Mainland investor
An unincorporated entity registered in the Mainland
A subsidiary of a Mainland-registered corporate entity which is lawfully registered in Hong Kong
explanation: A branch or subsidiary of a corporate or unincorporated entity registered in the Chinese Mainland is not regarded as a Mainland investor where that branch or subsidiary is lawfully registered in Hong Kong or overseas. The other three options are all expressly listed as persons regarded as Mainland investors: individuals possessing Mainland ID documents, joint account holders where one holder is a Mainland investor, and corporated and unincorporated entities registered in the Mainland. (PDF p.301)

question: Which of the following securities is NOT included in the Stock Connects?
options: Listed A shares that are constituent stocks of the SSE A Share Index meeting all eligibility criteria
B shares and bonds
Eligible exchange traded funds (ETFs)
SSE-listed A shares with corresponding H shares listed on the SEHK
explanation: Securities not included in the Stock Connects include other listed securities such as B shares and bonds. Eligible securities are listed shares and ETFs, so the third option is wrong. Qualifying SSE A Share Index constituents and SSE-listed A shares with corresponding H shares on the SEHK are both eligible for Northbound trading via the Shanghai Connect. (PDF p.301)

section: Northbound trading via the Shanghai Connect
question: For a constituent stock of the SSE A Share Index to be eligible for Northbound trading via the Shanghai Connect, what minimum daily average market capitalisation must it have had in the last six months?
options: RMB 500 million
RMB 5 billion
RMB 30 million
RMB 52 billion
explanation: The stock must have a daily average market capitalisation of at least RMB 5 billion in the last six months, along with a daily average turnover of at least RMB 30 million in the last six months and not having been suspended on 50% or more of trading days in the last six months. RMB 500 million is the minimum daily average AUM for SSE-listed ETFs, RMB 30 million is the turnover criterion rather than the market capitalisation criterion, and RMB 52 billion is the Northbound daily trading quota. (PDF p.302)

question: A constituent stock of the Hang Seng Composite SmallCap Index is eligible for Southbound trading only if it has a market capitalisation of:
options: HKD 5 billion or above
HKD 550 million or above
RMB 5 billion or above
HKD 500,000 or above
explanation: Eligible SEHK-listed stocks for Southbound trading include constituent stocks of the Hang Seng Composite SmallCap Index with a market capitalisation of HKD 5 billion or above; LargeCap and MidCap constituents carry no such stated threshold. HKD 550 million is the minimum daily average AUM for an eligible SEHK-listed ETF, RMB 5 billion is the market capitalisation criterion for SSE A Share Index constituents on the Northbound side, and HK$500,000 is the fee for a trading right. (PDF p.303)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative at a CCEP, learns mid-morning that the Northbound daily quota for the Shanghai Connect has been fully used. His client holds SSE-listed A shares bought via the Stock Connect and wants to sell them, and also wants to buy more A shares. What is the position?
options: Both the sell and the buy orders are blocked until the next trading day

The sell order may proceed, because investors are always allowed to sell cross-border securities irrespective of the daily quota balance, but the buy order is subject to there being available daily quota
The buy order may proceed, but the sell order is blocked
Both orders may proceed because the quota only applies to ETFs
explanation: The daily quota is calculated on a net buy basis: investors are always allowed to sell their cross-border securities irrespective of the daily quota balance, while buy trades are subject to there being available daily quota, and sell trades effectively increase the available quota. The first and third options reverse or overstate the restriction. The last option is wrong because trading of A shares and ETFs shares the same daily quota; the quota is not limited to ETFs. (PDF p.304)

question: Under the standard pre-trade check for Northbound trades, by when must eligible securities be deposited with the exchange participant acting as the selling broker?
options: Not later than the end of the trading day prior to the intended date of sale
By the commencement of trading on the intended date of sale
At any time before the sell order is executed and matched
Not later than the end of the intended date of sale
explanation: Under the standard pre-trade check, eligible securities can only be sold via the Northbound trading links if deposited with the selling broker not later than the end of the trading day prior to the intended date of sale. Commencement-of-day timing applies to borrowed securities for short selling, not the standard check. Transfer after execution and matching is only possible under the enhanced (SPSA) model, and end of the sale day itself would be too late. (PDF p.306)

question: Under the enhanced pre-trade checking model using a special segregated account (SPSA), when do shares need to be transferred to the investor's account maintained with the exchange participant (selling broker)?
options: Before the sell order is placed
Only after the sell order has been executed and matched
By the end of the trading day prior to the intended date of sale
No transfer to the exchange participant is ever required
explanation: With an SPSA, the CCASS takes a snapshot of the investor's holding and the order can be executed if there are sufficient shares in the SPSA, so shares only need to be transferred to the account with the exchange participant after the sell order is executed and matched. Transfer before the order is placed is what the standard pre-trade check requires. End of the prior trading day is the standard-check deposit deadline, and a transfer is still required after execution, just not beforehand. (PDF p.306)

section: Investor identification regime for Northbound trading
question: In order to input a Northbound order on a trading day (T day) under Stock Connect, by when must a CCEP send the BCAN-to-client identification mapping information file to SEHK?
options: On or before T-1 day, and the file must have passed SEHK's validation
Before market open on T day
On or before T-2 day
Together with each Northbound order as it is input
explanation: The CCEP must send the mapping information file to SEHK on or before T-1 day and the file must have passed SEHK's validation. Sending it on T day before market open or on T-2 day are not the stated deadlines. What accompanies each order is the corresponding BCAN itself, not the mapping file. (PDF p.306)

question: If ChinaClear defaults in relation to a Northbound trade, what will the HKSCC do?
options: Use the Guarantee Fund contributions of CCCPs to offset the close-out loss
Seek recovery from ChinaClear through available legal channels or its liquidation process, and distribute recovered stocks and monies to CCCPs on a pro-rata basis
Compensate CCCPs in full from its own resources
Require ChinaClear to contribute to the HKSCC Guarantee Fund to cover the loss
explanation: On a ChinaClear default, the HKSCC would in good faith seek recovery of outstanding stocks and monies through available legal channels or ChinaClear's liquidation process, then distribute what is recovered to CCCPs pro-rata. The Guarantee Fund contributions of CCCPs will not be utilised to offset close-out loss in the event of ChinaClear's default. ChinaClear is not required to contribute to the HKSCC Guarantee Fund, and nothing in the text says HKSCC compensates CCCPs in full. (PDF p.307)

question: For shares eligible for margin trading under the Northbound trading links, at what level of the "margin trading indicator" will the SSE/SZSE suspend further margin trading, and below what level will it allow margin trading to resume?
options: Suspend at 25%; resume below 20%
Suspend at 75%; resume below 70%
Suspend at 30%; resume below 25%
Suspend at 20%; resume below 15%
explanation: For shares, the SSE/SZSE suspends further margin trading after the margin trading indicator reaches 25% and allows it to resume when the indicator drops below 20%. The 75%/70% thresholds apply to ETFs (based on the margin balance and collateral value against the ETF's listed and tradable market capitalisation). 30% is the aggregate foreign ownership limit, and 20%/15% are not figures stated in the text. (PDF p.308)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, has a client who intends to sell Northbound trading link eligible securities but cannot transfer a sufficient quantity to the selling broker in time. The client borrows the securities under an SBL agreement to meet the pre-trade checking requirements. What is the maximum permitted period of this SBL agreement?
options: One day, and roll-over is not allowed
One calendar month
Ten consecutive trading days
One day, but it may be rolled over once
explanation: An SBL agreement entered into for meeting pre-trade checking requirements cannot be for a period longer than one day, and roll-over is not allowed. One calendar month is the maximum for SBL used for covered short selling, not pre-trade checking. Ten consecutive trading days relates to the 5% cumulative short selling limit, and roll-over is expressly prohibited. (PDF p.308)

question: What limits does the Chinese Mainland impose on foreign investment in a listed company?
options: No single foreign investor may own more than 10% of the issued shares, and total foreign ownership cannot exceed 30%; ETFs listed on the SSE or SZSE are not subject to these restrictions
No single foreign investor may own more than 30% of the issued shares, and total foreign ownership cannot exceed 10%; ETFs are also subject to these restrictions
No single foreign investor may own more than 5% of the issued shares, and total foreign ownership cannot exceed 25%
No single foreign investor may own more than 10% of the issued shares, and total foreign ownership cannot exceed 30%; the same limits apply to ETFs listed on the SSE or SZSE
explanation: No single foreign investor can own more than 10% of a listed company's issued shares and total foreign ownership cannot exceed 30%, and ETFs listed on the SSE or SZSE are not subject to shareholding restrictions. The second option swaps the two percentages and wrongly includes ETFs. 5% is the separate Mainland disclosure threshold and 25% is the margin trading indicator suspension level, not ownership limits. The last option states the correct percentages but wrongly extends the limits to ETFs. (PDF p.308)

section: Short selling via the Northbound trading links
question: For short selling via the Northbound trading links, what quantity restrictions apply to the number of shares that may be short sold in a security?
options: Not more than 1% of the shares of that security held by all investors through the CCASS at the commencement of the trading day, and not more than 5% cumulatively in any period of ten consecutive trading days
Not more than 5% of the shares of that security held through the CCASS per day, and not more than 10% cumulatively in any period of ten consecutive trading days
Not more than 1% of the security's listed and tradable shares per day, with no cumulative limit
Not more than 25% of the security's listed and tradable shares per day, falling to 20% on resumption
explanation: The daily limit is 1% of the shares of that short selling security held by all investors through the CCASS at the commencement of the trading day, with a cumulative cap of 5% in any period of ten consecutive trading days. The 5%/10% combination is wrong, and a cumulative limit does exist. The 25%/20% figures are the thresholds at which SSE/SZSE may suspend and resume short selling activities in an A share, not per-day order limits. (PDF p.309)

section: Short selling via the Northbound trading links
question: Ms Wong, a Hong Kong investor, asks her broker to place a covered short selling order via the Northbound trading link on an ETF listed on the SSE. How should the broker respond?
options: The order cannot be placed, because short selling is not allowed for ETFs
The order can be placed, provided the ETF is on the list of securities specified by the SSE
The order can be placed, provided it does not exceed 1% of the ETF units held through the CCASS that day
The order can be placed, provided the margin trading indicator for the ETF is below 75%
explanation: Covered short selling via the Northbound trading links is permitted only on securities specified by the SSE or SZSE, and short selling is not allowed for ETFs. The specified-securities condition and the 1% daily limit apply to eligible securities, but ETFs are excluded from short selling altogether. The 75% threshold relates to suspension of ETF margin trading, which is a separate restriction. (PDF p.309)

section: Updates to the mutual market access scheme
question: When was the Northbound channel of Swap Connect launched, and what does it allow international investors to do?
options: May 2023; trade and clear onshore RMB interest rate swaps without changing their existing trading and settlement practices
July 2017; invest in the Mainland bond market
September 2021; invest in the Hong Kong bond market
May 2023; trade equity index swaps listed on the SSE and SZSE
explanation: The Northbound channel of Swap Connect was launched in May 2023 and allows international investors to trade and clear onshore RMB interest rate swaps without changing their existing trading and settlement practices. July 2017 is when Northbound trading of Bond Connect commenced, and September 2021 is when Bond Connect's Southbound trading began. Swap Connect concerns interbank interest rate swaps, not equity index swaps. (PDF p.310)

question: Which of the following statements about an Exchange Participant of the SEHK is correct?
options: It must be a company and shall at all times have at least one executive director registered as a responsible officer
It may be a company or a partnership, provided it meets the capital requirements
It must at all times have at least two executive directors registered as responsible officers
Its responsible officers approved by the SFC need not be registered with the SEHK
explanation: An Exchange Participant must be a company and shall at all times have at least one executive director registered as a responsible officer. Partnerships are not permitted, and the minimum is one executive director, not two. Unless otherwise determined by the SEHK, all licensed representatives approved by the SFC as responsible officers in relation to relevant regulated activities shall be registered with the SEHK as responsible officers. (PDF p.311)

question: For full day trading on the SEHK securities market, when does the closing auction session (CAS) end?
options: At a random closing time between 16:08 and 16:10
Exactly at 16:10
At a random closing time between 12:08 and 12:10
Exactly at 16:00
explanation: For full day trading, the closing time is a random closing at any time between 16:08 and 16:10. It is not a fixed close at 16:10, so the random element matters. The 12:08-12:10 random closing applies to half day trading, and 16:00 is when the afternoon continuous session ends and the CAS begins. (PDF p.312)

question: How is the closing price of a stock on the SEHK determined?
options: By taking the median of five nominal prices in the last minute of the continuous trading session
By taking the average of five nominal prices in the last minute of the continuous trading session
By taking the last traded price of the day
By taking the median of five nominal prices during the closing auction session
explanation: The closing price is determined by taking the median of five nominal prices in the last minute of the continuous trading session (CTS). It is a median, not an average, and is not simply the last traded price. The five nominal prices are taken during the CTS (the morning, extended morning and afternoon sessions), not during the CAS. (PDF p.312)

section: Volatility control mechanism (VCM) applicable during CTS
question: A VCM stock attempts to trade at a price beyond the specified triggering threshold away from its last traded price five minutes ago. What happens?
options: A five-minute cooling-off period applies to that instrument, during which trading is still allowed but within certain price limits
Trading in that instrument is completely suspended for five minutes
Trading in that instrument is halted for the remainder of the trading day
A cooling-off period applies, and the VCM can only be triggered once per CTS for that stock
explanation: Triggering the VCM starts a five-minute cooling-off period on the particular instrument, during which the instrument is still allowed to trade but within certain price limits, and normal continuous trading resumes when the period ends. Trading is not completely suspended and is not halted for the rest of the day. There are multiple triggers per CTS for each VCM stock, so a once-per-session limit is wrong. (PDF p.313)

question: Mr Chan's firm, an Exchange Participant, acts for both the buyer and the seller in a transaction executed through an automated trading services facility as defined in the SFO. Within what time must the details of this direct business transaction be inputted?
options: Within one minute of the conclusion of the transaction
Within 15 minutes of the conclusion of the transaction
Within five minutes of the conclusion of the transaction
Before the end of the trading day
explanation: Direct business executed, concluded or entered into through an automated trading services facility as defined in the SFO must have its details inputted within one minute of the conclusion of the transaction. The 15-minute limit applies to all other direct business transactions, not to those through automated trading services. Five minutes and end-of-day are not deadlines stated in the rules. (PDF p.314)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, executes a purchase of ETF units listed on the SEHK for a client. Which of the following charges does NOT apply to this transaction?
options: Stamp duty of 0.1% on the value of the transaction
SFC Transaction Levy of 0.0027%
AFRC Transaction Levy of 0.00015%
Trading Fee of 0.00565% of the consideration
explanation: Stamp duty of 0.1% is levied on each stock transaction on both buyer and seller, but there is no stamp duty on ETFs, derivative warrants and callable bull/bear contracts trading. The SFC Transaction Levy (0.0027%) and AFRC Transaction Levy (0.00015%) are charged on each purchase and sale and paid by both buyer and seller. The Trading Fee of 0.00565% per side is payable to the SEHK, subject only to exemptions for Securities Market Maker transactions. (PDF p.315)

question: Under s. 170, SFO, a short sale made where the seller does NOT have (and does not believe he or his principal has) a presently exercisable and unconditional right to vest the securities in the buyer is known as what, and what is its status?
options: A naked short sale; contravention of the short selling prohibition is an offence
A covered short sale; it is a permitted short sale
A regulated short sale; it is permitted if reported to the SEHK
A naked short sale; it is permitted provided it is indicated to the counterparty
explanation: Where the s. 170 conditions are not satisfied, the sale is called a naked short sale, and contravention of the short selling prohibition is an offence. A covered short sale is the opposite: a sale that does satisfy the conditions and is permitted. Regulated short selling is a separately defined term in Schedule 1, SFO and the Eleventh Schedule of the Rules of the Exchange, and indicating the order to the counterparty does not legitimise a naked short sale. (PDF p.316)

question: Which of the following correctly states a practical requirement for short selling on the SEHK?
options: Exchange Participants must make stock borrowing arrangements for settlement before the short sale
Short selling may be undertaken in any security listed on the SEHK
Short sell orders need not be specially indicated when inputting the orders to the trading system
Only a small number of securities are currently designated by the SEHK for short selling
explanation: Exchange Participants must make stock borrowing arrangements for settlement before the short sale. Short selling may be undertaken only in securities designated by the SEHK (Designated Securities), not in any listed security, and there is currently a large number of Designated Securities, not a small number. Exchange Participants must indicate short sell orders when inputting them to the trading system. (PDF p.317)

question: An Exchange Participant engaged in securities borrowing and lending must collect collateral of at least what percentage of the value of the securities loaned, where the borrowing is for short selling?
explanation: Where the borrowing is for short selling, collateral of at least 105% of the value of the securities loaned must be collected. 100% is the requirement for SBL that is NOT for short selling, so it is the natural trap. 110% and 120% are not figures stated anywhere in the text. (PDF p.318)

question: Exchange Participants must maintain ledgers recording stock borrowing and lending transactions and make them available to which body, so it can satisfy itself that the stamp duty exemption is properly applied?
options: The Securities and Futures Commission
The Inland Revenue Department
The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong
The Hong Kong Monetary Authority
explanation: The ledgers must be made available to the Inland Revenue Department, which checks that the stamp duty exemption requirements for genuine SBL transactions are properly applied. The SFC and SEHK regulate other aspects of SBL and short selling but are not the body named for the stamp duty check, and the HKMA is not mentioned in this context at all. (PDF p.318)

question: Under the Short Position Reporting Rules, what is the reporting threshold for specified shares which are interests in a collective investment scheme (CIS)?
options: A net short position value equal to or more than HK$30 million
The lower of HK$30 million and 0.02% of the value of the total shares issued
A net short position value equal to or more than 0.02% of the total shares issued
The higher of HK$30 million and 0.02% of the value of the total shares issued
explanation: For specified shares which are interests in a CIS, the threshold is simply a net short position value of HK$30 million or more. The 'lower of HK$30 million and 0.02%' test applies to specified shares OTHER than CIS interests, making option (b) the key trap. Option (c) is only half of the non-CIS test, and a 'higher of' test appears nowhere in the rules. (PDF p.319)

question: Under the normal (weekly) regime of the Short Position Reporting Rules, a reportable short position must be reported to the SFC within what period after the reporting day?
options: One business day
Two business days
Three business days
One week
explanation: The report must be made to the SFC within two business days after the reporting day, which is normally the last trading day of each week. One business day is the deadline only under the daily reporting regime triggered by an SFC notice, so it is the obvious confusion. Three business days and one week are not periods stated in the rules. (PDF p.319)

question: Believing that circumstances threatening the financial stability of Hong Kong exist, the SFC publishes a daily reporting requirement notice. Ms Wong, a market participant, holds a reportable short position in shares covered by the notice at the close of trading on Tuesday. By when must she report it to the SFC?
options: Within one business day after Tuesday
Within two business days after Tuesday
By the last trading day of that week
Within one hour after notification
explanation: When the SFC publishes a daily reporting requirement notice, each trading day on which the person holds a reportable short position becomes a reporting day, and the report must be made within ONE business day after it. Two business days is the deadline under the normal weekly regime. The last trading day of the week is the normal reporting day, not a deadline, and the one-hour limit relates to SEOCH intra-day margin calls, not short position reporting. (PDF p.319)

question: When are Final Clearing Statements made available to CCASS Clearing Participants for confirmation of settlement?
options: Shortly after 5:00pm on the trade day (T)
Shortly after 8:00pm on the trade day (T)
Shortly after 2:00pm on T+1
Shortly after 2:00pm on T+2
explanation: Final Clearing Statements are available shortly after 2:00pm on T+1. The 5:00pm and 8:00pm times on T relate to the PROVISIONAL Clearing Statements (for the day's SEHK trades, and for SEHK trades plus exercised options trades respectively). T+2 is the securities settlement day, not when the Final Clearing Statement is issued. (PDF p.320)

question: Under the Continuous Net Settlement system, how does the HKSCC guarantee settlement of SEHK trades?
options: By collecting collateral of at least 105% of the value of every trade
Through novation, acting as the settlement counterparty to CCASS Clearing Participants and assuming the settlement risk
By requiring payment before delivery of securities in all cases
By executing a compulsory buy-in on the trade day for every unsettled position
explanation: Through the legal process of novation, the HKSCC becomes the settlement counterparty to CCASS Clearing Participants and assumes the settlement risk. The 105% collateral figure relates to securities borrowing for short selling, not the HKSCC guarantee. Money settlement is on a Delivery Versus Payment basis (money settled after stock), not payment-first, and a compulsory buy-in occurs on T+3 only when a participant has insufficient stock, not for every position on the trade day. (PDF p.320)

question: In CCASS, which account number is assigned to the Stock Collateral Control Account of a CCASS Clearing Participant?
options: No. 01
No. 02
No. 20
No. 21
explanation: The Stock Collateral Control Account is assigned account number 20. No. 01 is the clearing account where stocks are maintained for settlement, No. 02 handles stock dividends, and numbers from 21 onwards are assigned to Stock Segregated Accounts with Statement Service. (PDF p.321)

question: Broker A, a CCASS Clearing Participant, does not have sufficient stock in its CCASS stock account to settle its stock position by the end of T+2. What happens next?
options: The HKSCC executes a compulsory buy-in on T+3, with Broker A bearing the cost of the shares purchased plus a default fee
The HKSCC executes a compulsory buy-in on T+2 at no cost to Broker A
Broker A's exchange participantship is automatically suspended
The HKSCC absorbs the cost of closing out the position from its reserve funds
explanation: If a CCASS Clearing Participant lacks sufficient stock by the end of T+2, the HKSCC executes a compulsory buy-in on T+3, and the short participant is responsible for the cost of the shares purchased and is subject to a default fee. The buy-in is on T+3, not T+2, and is never cost-free to the participant. Suspension of participantship is an SEHK disciplinary action for conduct breaches, not the automatic consequence here, and the HKSCC does not absorb the cost. (PDF p.321)

question: Mr Chan's firm, a CCASS Clearing Participant, has received stock on T+2 but its payment has not yet been confirmed, so the securities are on hold. What can the firm do to make immediate use of the securities?
options: Give an Account Transfer Instruction to move the stock to a designated account
Make a cash prepayment to the HKSCC
Wait until the Final Clearing Statement is issued on T+3
Pledge other securities as collateral with the SEHK
explanation: Securities for which payment has not been confirmed are put on hold, but a CCASS Clearing Participant can make a cash prepayment to the HKSCC to make immediate use of them. An ATI only moves stock between the participant's own CCASS accounts and does not release the hold. Final Clearing Statements are issued shortly after 2:00pm on T+1, not T+3, and pledging collateral with the SEHK is not a mechanism stated in the text. (PDF p.322)

question: Under the planned uncertificated securities market (USM), what is the key change in the nature of an investor's interest in securities?
options: Investors will hold beneficial interest through CCASS as nominee
Investors will be able to hold legal title in their own name as registered shareholders, with a direct relationship with the issuer
Investors will receive paper certificates in their own name
Investors will hold securities only through a designated bank approved by the HKSCC
explanation: The USM dematerialises securities so investors can hold them in their own name, holding legal title as registered shareholders with a direct relationship with the issuer and legal protection. Holding only beneficial interest via a nominee such as CCASS is the position under the CURRENT paper-based system, which the USM replaces. Paper documents are being replaced by digitised holdings, not new certificates, and approved banks relate to CCASS money settlement, not USM holdings. (PDF p.322)

question: Which of the following disciplinary matters falls within the responsibility of the SEHK rather than the SFC?
options: Breaches of the SFO
Breaches of statutory rules and regulations
Breaches of codes of conduct
Trading, clearing and settlement offences and breaches of the Listing Rules
explanation: The SEHK's responsibility is limited to disciplinary matters outside the SFC's purview: specifically trading, clearing and settlement offences and breaches of the Listing Rules. The SFC, as front-line regulator of intermediaries, handles all disciplinary matters relating to breaches of the SFO, the statutory rules, regulations and codes of conduct, which is why the other three options are wrong. (PDF p.323)

section: Traded options on the SEHK (Type 1 regulated activity)
question: Traded Options on the SEHK are American-style options. What does this mean?
options: They are exercisable at any time during their outstanding life
They are exercisable only on the expiry date
They give the holder an obligation, not a right, to buy or sell the underlying
They are written only on stocks listed on US exchanges
explanation: American-style options are exercisable at any time during their outstanding life, i.e. on or before the specified date. Exercisability only on the expiry date describes European-style options, the direct contrast given in the text. Both call and put options give the holder a right but NOT an obligation, and Traded Options are written on actively traded stocks listed on the SEHK, not US exchanges. (PDF p.324)

question: An HKFE Participant which is not a participant of the HKCC (a non-clearing participant) enters into futures contracts. How must those contracts be cleared?
options: It may record, register and clear the contracts itself under the HKCC Rules
It must arrange for them to be recorded, registered and cleared on its behalf by a General Clearing Participant
It must arrange for them to be cleared by a Clearing Participant
They are cleared automatically by the HKSCC through novation
explanation: A non-clearing participant is not entitled to record, register and clear contracts itself; it must arrange for a General Clearing Participant to do so on its behalf in accordance with the HKCC Rules. A Clearing Participant may, at the clearing house's discretion, clear for OTHER Clearing Participants, but not for non-clearing participants. The HKSCC clears SEHK securities trades, not HKFE futures contracts. (PDF p.329)

question: Which of the following correctly describes an Options Broker Exchange Participant of the SEHK?
options: It must hold system access rights to the options system

It may not have system access rights, but may conduct Traded Options business for clients by entering into options broking client contracts with an Options Trading Exchange Participant, acting as principal
It may clear options trades directly with the SEOCH
It may act as a market maker in a particular option class
explanation: An Options Broker Exchange Participant is not permitted to have system access rights; it conducts Traded Options business for clients through options broking client contracts with an Options Trading Exchange Participant and corresponding client contracts with its clients, in each case acting as principal. Holding system access rights is required of Options TRADING Exchange Participants. Only Options Trading Exchange Participants may become SEOCH clearing participants, and it is an Options Trading Exchange Participant that may apply to make a market in an option class. (PDF p.325)

question: Money settlement of the SEOCH's daily mark-to-market margin, conducted by settlement banks through the Direct Margin Debiting System, should be confirmed by what time?
options: Before 9:15am, so all open positions are fully margined before the markets open
Before 2:00pm on T+1
Within one hour after notification
By 5:00pm on the trade day
explanation: Money settlement by settlement banks should be confirmed before 9:15am, ensuring all open positions are fully margined before the markets open. 2:00pm on T+1 is when CCASS Final Clearing Statements become available, the one-hour deadline applies to SEOCH INTRA-DAY margin calls, and 5:00pm on the trade day is when CCASS Provisional Clearing Statements are received. (PDF p.326)

question: During a highly volatile morning, the margin interval of one option class suffers a 50% margin erosion and the SEOCH notifies Mr Chan's firm, a SEOCH Clearing Participant, of an intra-day margin call at 11:00am. By when must the call be met?
options: By 12:00 noon, i.e. within one hour after notification
Before 9:15am the next business day
Within two business days
By the close of trading on the same day
explanation: All intra-day margin calls must be met by SEOCH Clearing Participants within one hour after notification, so a call notified at 11:00am must be met by 12:00 noon. A 50% margin erosion in the margin interval of an option class is indeed what automatically triggers the call. The 9:15am deadline applies to the routine DAILY mark-to-market margin, two business days is the short position reporting deadline, and the whole point of intra-day calls is that the SEOCH need not wait until later in the day or next-day settlement. (PDF p.327)

section: Dealing in futures contracts (Type 2 regulated activity)
question: A firm wishes to become an HKFE Participant able to trade futures contracts on its own account, for the account of other HKFE Participants and for the account of any other persons. Which category of HKFE participantship under Rule 312 of the HKFE Rules permits this?
options: Trader
Broker
Futures Commission Merchant
Merchant Trader
explanation: A Futures Commission Merchant may trade on its own account, for the account of other HKFE Participants and for the account of any other persons. A Trader may trade on his own account only. A Broker may trade on his own account and act as agent of an HKFE Participant solely for concluding trades, but not for any other persons generally. A Merchant Trader may trade only on its own account and only ancillary to its principal business or that of its holding company. (PDF p.328)

question: When the HKCC accepts an HKATS transaction for clearing, by what legal process does it become the buyer to each seller and the seller to each buyer?
options: Novation
Assignment
Close-out
Mark-to-market
explanation: The HKCC takes over the role of counterparty to each transaction by the legal process of novation. Assignment is not the term used in the source. Close-out is an equal and opposite transaction effected via DCASS to remove liability from the opening trade, not the process of becoming counterparty. Mark-to-market is the system used for variation adjustments, not for substituting counterparties. (PDF p.330)

question: Which statement best describes the margin levels set by the HKFE for products traded through its facilities?
options: They are minimum required levels, and each HKFE Participant may charge clients higher margins
They are maximum permitted levels, and Participants may charge clients less if they choose
They are fixed levels that every HKFE Participant must charge clients without variation
They are advisory levels with no binding effect on HKFE Participants
explanation: Margins set by the HKFE are designed and defined as minimum required levels; each Participant has discretion to charge higher levels, and may do so for risk management reasons. They are not maximums, so charging less is not permitted. They are not fixed amounts that exclude discretion, because Participants may exceed them. They are binding minimums, not merely advisory. (PDF p.330)

question: On what basis are HKFE margin requirements calculated?
options: Historical price volatilities, current and anticipated market conditions, and other relevant information
The net asset value of the HKFE Participant concerned
The closing quotation of each contract at the end of the T Session only
A fixed percentage of the contract value prescribed by the SFC
explanation: The source states the HKFE margin requirements are calculated on the basis of historical price volatilities, current and anticipated market conditions, and other relevant information. The Participant's net asset value is not mentioned as a basis. The closing quotation at the end of the T Session is used for variation adjustments under the mark-to-market system, not for setting margin levels. No fixed SFC percentage is stated. (PDF p.330)

question: Mr Chan is an established client of an HKFE Participant and opens a new futures position during the T Session on a business day without having provided adequate collateral in advance. By when must the Participant issue a call for the minimum margin?
options: Within that same business day
Within the next business day
Within two business days
Before the close of the T+1 Session of the following week
explanation: For a new position established during the T Session, the Participant must issue a call for the minimum margin within that business day. The next-business-day deadline applies instead to positions established during the T+1 Session. Two business days and a week-based deadline are not provided for anywhere in the rules described. (PDF p.331)

question: Ms Lee is an established client who has a history of transacting day trades exclusively. She asks her HKFE Participant to open and close a position within the same T Session without depositing collateral first. What must the Participant do?
options: Refuse to transact the day trade until she provides adequate collateral covering her minimum requirements
Transact the trade and issue a margin call within that business day, as for any established client
Transact the trade because day trades are exempt from margin requirements
Transact the trade only if she undertakes in writing to settle within the next T+1 Session
explanation: An HKFE Participant shall not transact a day trade for an established client who has a history of transacting day trades exclusively, until and unless the client has provided adequate collateral to cover her minimum requirements. The normal established-client concession of trading first and calling margin afterwards does not apply in this case. Day trades are not exempt from margin, and a written undertaking to settle later is not a substitute the rules allow. (PDF p.331)

question: A client's margin on deposit with an HKFE Participant falls below the maintenance margin requirement level. Under the HKFE Rules, to what level must the account be re-margined?
options: Back to the initial margin requirement level
Back to the maintenance margin requirement level
To the level of the variation adjustment for that day
To twice the maintenance margin requirement level
explanation: Should the margin on deposit fall below the maintenance level, the HKFE Rules require the account to be re-margined back to the initial margin requirement level. Topping up only to the maintenance level is the common confusion but is not what the rules require. The variation adjustment is a separate daily mark-to-market settlement, not a re-margining target, and no doubling requirement exists. (PDF p.331)

question: How does the HKCC determine variation adjustments on open futures and options positions?
options: At the close of trading on the T Session each business day, each open position is deemed closed out and reopened at the closing quotation, with resulting profits credited and losses debited
At the close of the T+1 Session each business day, positions are revalued at the opening quotation of the next day
Once a week, all open positions are revalued at the average traded price for that week
Only at contract expiry, when the final settlement price is compared with the opening trade price
explanation: The HKCC uses the mark-to-market system: at the close of trading on the T Session on each business day, each open position is deemed closed out and a new contract opened at the closing quotation, with profits credited and losses debited to HKCC participants' accounts. The valuation point is the T Session close, not the T+1 Session or the next day's open. Weekly or expiry-only revaluation would let losses accumulate, which the mark-to-market system does not allow beyond one business day. (PDF p.331)

question: In times of extreme price volatility, what authority does the HKCC have in relation to open positions?
options: To perform additional intra-day mark-to-market calculations and call for immediate payment of variation adjustment during the T Session
To suspend all margin requirements until volatility subsides
To allow losses to accumulate for up to five business days before calling for payment
To transfer all open positions to the SFC for central settlement
explanation: The HKCC has the authority to perform additional intra-day mark-to-market calculations on open positions and to call for immediate payment of variation adjustment during the T Session of any business day. Suspending margin requirements is the opposite of what the risk controls provide. The mark-to-market system does not allow losses to accumulate beyond one business day, so five days is wrong. The SFC plays no settlement role here. (PDF p.332)

question: How is a physical delivery futures contract settled?
options: The seller delivers the underlying instrument and the buyer pays cash
Both parties exchange cash payments equal to the contract value
The buyer delivers the underlying instrument and the seller pays cash
The HKCC delivers the underlying instrument to both parties
explanation: Physical delivery contracts are settled by delivery of the underlying instrument by the sellers and by payment of cash by the buyers. An exchange of cash payments describes cash settled contracts, not physical delivery. Reversing the roles of buyer and seller is wrong. The HKCC acts as central counterparty but the source describes delivery obligations as resting on sellers and payment on buyers. (PDF p.332)

section: Trading position limits and reporting requirements
question: Under the Contracts Limits Rules, what is the prescribed position limit for stock index futures contracts on the Hang Seng Index?
options: 15,000 net long or short positions for all contract periods combined
25,000 net long or short positions for all contract periods combined
250,000 contracts per option class in any one market direction
1,000 contracts for any one contract month
explanation: The prescribed limit for Hang Seng Index stock index futures is 15,000 net long or short positions for all contract periods combined. 25,000 is the limit for Hang Seng China Enterprises Index futures (and for SEHK stock futures contracts). 250,000 contracts per option class is the limit for SEHK stock and ETF options. 1,000 contracts is the reporting level for SEHK stock futures, not a position limit. (PDF p.333)

section: Trading position limits and reporting requirements
question: Mr Wong's holdings of SEHK stock options in a single option class reach the reportable position level on Monday. What must be done under the Contracts Limits Rules?
options: Notify the SEHK in writing within one business day following the day on which first holding occurs, and on each succeeding day the position continues
Notify the SFC orally within three business days of first holding the position
Notify the HKFE in writing within one business day, with no further notification needed while the position continues
No notification is required unless the position also exceeds the prescribed position limit
explanation: Where a position reaches a specified reporting level, it must be notified in writing and lodged with the exchange company concerned (the SEHK for stock options) within one business day following the day on which first holding/control occurs, and on each succeeding day the position continues. Notification is written, goes to the exchange rather than the SFC, and the deadline is one business day, not three. For stock options the relevant exchange is the SEHK, not the HKFE, and continued holding requires continued reporting. Reporting applies at the reporting level regardless of whether the position limit is exceeded. (PDF p.333)

section: Trading position limits and reporting requirements
question: What is the reporting level for stock index futures contracts on the Hang Seng Index under the Contracts Limits Rules?
options: 500 open contracts multiplied by the ratio of HK$50 per index point to the contract multiplier, for any one contract period
1,000 contracts for any one contract month
15,000 net long or short positions for all contract periods combined
1,000 contracts per option class per expiry
explanation: The reporting level for Hang Seng Index stock index futures is 500 open contracts multiplied by the ratio of HK$50 per index point to the contract multiplier for any one contract period. 1,000 contracts for any one contract month is the reporting level for SEHK stock futures. 15,000 net long or short positions is the Hang Seng Index position limit, not its reporting level. 1,000 contracts per option class per expiry is the reporting level for SEHK stock and ETF options. (PDF p.334)

section: Trading position limits and reporting requirements
question: What is the consequence of violating the holding or reporting provisions of the Contracts Limits Rules?
options: It is an offence subject to potential fine and imprisonment
It reflects adversely on fitness and properness but is not an offence
It results only in automatic close-out of the excess positions by the HKCC
It is a civil matter giving rise to compensation claims only
explanation: The source states it is an offence subject to potential fine and imprisonment to violate either the holding or reporting provisions. The fitness-and-properness consequence applies to non-compliance with the Structured Products Guidelines, which do not have the force of law, not to the Contracts Limits Rules. Automatic close-out by the HKCC and civil compensation are not the consequences the source describes. (PDF p.334)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, distributes marketing materials for derivative warrants that do not comply with the SFC's Guidelines on Marketing Materials for Listed Structured Products. What is the regulatory effect of his non-compliance?
options: It will reflect adversely on his fitness and properness, although the Guidelines do not have the force of law
He commits a criminal offence subject to fine and imprisonment because the Guidelines have the force of law
There is no consequence because the Guidelines apply only to the HKEX and its subsidiaries
The HKCC will refuse to clear his transactions until the materials are corrected
explanation: The Structured Products Guidelines do not have the force of law, but a failure to comply with them will reflect adversely on the fitness and properness of licensed or registered persons. Fine and imprisonment is the sanction for violating the Contracts Limits Rules' holding or reporting provisions, not these Guidelines. The Guidelines are SFC guidelines applying to licensed or registered persons, not rules limited to the HKEX, and HKCC clearing is unrelated to marketing material compliance. (PDF p.334)

question: Which of the following is NOT among the matters on which the Structured Products Guidelines provide guidance for marketing materials?
options: The minimum commission to be charged on sales of the structured products
Explanation of risks of the underlying investment as well as of the structured products themselves
Presentation of a balanced view of the structured product, including potential gains and losses
Legibility and prominence of required information
explanation: Commission levels are not covered by the Guidelines; elsewhere the source notes there is no requirement for minimum commission on SEHK business. The Guidelines do provide guidance on the contents of marketing materials, explanation of risks of both the underlying investment and the structured products themselves, presentation of a balanced view including potential gains and losses, and the legibility and prominence of required information. (PDF p.335)

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the stated intentions of the Listing Rules?
options: Ensuring the suitability of applicants for listing
Ensuring the fair and equal treatment of shareholders
Guaranteeing the profitability of newly listed issuers
Ensuring that all new issues are first offered to existing equity shareholders as rights issues unless they agree otherwise
explanation: The Listing Rules are intended to ensure suitability of applicants, fair and orderly issue and marketing of securities, provision of sufficient and timely information, fair and equal treatment of shareholders, directors acting in the interests of shareholders as a whole, and that new issues (or sales/transfers of treasury shares) are first offered to existing shareholders as rights issues unless they agree otherwise. Guaranteeing issuer profitability is not among the listed purposes. Options A, B and D are all expressly stated in the Listing Rules' intentions. (PDF p.343)

question: Under the Listing Rules, what is the minimum requirement for independent non-executive directors (INEDs) on the board of a listed issuer?
options: At least two INEDs, at least one of whom has legal qualifications
At least three INEDs, at least one of whom has appropriate professional qualifications or accounting or related financial management expertise, and INEDs must make up at least one-third of the board
At least three INEDs, all of whom must have accounting qualifications, making up at least half of the board
At least one-third of the board must be INEDs, with no requirement as to their qualifications
explanation: Every board must have at least three INEDs, at least one of whom must have appropriate professional qualifications or accounting or related financial management expertise (MBLR 3.10; GLR 5.05), and at least one-third of the board must be INEDs (MBLR 3.10A; GLR 5.05A). Option A understates the number; option C wrongly requires all INEDs to have accounting qualifications and a half-board proportion; option D omits the qualification requirement for at least one INED. (PDF p.344)

question: Ms Wong is proposed as an INED of a listed issuer. She holds 6% of the issuer's issued shares (excluding treasury shares). Under the factors in MBLR 3.13, how is her independence likely to be assessed?
options: She will normally not be considered independent, because a person holding an interest of 5% or more will normally not be considered independent
She is automatically independent because the threshold for questioning independence is 10%
Her shareholding is irrelevant to independence as long as she has no business dealings with the issuer
She is independent because only holdings above 30% affect independence
explanation: Under MBLR 3.13, holding more than 1% of the issued shares (excluding treasury shares) is a factor against independence, and a person holding an interest of 5% or more will normally not be considered independent — so at 6% Ms Wong would normally fail. There is no 10% threshold in the text, shareholding is expressly a relevant factor, and 30% is the controlling shareholder threshold, not the INED independence test. (PDF p.344)

section: Persons involved in listings - Controlling shareholders
question: Under the Listing Rules, a controlling shareholder is a person (or group of persons together) who is:
options: Entitled to exercise or control 50% or more of the voting shares of an issuer
Entitled to exercise or control 30% or more of the voting shares (excluding votes attaching to treasury shares), or in a position to control the composition of a majority of the board of directors
Holding 5% or more of the issued shares of an issuer
Entitled to exercise or control 30% or more of the voting shares, including votes attaching to treasury shares
explanation: A controlling shareholder is any person who is, or group who together are, entitled to exercise or control 30% or more of the voting shares (excluding votes attaching to treasury shares) or in a position to control the composition of a majority of the board. Option A uses the wrong percentage; option C confuses this with the 5% INED independence factor; option D wrongly includes treasury share votes, which are expressly excluded. (PDF p.346)

section: Persons involved in listings - Authorised representatives
question: For a company listed on the Main Board, who may be appointed as the two authorised representatives acting as the principal channel of communication with the SEHK?
options: Any two senior managers of the issuer
Either two directors, or a director and the listed issuer's company secretary
The sponsor and the compliance adviser
Two individuals from among the issuer's executive directors and company secretary only
explanation: Under MBLR 3.05, the two authorised representatives of a Main Board issuer must be either two directors, or a director and the company secretary. Senior managers generally do not qualify; the sponsor and compliance adviser are separate roles. Option D describes the GEM requirement under GLR 5.24 (two individuals from among the executive directors and company secretary), not the Main Board rule. (PDF p.346)

question: A corporate finance firm wishes to act as a sponsor for a new listing applicant. At a minimum, which licence must it hold?
options: Type 1 - dealing in securities
Type 4 - advising on securities
Type 6 - advising on corporate finance
Type 9 - asset management
explanation: A sponsor must be appropriately licensed or registered and will need to hold a Type 6 licence (advising on corporate finance) at the minimum, and must meet additional eligibility requirements set by the SFC.
