Types 1, 4 and 9 are real regulated activities but are not the minimum licence specified for sponsor work in the text. (PDF p.347)

section: Persons involved in listings - Overall coordinators
question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, is advising a company seeking a Main Board IPO involving a bookbuilding exercise. Which statement about the "sponsor coupling" requirement is correct?
options: There must be at least one overall coordinator which also acts as, or is from the same group of companies as, the independent sponsor
Every overall coordinator must also be a sponsor of the listing applicant
The sponsor coupling requirement applies equally to GEM listing applications
The listing applicant must appoint exactly one overall coordinator, chosen by the SEHK
explanation: Where a Main Board listing involves bookbuilding or placing activities, there must be at least one overall coordinator which also acts as, or is from the same group of companies as, the independent sponsor. Only one such coupled overall coordinator is needed, not every coordinator (option B). There is no equivalent requirement for GEM (option C), and how many overall coordinators to appoint is the listing applicant's commercial decision, not the SEHK's (option D). (PDF p.348)

section: Persons involved in listings - Compliance advisers
question: For how long, at a minimum, must a newly listed issuer's compliance adviser appointment continue?
options: Until the completion of the IPO
At least until the publication of the listed issuer's financial results for the first full financial year after listing
For at least three full financial years after listing
Until the SEHK confirms in writing that the appointment may cease
explanation: Under MBLR 3A.19 and GLR 6A.19, the compliance adviser appointment must continue at least until publication of the issuer's financial results for the first full financial year after listing. The role starts at initial listing, not ends at IPO completion (option A); the text specifies one full financial year, not three (option C); no SEHK written confirmation is required to end the minimum period, although the SEHK may direct an issuer to appoint a compliance adviser after the specified minimum period (option D). (PDF p.349)

question: A new listing applicant's shares are likely to attract significant public demand, and its sponsor is considering methods of admission to listing. Which of the following statements is correct?
options: A placing only will not be permitted if there is likely to be significant public demand
An offer for subscription does not need to be underwritten
Active marketing is required in connection with an introduction
Only one method of admission may be used for any listing
explanation: A placing only will not be permitted if there is likely to be significant public demand, so option A is correct. An offer for subscription must be fully underwritten (option B is wrong), and no active marketing may be undertaken in connection with an introduction (option C reverses the rule). More than one method may be combined, for example an offer for subscription with an offer for sale or a placing (option D is wrong). (PDF p.350)

question: At the time of listing on the Main Board, a listing applicant must have a minimum market capitalisation of HK$500 million. What public float and shareholder spread must this include?
options: A public float of at least HK$125 million and a minimum of 300 shareholders
A public float of at least HK$100 million and a minimum of 300 shareholders
A public float of at least HK$125 million and a minimum of 500 shareholders
A public float of at least HK$500 million and a minimum of 100 shareholders
explanation: The HK$500 million minimum market capitalisation must include a public float of at least HK$125 million, with a sufficient spread of shareholders numbering at least 300. HK$100 million is the threshold below which the Price Stabilizing Rules do not apply, not the float requirement; 500 and 100 shareholders are not the stated minimum; and HK$500 million is the total market capitalisation, not the float. (PDF p.352)

question: Under the Main Board profit test, what profits attributable to shareholders must a listing applicant show?
options: Not less than HK$35 million for the most recent year and not less than HK$45 million in aggregate for the two preceding years
Not less than HK$45 million for the most recent year and not less than HK$35 million in aggregate for the two preceding years
Not less than HK$35 million in aggregate over the three preceding financial years
Not less than HK$100 million in aggregate for the three preceding financial years
explanation: The profit test requires profits attributable to shareholders of not less than HK$35 million for the most recent year and not less than HK$45 million in aggregate for the two preceding years. The second option reverses the two figures. The third option misstates the structure of the test, and HK$100 million is the aggregate positive operating cash flow figure in the market capitalisation/revenue/cash flow test, not a profit figure. (PDF p.352)

question: Which of the following correctly describes the listing requirements for a Biotech Company on the Main Board?
options: A two-year trading record under substantially the same management and an initial market capitalisation of at least HK$1.5 billion, with no other quantitative test
A three-year trading record under substantially the same management and an initial market capitalisation of at least HK$1.5 billion
A two-year trading record and satisfaction of the profit test of HK$35 million for the most recent year
A two-year trading record and an initial market capitalisation of at least HK$4 billion
explanation: Biotech Companies need a two-year trading record period under substantially the same management and are not required to satisfy any quantitative test other than an initial market capitalisation of at least HK$1.5 billion. Three years is the standard trading record for ordinary applicants, not Biotech Companies; the profit test does not apply to them; and HK$4 billion is the market capitalisation figure in the market capitalisation/revenue test (and for Commercial specialist technology companies), not for Biotech Companies. (PDF p.353)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, is asked by a retail client (who is not a professional investor) to buy shares in a newly listed SPAC. He also wants to know how long the SPAC has to complete its De-SPAC Transaction. Which statement is correct?
options: The client cannot trade the SPAC's securities because they may not be marketed to or traded by the public who are not professional investors, and the De-SPAC Transaction must be completed within 36 months of listing
The client may trade the SPAC's securities freely, and the De-SPAC Transaction must be completed within 36 months of listing
The client cannot trade the SPAC's securities, and the De-SPAC Transaction must be completed within 24 months of listing
The client may trade the SPAC's securities only after the De-SPAC Transaction, which must be completed within 18 months of listing
explanation: SPAC securities may not be marketed to or traded by the public in Hong Kong who are not professional investors, so the retail client cannot trade them, and the De-SPAC Transaction must be completed within 36 months of the date of listing. The second option wrongly allows retail trading. The 24-month figure is the typical pre-defined period within which a suitable business must be acquired, not the completion deadline, and 18 months is the continuous suspension period after which the SEHK may cancel a listing. (PDF p.353)

question: An innovative growth company applying to list with a weighted voting rights (WVR) structure has a market capitalisation at listing of HK$12 billion. What additional condition must it meet to satisfy the HKEX's WVR eligibility tests?
options: Revenue in the most recent audited financial year of at least HK$1 billion
Nothing further, because its market capitalisation already exceeds HK$10 billion
Revenue in the most recent audited financial year of at least HK$500 million
Positive operating cash flow of at least HK$100 million in aggregate over the three preceding financial years
explanation: A WVR applicant must either have a market capitalisation of at least HK$40 billion, or at least HK$10 billion together with revenue of at least HK$1 billion in the most recent audited financial year; at HK$12 billion it falls under the second limb and needs the HK$1 billion revenue. Market capitalisation alone only suffices at HK$40 billion or more. HK$500 million revenue and HK$100 million cash flow are figures from the standard quantitative tests for ordinary listing applicants, not the WVR tests. (PDF p.354)

question: Under MBLR Chapter 17, what is the scheme mandate limit for a listed company's share scheme?
options: The total shares issuable under the scheme and any other schemes must not exceed 10% of the relevant class of shares in issue (excluding treasury shares) at the time the scheme is approved
The total shares issuable under the scheme must not exceed 20% of the issued shares of the issuer
The total shares issuable under the scheme must not exceed 10% of the relevant class of shares, including treasury shares
The total shares issuable under the scheme must not exceed 5% of the relevant class of shares in issue
explanation: The scheme mandate limit caps shares issuable under the scheme and any other schemes at 10% of the relevant class of shares in issue, excluding treasury shares, at the time of approval of the scheme. The 20% figure is the limit for warrant issues under MBLR Chapter 15, not share schemes. Treasury shares are excluded, not included, and 5% is the percentage ratio threshold for a discloseable transaction, not the scheme mandate limit. (PDF p.355)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, is advising a listed issuer that proposes to acquire a business where the highest percentage ratio under the Main Board Listing Rules is 30%. How is this transaction classified under MBLR Chapter 14?
options: A major transaction
A discloseable transaction
A very substantial acquisition
A share transaction
explanation: A major transaction is one where any percentage ratio is at least 25% but less than 100% for an acquisition, so a 30% ratio acquisition is a major transaction. A discloseable transaction applies where any ratio is at least 5% but less than 25%; a very substantial acquisition requires a ratio of 100% or more; and a share transaction requires all ratios to be less than 5% with consideration including securities to be listed and/or treasury shares. (PDF p.356)

section: Trading halt, suspension, cancellation and withdrawal of listings
question: How does a trading halt differ from a trading suspension under the Listing Rules?
options: A trading halt is a brief interruption pending disclosure of information lasting no more than two trading days, after which it automatically becomes a trading suspension
A trading halt can only be directed by the SFC, whereas a suspension can only be directed by the SEHK
A trading halt may last up to five trading days before it becomes a trading suspension
A trading halt can only be requested by the issuer, whereas a suspension can only be imposed by the SEHK without request
explanation: A trading halt is intended as a brief interruption of trading, requested or directed pending disclosure of information, extending for no more than two trading days; if it exceeds two trading days it automatically becomes a trading suspension. The SEHK may direct a trading halt or suspend dealings whether or not requested by the issuer, so the second and fourth options are wrong, and the SFC's separate power is to direct the SEHK to suspend dealings, not to be the sole source of halts. Five trading days is not the stated limit. (PDF p.358)

question: Following an SFC-initiated review, the Listing Review Committee modifies a disciplinary decision of the Listing Committee. What may the relevant party do?
options: Seek a further review by a new Listing Review Committee comprised of persons not involved in the prior decision
Nothing, because a decision of the Listing Review Committee is always final
Appeal directly to the SFC for a final determination
Require the Listing Committee to rehear the original disciplinary proceedings
explanation: Where the Listing Review Committee overturns, modifies or varies a Listing Committee decision as a result of an SFC-initiated review, the relevant party may seek a further review by a new Listing Review Committee made up of persons not involved in the prior decision. The Listing Review Committee's review is final only in the ordinary route where the sanctioned party itself refers the decision; this special further review applies after SFC-initiated reviews. There is no appeal to the SFC for a final determination, and no rehearing by the Listing Committee is provided for. (PDF p.361)

question: Under Chapter 37 of the Main Board Listing Rules, debt securities listed under that chapter in Hong Kong may be issued to which investors?
options: Professional investors in Hong Kong only (and similar classes of overseas investors where a local exemption applies)
Retail investors in Hong Kong, provided a suitability assessment is performed
Any investor, as long as the listing document is published before the listing date
Institutional professional investors only, excluding all other classes of professional investors
explanation: Chapter 37 provides for the issue of debt securities only to PIs in Hong Kong (as defined by the SFO) and to other similar classes of overseas investors covered by a local-law exemption. The SFC has stated Chapter 37 securities are unsuitable for sale to retail investors, so the retail option is wrong even with a suitability assessment. The securities are not open to any investor, and the restriction is to PIs generally, not only institutional PIs. (PDF p.364)

section: Takeovers and mergers and share buy-backs - Introduction
question: Who is responsible for the day-to-day administration of the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs?
options: The Takeovers and Mergers Panel
The Executive Director of the Corporate Finance Division of the SFC (including his delegates)
The Takeovers Appeal Committee
The SEHK Listing Division
explanation: The Executive Director of the Corporate Finance Division of the SFC (the "Executive", including his delegates) is responsible for the day-to-day administration of the Codes and gives rulings on matters to which they apply. The Panel considers referrals, conducts first-instance disciplinary hearings and reviews Executive rulings, not day-to-day administration. The Takeovers Appeal Committee only reviews Panel sanctions, and the SEHK has no such role under the Codes. (PDF p.365)

question: Which statement best describes the legal status of the Codes on Takeovers and Mergers and Share Buy-backs?
options: They are statutory rules made under the SFO, breach of which is a criminal offence
They are non-statutory, but Panel rulings can be judicially reviewed and the Listing Rules require compliance with the Codes
They are non-statutory and have no connection with the Listing Rules
They are statutory, but breaches can only be sanctioned by the courts
explanation: The Codes are non-statutory, yet the Hong Kong courts have accepted that Panel rulings can be judicially reviewed for procedural fairness and natural justice, and the Listing Rules (MBLR 13.23(2) and 14.78) require compliance with the Codes, so a breach of the Codes is a breach of the Listing Rules. The two "statutory" options are wrong because the Codes are not statutory, and the Codes are expressly linked to the Listing Rules, so the third option fails. (PDF p.365)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, advises a client who currently controls 28% of the voting rights of a Hong Kong listed company. The client proposes to buy additional shares that would bring his holding to 31% of the voting rights. What is the consequence under the Takeovers Code?
options: No obligation arises because the increase is only 3%, which is below the 5% threshold
The client must make a mandatory general offer under Rule 26, as his holding will reach 30% or more of the voting rights
The client may proceed freely as the obligation only applies at 50% or more of the voting rights
The client need only notify the Executive, who will automatically grant a dispensation
explanation: Acquiring voting rights that take a person to 30% or more of a company's voting rights is the "trigger" requiring a mandatory offer under Rule 26, so moving from 28% to 31% creates the obligation. There is no 5% threshold for the trigger, and the relevant level is 30%, not 50%. The Executive can grant a dispensation in certain circumstances, but it should not be assumed that one will be granted, so it is never automatic. (PDF p.367)

question: Under the "creeper" provision of the Takeovers Code, a mandatory offer is required when a person holding between 30% and 50% of a company's voting rights increases that holding by more than:
options: 2% from the lowest percentage holding in the preceding 12-month period
5% from the lowest percentage holding in the preceding 12-month period
2% from the lowest percentage holding in the preceding 6-month period
10% in any single transaction
explanation: The creeper applies when a person (or persons acting in concert) holding not less than 30% but not more than 50% of the voting rights increases the holding by more than 2% from the lowest percentage holding in the 12-month period ending on the date of the acquisition. 5% and 10% are not the figures stated in the Code, and the look-back period is 12 months, not 6 months (6 months is the look-back used to set the minimum price of a mandatory offer). (PDF p.368)

question: Which of the following correctly contrasts mandatory offers with voluntary offers under the Takeovers Code?
options: A mandatory offer must include a cash component with a 6-month price look-back; a voluntary offer uses a 3-month look-back and may be in cash or other consideration
A mandatory offer uses a 3-month price look-back; a voluntary offer uses a 6-month look-back and must always be in cash
Both types of offer must be entirely in cash, with a 6-month price look-back in each case
A voluntary offer may be made at any price, including one substantially below the market price of the offeree's shares
explanation: All mandatory offers must include a cash component at a price not less than the highest price paid by the offeror or concert parties during, or within six months before, the offer period; a voluntary offer uses a three-month look-back and may be in cash or some other consideration, typically transferable securities. The second and third options reverse or misstate these rules. The fourth option is wrong because voluntary offers are not permitted at a price substantially below the market price of the offeree company's shares. (PDF p.369)

question: Under the Codes, what is a "cold shoulder order"?
options: A public statement issued by the Panel which involves criticism of an offender
A requirement that intermediaries, such as dealers and advisers, not act for a stated period in any or a stated capacity for a person who has failed to comply with the Codes or a ruling
A ban preventing advisers from appearing before the Executive or the Panel for a stated period
An order suspending an intermediary's licence issued directly by the Panel
explanation: A cold shoulder order is a Panel sanction requiring intermediaries (such as dealers and advisers) not to act, or continue to act, for a stated period in any or a stated capacity for a person who has failed or indicated he does not intend to comply with the Codes or a ruling. A public statement involving criticism and a ban on advisers appearing before the Executive or Panel are separate, distinct sanctions. Licence suspension is not a Panel sanction; non-compliance may instead call an intermediary's fitness and properness into question, leading to licensing consequences under the relevant ordinances. (PDF p.370)

section: Part IV, SFO: collective investment schemes
question: Which of the following arrangements is EXCLUDED from the definition of a collective investment scheme under Schedule 1, SFO?
options: A franchise arrangement
An arrangement where participants' contributions and accruing profits are pooled
An arrangement where property is managed as a whole by the person operating the arrangement
An arrangement whose purpose is to enable participants to receive profits or income from the property
explanation: Franchise arrangements are expressly excluded from the CIS definition, along with arrangements where the participants and operator belong to the same group of companies and arrangements where a solicitor holds client money in his professional capacity. The other three options are positive elements of what constitutes a CIS: pooling of contributions and profits, management of the property as a whole, and the purpose of enabling participants to receive profits, income or other returns. (PDF p.372)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, carelessly (negligently, but not fraudulently or recklessly) misrepresents the features of a structured product, inducing a client to enter into an agreement to acquire it. Under the SFO, which provision covers this negligent misrepresentation?
options: Section 107, SFO, which makes negligent misrepresentation a criminal offence
Section 108, SFO, which provides civil remedies and, unlike s. 107, also covers negligent misrepresentation
Section 103, SFO, which prohibits unauthorised advertisements
Section 104, SFO, which deals with the authorisation of CISs
explanation: Section 108, SFO establishes civil remedies for fraudulent, reckless or negligent misrepresentation that induces a person to enter into the relevant agreements, and it is the only one of these provisions that extends to negligence. Section 107, SFO is the criminal offence, but it covers only fraudulent or reckless misrepresentation, not negligence. Section 103, SFO deals with the issue of unauthorised Advertisements, and s. 104, SFO with SFC authorisation of CISs, neither of which addresses misrepresentation. (PDF p.375)

section: Part IV, SFO: structured products (misrepresentation definitions)
question: Under the SFO, a statement which, at the time it is made, is false, misleading or deceptive, and is made without reasonable care having been taken to ensure its accuracy, is which type of misrepresentation?
options: Fraudulent misrepresentation
Reckless misrepresentation
Negligent misrepresentation
Innocent misrepresentation
explanation: A negligent misrepresentation is a false, misleading or deceptive statement made without reasonable care having been taken to ensure its accuracy. A fraudulent misrepresentation requires the maker to know the statement is false at the time it is made, and a reckless misrepresentation is one made recklessly, so both involve more than a lack of reasonable care. "Innocent misrepresentation" is not one of the three types defined in the SFO in this context. (PDF p.376)

question: Which of the following is specifically EXCLUDED from the SFO definition of a "structured product"?
options: A regulated investment agreement
A convertible debenture
An instrument whose return is determined by reference to changes in currency exchange rates
An instrument whose settlement method is determined by the occurrence of specified events
explanation: CISs, convertible debentures and subscription warrants are specific exclusions from the definition of a structured product. A regulated investment agreement is positively included in the definition, as are instruments whose return, amount due or settlement method is determined by reference to changes in the price, value or level of other financial products (including currency exchange rates) or by the occurrence or non-occurrence of specified events. (PDF p.377)

question: In which TWO circumstances may a structured product be offered or marketed to the public in Hong Kong?
options: Listed on the SEHK, or authorised by the SFC under s. 104A, SFO
Authorised by the SFC under s. 104A, SFO, or prescribed by the Financial Secretary under s. 392, SFO
Listed on the SEHK, or issued by an authorised financial institution
Authorised by the HKMA, or authorised by the SFC under s. 104A, SFO
explanation: A structured product may only be offered or marketed to the public if it is either listed on the SEHK or authorised by the SFC under s. 104A, SFO, with authorisation being by far the most common route. Section 392, SFO is the Financial Secretary's power to extend the definition of structured products, not an offering route. Issue by an AFI only attracts an SFC exemption for certain currency-linked and interest rate-linked instruments, and the HKMA does not authorise structured products for public offer. (PDF p.377)

section: Codes and guidelines issued by the SFC (Handbook)
question: Which of the following correctly states a requirement for a Product Key Facts Statement (Product KFS)?
options: It should not be more than four pages long and must carry a first-page warning that investors should not invest based on the Product KFS alone
It should not be more than two pages long and must disclose the product provider's seven general principles
It is always a stand-alone document and never forms part of the offering document
It need not disclose whether the CIS will make derivatives investments, as this appears only in the offering document
explanation: A Product KFS should not exceed four pages and must contain a warning statement on the first page that investors should not invest in the product based on the Product KFS alone. The two-page limit is wrong (four is the stated limit), and the seven general principles are Handbook obligations on product providers, not KFS contents. The Product KFS is normally regarded as part of the offering document (unless the applicable product code states otherwise), and it must disclose whether the CIS will make derivatives investments. (PDF p.379)

question: Which of the following functions relating to MPF schemes is retained by the SFC rather than the MPFA?
options: Overall management and administration of the MPF system
Registration of schemes established under the MPFSO
Issuing the Code on MPF Investment Funds
Authorising MPF scheme offering documents and marketing materials prior to their issue in Hong Kong
explanation: The SFC is responsible for authorising MPF scheme offering documents and marketing materials prior to their issue or publication in Hong Kong; it also issued the SFC Code on MPF Products and approves the investment managers of scheme products. The MPFA is the primary regulator under the MPFSO, responsible for overall management and administration of the MPF system and registration of schemes, and it issues the Code on MPF Investment Funds and the Code on Disclosure for MPF Investment Funds. (PDF p.381)

section: The SFC's special requirements for parties involved in authorised CISs (trustees/custodians)
question: Mr Chan is reviewing a trustee of an SFC-authorised unit trust that is NOT licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity. What minimum capital requirement applies to that trustee?
options: Minimum paid-up share capital and non-distributable capital reserves of HK$10 million, or its foreign currency equivalent
Minimum paid-up share capital and non-distributable capital reserves of HK$2 billion, or its foreign currency equivalent
It must comply with the Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules
There is no capital requirement provided it is independently audited
explanation: Trustees/custodians not licensed or registered for Type 13 regulated activity must have minimum paid-up share capital and non-distributable capital reserves of HK$10 million or its foreign currency equivalent, although this may be waived for a wholly-owned subsidiary of a substantial financial institution that gives the required commitments or undertakings. HK$2 billion is the minimum NAV defining a substantial financial institution, not the trustee's capital requirement. The Financial Resources Rules apply only to those that ARE Type 13 licensed or registered, and independent audit is an additional requirement, not a substitute for capital. (PDF p.382)

section: The SFC's special requirements for parties involved in authorised unlisted structured investment products
question: Mr Chan, a director of the issuer of an unlisted SIP seeking SFC authorisation, is nominated as the approved person to be served with SFC notices and decisions. Which licensing requirement must he satisfy?
options: Licensed or registered in respect of Type 9 (asset management) regulated activity
Licensed or registered in respect of Type 1 (dealing in securities) or Type 4 (advising on securities) regulated activity
Licensed or registered in respect of Type 13 regulated activity
No licence is required as long as he is ordinarily resident in Hong Kong
explanation: The approved person nominated for an unlisted SIP must be licensed or registered in respect of Type 1 (dealing in securities) or Type 4 (advising on securities) regulated activity, as well as being a director of the issuer or guarantor (or responsible/executive officer of a product arranger) and ordinarily resident in Hong Kong. Type 9 is the regulated activity for CIS management companies/investment managers and Type 13 for depositaries acting as trustee/custodian, neither of which qualifies the approved person. Being ordinarily resident in Hong Kong is a separate, additional requirement and does not remove the licensing requirement. (PDF p.386)

question: An issuer of an authorised unlisted SIP has a minimum NAV of HK$2 billion but is not a bank regulated by the HKMA, not a corporation licensed by the SFC, and not an overseas banking entity subject to acceptable regulatory oversight. Under Appendix A of the SIP Code, how may it still be eligible?
options: By holding a credit rating in one of the top three investment grades from at least one acceptable rating agency of international standing and reputation
By appointing a representative in Hong Kong who is a registered trust company
By increasing its minimum paid-up share capital to HK$10 million
By providing investors with a cooling-off period
explanation: Where an issuer cannot satisfy any of the three institutional categories, it may nevertheless be eligible if it has a credit rating which is one of the top three investment grades awarded by at least one rating agency of international standing and reputation acceptable to the SFC. Appointing a Hong Kong representative is a requirement for authorised CISs whose management company is not incorporated or has no place of business in Hong Kong, not an SIP issuer eligibility route. HK$10 million paid-up capital is the requirement for CIS management companies and certain trustees/custodians, and the cooling-off period is an SIP Code requirement imposed on issuers in any event, not an eligibility substitute. (PDF p.387)

question: Under the SIP Code, in which of the following situations is the issuer of an unlisted structured investment product (SIP) required to appoint a product arranger?
options: Whenever the SIP involves a collateralised structure
Where the issuer is a special purpose vehicle, or neither the issuer nor any guarantor is a bank regulated by the HKMA or a corporation licensed by the SFC
Whenever the SIP is exposed to the creditworthiness of a key product counterparty
In every case where authorisation of an unlisted SIP is sought from the SFC
explanation: A product arranger must be appointed where (i) the issuer is a special purpose vehicle, or (ii) neither the issuer nor any guarantor is an HKMA-regulated bank or an SFC-licensed corporation. A collateralised structure instead triggers the requirement for a trustee/custodian, and a key product counterparty triggers independence and selection requirements, not the appointment of a product arranger. The appointment is conditional, not required in every authorisation case. (PDF p.388)

question: Under the SIP Code, a trustee/custodian holding collateral for an authorised unlisted SIP must be independently audited and have a minimum issued and paid-up capital and non-distributable reserves of the equivalent of:
options: HK$5 million
HK$10 million
HK$50 million
HK$100 million
explanation: Appendix B to the SIP Code requires the trustee/custodian to be independently audited and have minimum issued and paid-up capital and non-distributable reserves of the equivalent of HK$10 million. The other amounts are not stated in the SIP Code requirements; HK$5 million, HK$50 million and HK$100 million are all incorrect figures. The trustee/custodian must also be independent of the issuer, any guarantor, product arranger and key product counterparty. (PDF p.389)

question: Which of the following statements about crowdfunding in Hong Kong is CORRECT?
options: Equity crowdfunding (ECF) is the form of crowdfunding where the investment is in the nature of a loan
Both ECF and peer-to-peer lending (P2P) may involve the offering of securities and could constitute a collective investment scheme (CIS)
Crowdfunding falls outside the prospectus laws in the CWUMPO because it is conducted via website-based platforms
Persons promoting ECF or P2P investments never require a licence because crowdfunding is not a regulated activity
explanation: Both ECF and P2P give rise to regulatory concerns because both may involve the offering of securities and could constitute a CIS. ECF is where the investor obtains an interest in the equity of the business; it is P2P lending where the investment is in the nature of a loan. The prospectus laws in CWUMPO and the SFO advertisement restrictions still need to be considered, and promoters may be engaging in Type 1 regulated activity (dealing in securities) or other regulated activities requiring a licence. (PDF p.390)

question: Which of the following characteristics of a virtual asset (VA) would render it a security regulated by the SFO, rather than a VA regulated by the AMLO?
options: It is expressed as a unit of account or a store of economic value
It can be used as a medium of exchange
It represents equity or ownership interests in a corporation, i.e. it is a "share"
It can be transferred, stored or traded electronically
explanation: A VA is a security under the SFO where it represents equity or ownership interests in a corporation (a "share"), represents a debt or liability (a "debenture"), or its proceeds are managed collectively and invested for profit so as to constitute a CIS. The other three options are characteristics under which a VA falls to be regulated by the AMLO, not the SFO. Where a VA is a security, the usual securities laws, including Hong Kong's prospectus law and the Part IV offers of investments regime, apply. (PDF p.391)

question: According to the SFC's list of risks of investing in virtual assets, why do some market misconduct provisions of the SFO NOT apply to trading in VAs?
options: Because VAs are typically held and traded on an anonymous basis
Because trading in VAs does not take place on a "relevant recognised market" as defined in the SFO, i.e. the SEHK
Because there are no agreed accounting standards to ascertain ownership of VAs
Because offerings of VAs that are not securities are not subject to prospectus requirements
explanation: Some market misconduct provisions of the SFO do not apply because trading in VAs does not take place on a "relevant recognised market" as defined in the SFO, i.e. the SEHK. Anonymity is cited as the reason for money laundering and terrorist financing risk, not the inapplicability of market misconduct provisions. The lack of accounting standards is a separate accounting and auditing risk, and the prospectus point is a different example of unequal regulatory safeguards, not the reason the misconduct provisions fail to apply. (PDF p.392)

question: Which of the following statements about tokenised securities and digital securities is CORRECT?
options: All tokenised securities are regarded as complex products because of the tokenisation wrapper
A tokenised security is characterised as a complex product only if the underlying financial instrument is itself a complex product, applying a see-through approach
Digital securities that are not tokenised securities are unlikely to be regarded as complex products
Tokenised securities fall outside the definition of "securities" under the SFO because of their digital form
explanation: Tokenised securities may not be regarded as complex products: they are only characterised as complex if the underlying financial instrument is itself a complex product, and intermediaries should adopt a see-through approach with reference to the SFC's existing guidance for traditional securities. By contrast, digital securities that are not tokenised securities are likely to be regarded as complex products due to their bespoke nature, unique terms and heightened legal uncertainties. Tokenised securities are fundamentally traditional securities with a tokenisation wrapper and do amount to "securities" under the SFO. (PDF p.393)

question: Ms Wong manages a fund already authorised by the SFC. The fund now plans to obtain virtual asset exposure of 12% of its net asset value (NAV). What must be done?
options: Nothing, because the exposure is below the 20% of NAV threshold
Notify the SFC after the VA exposure has been obtained
Consult with and obtain the approval of the SFC before obtaining the exposure
Seek authorisation from the HKMA, as VA exposure falls under the AMLO
explanation: Prior consultation with and approval of the SFC are required for SFC-authorised funds that plan to obtain VA exposure of more than 10% of their NAV; 12% exceeds that threshold. The relevant threshold is 10% of NAV, not 20%, and the requirement is prior consultation and approval, not after-the-fact notification. The approval is given by the SFC, not the HKMA. Prior consultation and approval are also required for SFC-authorised funds intending to engage in staking and other VA-related activities. (PDF p.395)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, deals for clients in Bitcoin futures contracts traded on a regulated futures market overseas. Under the SFO, this dealing activity constitutes which type of regulated activity?
options: Type 1 regulated activity (dealing in securities)
Type 2 regulated activity (dealing in futures contracts)
Type 5 regulated activity (advising on futures contracts)
Type 9 regulated activity (asset management)
explanation: Dealing in futures contracts on VAs constitutes Type 2 regulated activity, because the SFO definition of futures contract captures contracts and options on contracts made under the rules or conventions of a futures market. Type 5 would apply to advising on such futures contracts, not dealing in them. Type 1 relates to dealing in securities, and Type 9 applies where an intermediary manages an investment portfolio investing in VAs structured as a CIS. (PDF p.396)

question: Under the takeovers regime, the "creeper" effect applies to a person (or persons acting in concert) holding between 30% and 50% of a company's voting rights. A mandatory offer is triggered when they increase their holding by more than what amount over what period?
options: More than 2% from their lowest percentage holding in the preceding 12-month period
More than 5% from their lowest percentage holding in the preceding 12-month period
More than 2% from their highest percentage holding in the preceding 6-month period
More than 30% of the total voting rights at any time
explanation: The creeper applies when persons holding between 30% and 50% of the voting rights increase their holding by more than 2% from their lowest percentage holding in the preceding 12-month period, obliging them to make an offer for each class of shares concerned. 5% is not the stated creeper figure; the measure is from the lowest (not highest) holding over 12 (not 6) months; and 30% is the separate initial trigger threshold for acquiring voting rights, not the creeper increment. (PDF p.400)

question: Mr Chan plans to set up a Hong Kong company providing trustee and custodian services to authorised collective investment schemes (CISs). Which statement best reflects the regulatory position described in the manual?
options: Providing trustee/custodian services is not a regulated activity, but the SFC exercises a degree of oversight through requirements placed on users of trustees/custodians, such as authorised CISs
Providing trustee/custodian services is a regulated activity, so Mr Chan's company must be licensed by the SFC
Trustee/custodian services are wholly unregulated, and the SFC has no oversight of them in any form
Trustee/custodian services may only be provided if the trustee itself is authorised under the SIP Code
explanation: The manual states that acting as trustee/custodian is not a regulated activity, but the SFC nevertheless exercises a degree of regulatory oversight through requirements placed on the users of trustees/custodians, such as authorised CISs. It is therefore wrong to say a licence is required as for a regulated activity, and equally wrong to say there is no SFC oversight at all. The SIP Code authorisation requirement applies to unlisted SIPs, not to trustees as such. (PDF p.400)

section: Market misconduct under the Securities and Futures Ordinance
question: What standard of proof applies in proceedings before the Market Misconduct Tribunal (MMT)?
options: Beyond reasonable doubt
On the balance of probabilities
Sufficiency of evidence and the public interest
Fair, just and reasonable
explanation: The MMT determines its proceedings on the civil standard, "on the balance of probabilities", a lower standard of proof. "Beyond reasonable doubt" is the high standard required for a criminal conviction before a court, not the MMT. "Sufficiency of evidence and the public interest" are the Department of Justice's prosecution criteria, not a standard of proof, and "fair, just and reasonable" is the test for awarding damages in private civil actions. (PDF p.405)

question: Which of the following is NOT one of the six forms of market misconduct defined in s. 245, SFO?
options: False trading
Price rigging
False representations to a person of having dealt in futures contracts on his behalf
Disclosure of information about prohibited transactions
explanation: Section 245, SFO defines market misconduct as insider dealing, false trading, price rigging, disclosure of information about prohibited transactions, disclosure of false or misleading information inducing transactions, and stock market manipulation. False representations of having dealt in futures contracts on a person's behalf is a separate Part XIV offence under s. 302, SFO, not part of the s. 245 definition. The other three options are all within the six defined forms. (PDF p.406)

question: For the purposes of insider dealing, a substantial shareholder counted as a person connected with a corporation is one holding at least what proportion of the total number of shares comprised in the relevant share capital?
explanation: Persons connected with a corporation include directors, employees or substantial shareholders, with substantial shareholders for this purpose being those holding at least 5% of the total number of shares comprised in the relevant share capital of the corporation or its related corporation. The 1%, 10% and 25% figures are not the thresholds stated in the source text. (PDF p.407)

question: Under the SFO, what is a wash trade?
options: A sale and purchase of securities without a change in their beneficial ownership
An offer to sell securities matched by an offer to buy the same securities at substantially the same price by an associate of the offeror
A transaction conducted through a recognised stock market that is not required to be recorded on or notified to that market
A fictitious or artificial transaction which has the effect of causing fluctuations in the price of securities
explanation: A wash trade is any transaction involving a sale and purchase of securities without a change in their beneficial ownership. The second option describes a matched order, a different transaction that also triggers the false trading presumption. The third option describes an off-market transaction, to which the presumption does not apply, and the fourth describes the fictitious or artificial transactions covered by price rigging. (PDF p.409)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, places an offer to sell shares on the SEHK which is matched by a proposed offer from his associate to buy the same shares in substantially the same quantity at substantially the same price. Mr Chan is presumed to have engaged in false trading. How can he overcome this presumption?
options: By showing the transactions were conducted in Hong Kong rather than on an overseas market
By showing the orders were not carried out for the purpose of creating a false or misleading appearance of active trading or with respect to the market or the price
By showing there was a genuine change in the beneficial ownership of the shares
By showing he acted in good faith and received no benefit from a party to the transaction
explanation: A person who enters matched orders is presumed to have engaged in false trading, and to overcome the presumption he must show the wash trades or matched orders were not carried out for the purpose of creating a false or misleading appearance of active trading in securities or with respect to the market or their price. The Hong Kong/overseas location does not rebut the presumption; false trading can occur in both. A change in beneficial ownership distinguishes wash trades, but matched orders are defined by matching offers, not ownership. Good faith without benefit is the defence to disclosure of information about prohibited transactions, not false trading. (PDF p.409)

question: To which transactions does the market misconduct of price rigging apply?
options: Only transactions entered on-exchange or via an authorised automated trading service
Only transactions conducted off-market
Any transaction, whether on-exchange, off-market or private
Only transactions in futures contracts, not securities
explanation: The source notes that price rigging only applies to transactions entered on-exchange or via an authorised ATS. Off-market transactions are relevant to false trading, where the wash trade/matched order presumption does not apply, not to the scope of price rigging. Price rigging covers fictitious or artificial transactions affecting the price of securities or futures contracts, so it is not limited to futures. (PDF p.409)

question: Which TWO elements must both be present for stock market manipulation under ss. 278 and 299, SFO?
options: A single transaction creating an artificial price, and recklessness as to its effect
Two or more transactions affecting the price of securities, and the intention of influencing the investment decisions of other persons
Disclosure of false or misleading information, and inducement of subscription, sale or purchase of securities
A wash trade, and the receipt of a benefit from an associate
explanation: Stock market manipulation requires a person to enter, directly or indirectly, into two or more transactions which have or are likely to have the effect of increasing, reducing, maintaining or stabilizing the price of securities, AND to do so with the intention of influencing the investment decisions of other persons. A single reckless transaction creating an artificial price describes false trading, not manipulation. Disclosure of false or misleading information inducing transactions is a separate form of market misconduct, and wash trades with benefits relate to false trading and disclosure of prohibited transactions. (PDF p.410)

question: Which statement about the composition of the Market Misconduct Tribunal is correct?
options: It is chaired by a judge and has two other members who shall not be public officers, all appointed by the Chief Executive
It is chaired by the Secretary for Justice and has two other members appointed by the SFC
It is chaired by a judge and has two other members who must be public officers, appointed by the Chief Executive
It is chaired by a judge and has four other members appointed by the Secretary for Justice
explanation: The MMT is chaired by a judge and has two other members, who shall not be public officers; all three members are appointed by the Chief Executive of the HKSAR, who may remove them on grounds specified in Schedule 9, SFO. The Secretary for Justice and the SFC have roles in deciding the route of proceedings but do not appoint MMT members. Requiring the other members to be public officers reverses the actual rule, and the MMT has three members in total, not five. (PDF p.412)

section: Criminal sanctions imposed by the courts
question: What are the maximum criminal sanctions under s. 303, SFO for a market misconduct offence on conviction on indictment?
options: Three years' imprisonment and a fine of HK$1 million
Five years' imprisonment and a fine of HK$5 million
Ten years' imprisonment and a fine of HK$10 million
Seven years' imprisonment and a fine of HK$10 million
explanation: On conviction on indictment, the SFO prescribes ten years' imprisonment and a fine of HK$10 million. Three years' imprisonment and HK$1 million is the maximum on summary conviction, not on indictment. Five years is the maximum duration of MMT disqualification and cold shoulder orders, not a term of imprisonment under s. 303, and seven years is not a figure stated in the source. (PDF p.413)

question: Ms Wong suffered pecuniary loss as a result of another person's market misconduct, but she never dealt at a price affected by that misconduct and the MMT has made no finding against the wrongdoer. Can she claim compensation under the SFO?
options: No, because she did not deal at a price affected by the market misconduct
No, because a finding of market misconduct by the MMT or a criminal court is required first
Yes, and damages will be payable automatically once pecuniary loss is shown
Yes, whether or not she dealt at an affected price, but damages are payable only if it is "fair, just and reasonable" in the circumstances
explanation: Under ss. 281 and 305, SFO a person who has suffered pecuniary loss from market misconduct may claim damages whether or not he has entered into a transaction or dealt at a price affected by the misconduct, so the first option is wrong. The right to seek compensation is independent of any finding by the MMT or a criminal court (although such a finding would assist), so no prior finding is required. Damages are not automatic: they are payable only if it is "fair, just and reasonable" in the circumstances. (PDF p.413)

question: A person enters into an agreement to buy securities as a result of an unsolicited call made in contravention of s. 174, SFO. Within what period may he rescind the agreement by written notice?
options: Within 28 days after the day of entering into the agreement or within 7 days after the day of finding out about the contravention, whichever is the earlier
Within 7 days after the day of entering into the agreement or within 28 days after the day of finding out about the contravention, whichever is the earlier
Within 28 days after the day of entering into the agreement or within 7 days after the day of finding out about the contravention, whichever is the later
Within 14 days after the day of entering into the agreement in all cases
explanation: The SFO allows rescission by written notice within 28 days after entering into the agreement or within 7 days after finding out about the contravention, whichever is the EARLIER. Option B reverses the two periods. Option C wrongly says "whichever is the later". Option D invents a 14-day period that does not appear in the provision. (PDF p.415)

question: On which of the following persons is an intermediary NOT permitted to make an unsolicited call under s. 174, SFO?
options: A money lender
A retail investor who has never dealt with the intermediary before
A certified public accountant acting in his professional capacity
An existing client of the intermediary
explanation: The exempt persons (who may be cold-called) include existing clients, licensed persons, registered institutions, professional investors, solicitors or certified public accountants acting in their professional capacity, and money lenders. A retail investor with no existing relationship is not on that list, so calling him remains prohibited. Options A, C and D are all expressly within the exemptions. (PDF p.415)

question: Under the Securities and Futures (Unsolicited Calls - Exclusion) Rules, an interactive dialogue in which statements and responses are exchanged immediately will nevertheless be regarded as a "permissible communication" if it has which of the following characteristics?
options: It is made to more than one person in identical terms
It is made by a licensed representative rather than by the intermediary itself
It is conducted only during the trading hours of The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
It relates only to securities listed on NASDAQ or another overseas market
explanation: An interactive dialogue is treated as a permissible communication if it is made to more than one person in identical terms, is made by way of a system that creates a record available to the recipient for later reference, and/or is made through a system not requiring an immediate response. Option B is irrelevant because s. 174 applies equally to representatives. Options C and D invent conditions not found in the rules — overseas-market products are actually a feature of boiler room activities, not of these rules. (PDF p.416)

question: Which of the following is a typical feature of "boiler room activities"?
options: Initial and continuing contacts are made face-to-face at the seller's local office
The investor is told the investment is offered to a select group of people for a short time only
The investor is asked to pay into a bank account held in the apparent seller's own name in Hong Kong
The products offered are limited to ordinary shares listed on The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
explanation: Telling the investor that the investment is offered to a select group for a short time only is a listed feature of boiler room activities. Option A is the opposite of the truth — contact is solely by telephone, email or facsimile with no face-to-face meetings. Option C is wrong because payment is pressured into a bank account in another country in a name OTHER than the apparent seller's. Option D is wrong because the products are exotic-sounding options, bonds or commodities, or claimed new stocks on NASDAQ or other markets. (PDF p.417)

question: Mr Chan, an account executive at a licensed corporation, executes a buy order for a client but delays allocating it. When the price of the securities rises, he allocates the order to his own related account at the lower execution price, then executes a new order for the client at the higher price. This practice is best described as:
options: Front running
Churning
Rat trading
Unsuitability
explanation: This is the textbook example of rat trading: re-allocating orders to benefit one person at the expense of a client by delaying allocation and taking the favourable price for one's own (or a related) account. Front running involves trading on one's own account AHEAD of clients' trades using knowledge of those trades, not re-allocation afterwards. Churning is executing an excessive number of orders for managed funds or discretionary accounts to generate commission. Unsuitability is recommending investments known to be unsuitable for the client. (PDF p.418)

question: What is the improper purpose behind "churning" of funds or discretionary accounts?
options: To create a false or misleading appearance of active trading in the market
To generate higher commission-based fee income for the intermediary
To profit by trading ahead of clients using knowledge of their pending orders
To conceal misappropriation of client assets by the account executive
explanation: Churning is the execution by fund managers or Type 1 or Type 2 intermediaries of an excessive number of orders that do not reflect the investment objectives of the fund or discretionary account, and its improper purpose is to generate higher commission-based fee income, which is not in the client's best interests. Option A describes false trading. Option C describes front running. Option D is a feature associated with unauthorised trades, not churning. (PDF p.417)

question: In an SFC enforcement case, a licensed representative recklessly represented in the account opening documents of five clients that he had witnessed the clients signing and had inspected their original identity documents when he had not. What action did the SFC take?
options: Revoked his licence and banned him from re-entering the industry
Suspended him for six weeks
Issued a reprimand and fined him HK$210,000
Issued a reprimand and fined him HK$17.3 million
explanation: In Case 2, the SFC reprimanded LR-B and fined him HK$210,000, taking into account that he consented to the SFC's decision. Option A was the outcome of Case 1 (misappropriation of client assets). Option B was the penalty in Case 3 (allowing unauthorised third-party trading) and Case 4 (short selling). Option D is the fine imposed on the licensed corporation LC-1 in Case 6. (PDF p.421)

question: In the SFC's stock price manipulation case, a client instructed LR-E to fix a stock's price at a level well over the prevailing price, and LR-F advised splitting the order into small lots placed in the last minute of trading. What penalties did the SFC impose?
options: LR-F was suspended for six months and LR-E for four months
LR-E was suspended for six months and LR-F for four months
Both LR-E and LR-F were suspended for six weeks and fined
Both LR-E and LR-F were reprimanded and fined HK$210,000 each
explanation: The SFC suspended LR-F for six months and LR-E for four months, taking into account that LR-E's conduct appeared not to be pre-meditated and was unlikely to be repeated. Option B reverses the two suspensions. Option C borrows the six-week suspension from Cases 3 and 4. Option D borrows the reprimand-and-HK$210,000-fine outcome from Case 2. (PDF p.422)

question: According to the elements of stock market manipulation, what is the minimum number of transactions required?
options: One transaction is sufficient
Two or more transactions
Three or more transactions
Five or more transactions within one trading day
explanation: Stock market manipulation requires two or more transactions which, by themselves or together with another transaction, have the effect or likely effect of increasing, reducing, maintaining or stabilizing the price of securities, thereby influencing other persons' investment decisions. Option A is wrong because a single transaction does not satisfy the stated element. Options C and D state higher thresholds that have no basis in the text. (PDF p.425)

question: According to the abbreviations list, what does "AMLO" stand for?
options: Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance
Anti-Money Laundering Office
Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Financing of Terrorism Guideline
Approved Money Lenders Ordinance
explanation: AMLO is listed as the Anti-Money Laundering and Counter-Terrorist Financing Ordinance. "Anti-Money Laundering Office" is not an entry in the list, and the guideline on anti-money laundering is abbreviated GAML, not AMLO. "Approved Money Lenders Ordinance" is a confusion with AMBs (approved money brokers), which is a different entry. (PDF p.435)

question: In the context of investor identification, the abbreviation "BCAN" stands for:
options: Bank Client Account Number
Broker-to-Client Assigned Number
Broker Central Account Number
Beneficial Client Authorisation Number
explanation: The abbreviations list defines BCAN as Broker-to-Client Assigned Number. The other three options are invented variants that do not appear in the list; note that the separate identification concepts in the list are CID (client identification data) and CE number (central entity identification number), neither of which matches BCAN. (PDF p.435)

question: Which abbreviation denotes the Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules?
options: RLC
FDRS
FRR
FMCC
explanation: FRR is listed as the Securities and Futures (Financial Resources) Rules. RLC means required liquid capital (a related financial-resources concept, not the rules themselves), FDRS is the Financial Dispute Resolution Scheme, and FMCC is the Fund Manager Code of Conduct. (PDF p.436)

question: According to the abbreviations list, "HKCC" stands for:
options: Hong Kong Securities Clearing Company Limited
HKFE Clearing Corporation Limited
Hong Kong Central Clearing Company
The SEHK Options Clearing House Limited
explanation: HKCC is defined as HKFE Clearing Corporation Limited. Hong Kong Securities Clearing Company Limited is abbreviated HKSCC, and The SEHK Options Clearing House Limited is abbreviated SEOCH — both are easy to confuse with HKCC. "Hong Kong Central Clearing Company" does not appear in the list at all. (PDF p.436)

question: What does the abbreviation "MMT" stand for?
options: Market Monitoring Team
Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal
Market Manipulation Taskforce
Market Misconduct Tribunal
explanation: MMT is listed as the Market Misconduct Tribunal. The Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal is a different body abbreviated SFAT. "Market Monitoring Team" and "Market Manipulation Taskforce" are plausible-sounding inventions that do not appear in the abbreviations list. (PDF p.437)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, reads a settlement notice referring to "CCASS". According to the abbreviations list, CCASS stands for:
options: Central Clearing and Settlement System
Derivatives Clearing and Settlement System
China Securities Depository and Clearing Corporation Limited
Central Counterparty Settlement System
explanation: CCASS is the Central Clearing and Settlement System. The Derivatives Clearing and Settlement System is abbreviated DCASS, and China Securities Depository and Clearing Corporation Limited is abbreviated ChinaClear. "Central Counterparty Settlement System" mixes in CCP (central counterparty), which is a separate entry. (PDF p.435)

question: In the glossary, the Chinese term “老鼠倉”交易 corresponds to which English term?
options: Churning
Front running
Rat trading
Cross trade
explanation: The glossary pairs "Rat trading" with “老鼠倉”交易. Churning is rendered as 過分頻密的交易, front running as 扒頭交易, and cross trade as 交叉盤交易 — all are different forms of dealing misconduct or trade types that are easily confused with rat trading. (PDF p.431)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, sees a reference to the "SFAT" in an SFC circular. According to the abbreviations list, SFAT stands for:
options: Securities and Futures Arbitration Tribunal
Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal
Securities and Futures Advisory Taskforce
Securities and Futures Administrative Tribunal
explanation: SFAT is defined as the Securities and Futures Appeals Tribunal. The other three options substitute "Arbitration", "Advisory" and "Administrative" for "Appeals" and none of them appears in the abbreviations list. Do not confuse the SFAT with the MMT (Market Misconduct Tribunal), which is a separate entry. (PDF p.438)

question: Which abbreviation refers to the Management, Supervision and Internal Control Guidelines for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the Securities and Futures Commission?
options: GDII
ODAP Guidelines
ICG
CFA Code
explanation: ICG stands for the Management, Supervision and Internal Control Guidelines for Persons Licensed by or Registered with the SFC. GDII is the Guidelines on Disclosure of Inside Information, the ODAP Guidelines are the Guidelines on Online Distribution and Advisory Platforms, and the CFA Code is the Corporate Finance Adviser Code of Conduct — all real but different SFC instruments. (PDF p.436)

question: In the HKSI LE Paper 1 study manual, what does the abbreviation "VCM" stand for?
options: Variation control margin
Volatility control mechanism
Virtual currency mechanism
Volatility capital margin
explanation: The abbreviations list defines VCM as "Volatility control mechanism". "Variation control margin" and "Volatility capital margin" wrongly blend in VM, which is a separate abbreviation for "Variation margin"; "Virtual currency mechanism" wrongly borrows from the virtual asset (VA) entries listed nearby. (PDF p.439)

question: According to the Major Updates in Version 3.5 of the Paper 1 manual (updated in April 2026), the Topic 1 update reflects the renaming of the Chinese Gold & Silver Exchange Society to which entity?
options: Hong Kong Gold Exchange
Hong Kong Gold & Silver Exchange
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
Hong Kong Bullion Society
explanation: Sections 2.17 and 2.18 of Topic 1 were updated for the renaming of the Chinese Gold & Silver Exchange Society to the Hong Kong Gold Exchange. "Hong Kong Gold & Silver Exchange" merely rewords the old name; Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited (HKEX) is a different body responsible for the Topic 8 Listing Rules updates; "Hong Kong Bullion Society" is not mentioned anywhere in the text. (PDF p.440)

question: Mr Chan, a licensed representative, trades exchange-traded derivatives based on the Hang Seng Index, the Hang Seng China Enterprises Index and the Hang Seng TECH Index. Under the Version 3.5 updates to Topic 7, which body issued the consultation conclusions proposing an increase of position limits for these products?
options: The Stock Exchange of Hong Kong Limited
Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing Limited
The Securities and Futures Commission
The Legislative Council of the Hong Kong Special Administrative Region
explanation: The Topic 7 update (sections 6.3 and 6.5) cites the Consultation Conclusions on Proposed Increase of Position Limits for Exchange-traded Derivatives Based on Hang Seng Index, Hang Seng China Enterprises Index and Hang Seng TECH Index issued by the SFC. The Stock Exchange's role in the Topic 7 updates concerns its Options Trading Rules introducing weekly expiries for single stock options; HKEX issued the Topic 8 Listing Rules consultation conclusions; LegCo enacted the Companies (Amendment) Ordinances noted under Topic 2. (PDF p.440)
